title: The Story of Tea: From Ancient China to the World
topic: The history of tea and its global spread
passage:
[A] According to Chinese legend, tea was discovered in 2737 BC by the emperor Shennong, when leaves from a wild tree blew into his pot of boiling water. Although this story cannot be verified, historians agree that tea drinking began in China. By the time of the Tang Dynasty, between the seventh and tenth centuries, tea had become the national drink of China. In the eighth century, a scholar named Lu Yu wrote the first book devoted entirely to tea, called The Classic of Tea, which described how the plant should be grown, prepared and served.

[B] Tea gradually spread beyond China's borders. In the early ninth century, Japanese Buddhist monks who had studied in China carried tea seeds home with them. Tea drinking in Japan eventually developed into a formal ritual known as the tea ceremony, which is still practised today. At the same time, tea travelled westwards along overland trade routes, reaching Tibet, Central Asia and the Middle East, where it was often exchanged for horses and other goods. In these regions, tea was sometimes pressed into solid bricks, which could be stored easily and even used as a form of money.

[C] Tea did not arrive in Europe until much later. Dutch traders shipped the first tea to Amsterdam in 1610, and it soon appeared in Portugal and France. In Britain, tea became fashionable after 1662, when the Portuguese princess Catherine of Braganza married King Charles II and introduced the habit to the royal court. At first, tea was an expensive luxury that only wealthy families could afford, partly because the government placed heavy taxes on it. As prices fell during the eighteenth century, however, tea became the everyday drink of ordinary British people. By 1750, it had replaced ale as the most popular drink in many British homes.

[D] During the nineteenth century, Britain wanted to reduce its dependence on Chinese tea. In the 1840s, a botanist named Robert Fortune secretly collected thousands of tea plants in China and transported them to India, which was then under British control. Large plantations were established in the Indian regions of Assam and Darjeeling, and later on the island of Ceylon, now known as Sri Lanka. These new sources transformed the world tea trade. Today, tea is grown in more than sixty countries and is the most widely consumed beverage in the world after water, with several billion cups drunk every day.

questions.q: The story of Shennong's discovery of tea has been proved to be true.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าตำนานนี้ 'cannot be verified' คือพิสูจน์ไม่ได้ จึงขัดแย้งกับคำกล่าวที่ว่าพิสูจน์แล้วว่าเป็นจริง
questions.evidence: Although this story cannot be verified, historians agree that tea drinking began in China.

questions.evidence: In the early ninth century, Japanese Buddhist monks who had studied in China carried tea seeds home with them.

title: How Earthquakes Are Measured
passage:
[A] Every year, thousands of earthquakes occur around the world, ranging from tiny tremors that nobody notices to violent events that destroy entire cities. To understand and compare these events, scientists need reliable ways of measuring them. The main instrument used for this purpose is the seismograph, a device that records the movement of the ground. The earliest known earthquake detector was invented in China in 132 AD by Zhang Heng. His device could indicate the direction of a distant earthquake, although it could not measure its strength. Modern seismographs, by contrast, produce detailed records of ground motion, which scientists use to calculate both the location and the size of an earthquake.

[B] The most famous measurement system is the Richter scale, developed in 1935 by the American scientist Charles Richter. This scale is based on the height, or amplitude, of the waves recorded on a seismograph. The Richter scale is logarithmic, which means that each whole number represents a tenfold increase in the amplitude of the waves. In terms of energy, the difference is even greater: an earthquake measuring 6.0 releases about thirty-two times more energy than one measuring 5.0. This is why even a small difference in magnitude can mean a huge difference in destructive power.

[C] Although the Richter scale is widely known to the public, most scientists today prefer the moment magnitude scale, which was introduced in the 1970s. The Richter scale was designed for earthquakes in California and tends to underestimate the size of very large earthquakes. The moment magnitude scale, on the other hand, calculates the total energy released by an earthquake, so it gives accurate results for events of all sizes. News reports often still use the term Richter scale, but the figures they quote are usually moment magnitude values.

[D] Magnitude scales measure the physical size of an earthquake, but they do not describe its effects on people. For this purpose, scientists use the Mercalli intensity scale, which ranks the damage caused by an earthquake from I to XII using Roman numerals. Unlike magnitude, intensity varies from place to place: a single earthquake may cause serious destruction near its centre yet be barely felt a few hundred kilometres away. The intensity also depends on the quality of local buildings and the type of ground. Together, magnitude and intensity give a complete picture, and this information helps engineers design safer structures for the future.

questions.q: The earliest earthquake detector was able to measure the strength of an earthquake.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเครื่องของจางเหิงชี้ทิศทางของแผ่นดินไหวได้ แต่วัดความแรงไม่ได้ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: His device could indicate the direction of a distant earthquake, although it could not measure its strength.

questions.q: On the Richter scale, each whole number represents a ten times increase in the amplitude of the waves.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่ามาตราริกเตอร์เป็นแบบลอการิทึม โดยแต่ละเลขจำนวนเต็มหมายถึงแอมพลิจูดของคลื่นเพิ่มขึ้นสิบเท่า ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: The Richter scale is logarithmic, which means that each whole number represents a tenfold increase in the amplitude of the waves.

questions.evidence: In terms of energy, the difference is even greater: an earthquake measuring 6.0 releases about thirty-two times more energy than one measuring 5.0.

questions.evidence: For this purpose, scientists use the Mercalli intensity scale, which ranks the damage caused by an earthquake from I to XII using Roman numerals.

questions.evidence: Together, magnitude and intensity give a complete picture, and this information helps engineers design safer structures for the future.

title: Finding the Way: How Birds Use the Earth's Magnetic Field
passage:
[A] Every year, millions of birds travel enormous distances between their breeding and wintering grounds. Some species fly thousands of kilometres, often crossing oceans and deserts where there are no obvious landmarks to guide them. For many years, scientists were puzzled about how these birds manage to find their way so accurately. Research now suggests that birds use several different methods at the same time. They rely on the position of the sun during the day and on the pattern of the stars at night. However, one of the most surprising discoveries is that many birds can also sense the Earth's magnetic field and use it as a kind of natural compass.

[B] The Earth behaves rather like a giant magnet, producing an invisible magnetic field that surrounds the whole planet. This field is strongest near the poles and provides information about both direction and location. A bird that can detect the field is able to work out which way is north, even when the sky is completely covered by cloud. Because the magnetic field is always present, it offers a reliable source of information that does not depend on the weather. This may explain why some birds continue to migrate successfully on dark, cloudy nights when the stars cannot be seen.

[C] Exactly how birds detect the magnetic field is still not fully understood, but scientists have proposed two main explanations. The first involves a substance called magnetite, a tiny magnetic mineral found in the beaks of some birds. It is thought that this material responds to the magnetic field and sends signals to the brain. The second explanation is more unusual. It suggests that special proteins in the birds' eyes react to the field when light strikes them, allowing the birds to actually see magnetic information. Many researchers now believe that this second mechanism plays the more important role.

[D] To test these ideas, scientists have carried out numerous experiments with migratory birds such as the European robin. In one well-known type of study, birds were placed in circular cages during the migration season and their movements were recorded. When researchers used artificial magnets to change the direction of the magnetic field around the cage, the birds altered the direction in which they tried to fly. This provided strong evidence that the birds were indeed responding to magnetic signals rather than to other cues.

[E] Understanding bird navigation has become more urgent in recent years. Human activities produce weak electromagnetic signals that may interfere with the birds' natural compass. In addition, tall buildings and bright city lights can confuse migrating birds and cause them to lose their way. By learning more about how birds sense direction, scientists hope to find practical ways to protect these remarkable travellers on their long and dangerous journeys.

questions.evidence: Some species fly thousands of kilometres, often crossing oceans and deserts where there are no obvious landmarks to guide them.

title: Surviving the Dry Lands: How Desert Plants Endure
passage:
[A] Deserts are among the harshest environments on Earth. They receive very little rainfall, often less than 250 millimetres in an entire year, and daytime temperatures can rise above 45 degrees Celsius. Water quickly evaporates from the soil, and rain may not fall for many months. Despite these difficult conditions, a surprising number of plants manage to grow and reproduce in desert regions. Over millions of years, these plants have developed special features, known as adaptations, that allow them to collect water, store it, and reduce the amount they lose.

[B] One of the best-known desert plants is the cactus. The cactus has a thick, fleshy stem that acts as a store for water. After rain, the stem swells as it absorbs moisture, and during long dry periods the plant slowly uses this supply. Instead of broad leaves, which would lose large amounts of water, the cactus has sharp spines. These spines also protect the plant from thirsty animals that might try to eat it. In addition, many cacti have a waxy outer surface that helps to trap moisture inside the plant.

[C] Roots play an equally important role in survival. Some desert plants, such as the mesquite tree, grow extremely long roots that reach deep underground to find hidden water supplies. Other plants take a different approach: they spread a wide network of shallow roots close to the surface. These shallow roots can quickly absorb rainwater before it evaporates in the heat. By collecting water rapidly after a rare shower, such plants gain enough moisture to survive until the next rainfall.

[D] Timing is another clever strategy. Certain small desert plants avoid the worst conditions altogether by remaining as seeds during dry periods. When rain finally arrives, the seeds germinate, grow, flower, and produce new seeds within just a few weeks. These plants are sometimes called ephemerals because their lives are so short. The desert may appear suddenly covered in colourful flowers, a display that disappears almost as quickly as it came. In this way, the plants complete their entire life cycle while water is still available.

[E] Reducing water loss is essential for plants that stay alive throughout the year. Many desert species have small leaves or none at all, which limits evaporation from the surface. Some open the tiny pores on their leaves only at night, when the air is cooler, so that less water escapes. Others have pale or silvery surfaces that reflect sunlight and keep the plant cooler. Together, these varied strategies show how life can adapt in remarkable ways to one of the planet's most demanding habitats.

title: How Tsunamis Form
passage:
[A] The word 'tsunami' comes from Japanese and means 'harbour wave'. A tsunami is a series of enormous waves that can cross an entire ocean. Unlike ordinary waves, which are created by the wind blowing across the surface of the sea, tsunamis are produced by the sudden movement of a large volume of water. Because they carry energy through the whole depth of the ocean, they are far more destructive than even the largest storm waves when they reach land.

[B] The most common cause of a tsunami is an underwater earthquake. Approximately eighty per cent of all tsunamis are generated in the Pacific Ocean, where sections of the Earth's crust, known as tectonic plates, push against one another. When one plate slides suddenly beneath another, in a process called subduction, the sea floor may be lifted or dropped by several metres. This movement displaces the enormous body of water above it, sending waves outwards in all directions, much like the ripples produced when a stone is thrown into a pond.

[C] Earthquakes are not the only trigger. Volcanic eruptions, underwater landslides and, on very rare occasions, meteorite impacts can also push large amounts of water out of place. In 1883, the eruption of the volcano Krakatoa in Indonesia produced waves over thirty metres high, which killed more than 36,000 people. Coastal landslides can create locally powerful tsunamis, although these usually affect a much smaller area than those caused by earthquakes.

[D] In deep water, a tsunami behaves very differently from the way most people imagine. It may travel at speeds of up to 800 kilometres per hour, roughly as fast as a passenger jet, yet its height at the surface is often less than one metre. Ships in the open ocean frequently pass over a tsunami without noticing it. As the waves approach the shore, however, the shallow water slows them down. The energy is compressed, the distance between waves shortens, and the water rises dramatically, sometimes to heights of thirty metres or more.

[E] One warning sign that a tsunami is approaching is the sudden retreat of the sea from the beach, which exposes the sea floor. This happens when the trough of the wave arrives before its crest. Scientists today use a network of ocean sensors and buoys to detect tsunamis early and to send alerts to coastal communities, a development that has saved many lives since the devastating Indian Ocean tsunami of 2004.

questions.q: Tsunamis are created by the wind blowing across the surface of the sea.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าคลื่นธรรมดาเกิดจากลม แต่สึนามิเกิดจากการเคลื่อนที่ฉับพลันของมวลน้ำขนาดใหญ่ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: Unlike ordinary waves, which are created by the wind blowing across the surface of the sea, tsunamis are produced by the sudden movement of a large volume of water.

questions.q: The majority of tsunamis occur in the Pacific Ocean.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าประมาณร้อยละ 80 ของสึนามิทั้งหมดเกิดในมหาสมุทรแปซิฟิก ซึ่งถือเป็นส่วนใหญ่
questions.evidence: Approximately eighty per cent of all tsunamis are generated in the Pacific Ocean, where sections of the Earth's crust, known as tectonic plates, push against one another.

questions.evidence: In 1883, the eruption of the volcano Krakatoa in Indonesia produced waves over thirty metres high, which killed more than 36,000 people.

questions.evidence: When one plate slides suddenly beneath another, in a process called subduction, the sea floor may be lifted or dropped by several metres.

questions.evidence: It may travel at speeds of up to 800 kilometres per hour, roughly as fast as a passenger jet, yet its height at the surface is often less than one metre.

questions.evidence: Scientists today use a network of ocean sensors and buoys to detect tsunamis early and to send alerts to coastal communities, a development that has saved many lives since the devastating Indian Ocean tsunami of 2004.

title: The Songs of the Sea
passage:
[A] Whales are among the most vocal animals in the ocean. Because water carries sound far more efficiently than air, whales rely on acoustic signals rather than sight to stay in contact. Many species produce a wide range of clicks, whistles and long, patterned sequences known as songs. These sounds allow whales to find food, navigate through dark or murky water, and communicate with other members of their species across great distances.

[B] The humpback whale is especially famous for its songs. Only male humpbacks are known to sing, and they do so mainly during the breeding season. A single song may last up to twenty minutes and is often repeated for hours without a break. Remarkably, all the males in one population tend to sing the same song, and this shared song gradually changes over the months and years. Scientists believe that singing may help males attract females or compete with other males, although the exact purpose is still debated.

[C] Other whales communicate in different ways. Sperm whales produce short bursts of clicks called codas, which vary between family groups and may act like a form of identity. Killer whales, or orcas, live in tight social groups called pods, and each pod uses its own set of calls that young whales learn from their mothers. This passing of sounds from one generation to the next is a kind of culture, similar in some ways to human dialects.

[D] Low-frequency sounds made by large whales such as the blue whale can travel for hundreds of kilometres through deep water. Unfortunately, the modern ocean is far noisier than it once was. Ships, drilling and military sonar create background noise that can mask whale calls and make communication difficult. Researchers have found that some whales now call more loudly, or shift the pitch of their calls, in an effort to be heard. Understanding how whales use sound is therefore not only scientifically interesting but also important for protecting these animals in a changing environment.

questions.q: Whales depend more on sound than on vision to keep in contact with each other.
questions.why_th: ประโยคระบุว่าวาฬพึ่งสัญญาณเสียงมากกว่าการมองเห็น
questions.evidence: Because water carries sound far more efficiently than air, whales rely on acoustic signals rather than sight to stay in contact.

title: Mountains and the Making of Rain
passage:
[A] Mountains have a powerful effect on the amount of rain that falls in different places. When moist air moving across the land meets a mountain range, it cannot pass through the rock and is forced to rise. As the air climbs higher, the temperature around it falls. Because cooler air is unable to hold as much water vapour as warm air, the vapour begins to change into tiny droplets of water, forming thick clouds. These clouds eventually release their moisture as rain or snow. This process, in which rising air produces precipitation on the side of a mountain facing the wind, is known to scientists as orographic rainfall.

[B] The side of the mountain that faces the incoming wind is called the windward side, and it usually receives a great deal of rain. Forests on this slope are often green and dense because the supply of water is reliable throughout much of the year. Some of the wettest places on Earth are found on windward slopes. For example, certain mountain regions in India and Hawaii record several metres of rainfall annually, far more than the surrounding lowlands. Farmers in these areas rarely need to worry about a lack of water for their crops.

[C] The situation on the opposite side of the mountain is very different. Once the air has crossed the summit and begins to descend, it has already lost most of its moisture. As this dry air sinks, it becomes warmer and its ability to hold water increases, so little or no rain falls. The dry region created behind a mountain is called a rain shadow. Deserts in several parts of the world exist precisely because they lie in the rain shadow of a large mountain range. The land here is frequently brown, bare and poorly suited to farming.

[D] The influence of mountains reaches beyond the immediate landscape. Rivers that begin high in the mountains carry water and melted snow down to drier regions far below, supporting millions of people who live nowhere near a mountain. In addition, the pattern of wet and dry slopes shapes where particular plants and animals can survive. Scientists who study the climate must therefore pay close attention to mountains, since even a single range can divide a country into strikingly different zones of wet and dry.

questions.q: Air is forced upwards when it meets a mountain range because it cannot pass through the rock.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงว่าอากาศไม่สามารถผ่านหินได้จึงถูกดันให้ลอยสูงขึ้น
questions.evidence: When moist air moving across the land meets a mountain range, it cannot pass through the rock and is forced to rise.

questions.q: Warm air can hold more water vapour than cool air.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าอากาศที่เย็นกว่าเก็บไอน้ำได้น้อยกว่าอากาศอุ่น ซึ่งหมายความว่าอากาศอุ่นเก็บได้มากกว่า
questions.evidence: Because cooler air is unable to hold as much water vapour as warm air, the vapour begins to change into tiny droplets of water, forming thick clouds.

questions.evidence: As this dry air sinks, it becomes warmer and its ability to hold water increases, so little or no rain falls.

questions.q: What is orographic rainfall?
questions.a: Rain produced when rising air releases moisture on the windward side of a mountain
options: Rain produced when rising air releases moisture on the windward side of a mountain

title: The Language of the Waggle Dance
topic: How honeybees communicate through the waggle dance
passage:
[A] Honeybees are among the most social insects on Earth, living in colonies that may contain tens of thousands of individuals. When a worker bee discovers a rich source of nectar or pollen, it faces a remarkable problem: how to tell its nestmates exactly where the food can be found. The solution, first decoded by the Austrian scientist Karl von Frisch in the middle of the twentieth century, is a form of movement known as the waggle dance. Through this behaviour, a single forager can transmit precise information about the distance and direction of a food source to bees that have never visited it.

[B] The dance is performed on the vertical surface of the honeycomb, deep inside the darkness of the hive. A returning forager walks in a figure-of-eight pattern, and the central portion of this pattern, called the waggle run, carries the essential message. During the waggle run, the bee shakes its abdomen rapidly from side to side while moving forward in a straight line. The other bees crowd closely around the dancer, touching it with their antennae and detecting the vibrations it produces. In this way, the information is passed through touch and sound rather than sight.

[C] The direction of the food source is encoded through the angle of the waggle run. Because the hive is dark, bees cannot see the sun, yet the dance still refers to the sun's position. A run performed straight upwards indicates that food lies in the direction of the sun, while a run angled to the left or right of vertical shows that the source lies at that same angle relative to the sun. The distance to the food is communicated by the duration of the waggle run: the longer a bee waggles, the further away the food source is. A run lasting roughly one second typically signals a distance of about one kilometre.

[D] The waggle dance is a striking example of symbolic communication in a creature with a very small brain. Von Frisch was awarded a Nobel Prize in 1973 for his work in uncovering its meaning, and researchers have continued to study it ever since. Modern experiments, some using tiny robotic bees, have confirmed that hive-mates genuinely act on the information contained in the dance. Nevertheless, the dance is not perfectly accurate, and foragers often adjust their search once they arrive in the approximate area indicated by their nestmate.

title: Machines, Metal and the Spread of Words
topic: The history and science of the printing press
passage:
[A] Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, a slow process carried out mainly by monks in monasteries. A single volume could take months to complete, which meant that books were rare and expensive. This situation changed dramatically around 1450, when a German goldsmith named Johannes Gutenberg developed a system of printing that used movable metal type. Although printing with carved wooden blocks had existed in East Asia for centuries, Gutenberg's method was better suited to the many letters of the European alphabet, and it allowed pages to be produced far more quickly.

[B] The success of Gutenberg's invention depended on a combination of several technologies rather than a single idea. He created individual metal letters by pouring a molten alloy of lead, tin and antimony into a small mould. This alloy was chosen because it melted at a relatively low temperature and, crucially, expanded slightly as it cooled, so that each letter filled the mould completely and formed a sharp, even shape. The letters could then be arranged into words and lines, locked into a frame, inked and pressed onto paper. Once a page had been printed, the type could be taken apart and reused for a completely different text.

[C] The press itself was adapted from machines already familiar in Europe, particularly the screw presses used for producing wine and olive oil. A heavy wooden screw was turned to bring a flat plate down onto the inked type with firm, even pressure, producing a clear impression. Equally important was the ink. Water-based inks used by earlier copyists did not stick well to metal, so printers developed a thicker, oil-based ink that adhered to the type and transferred cleanly to the page.

[D] The consequences of the new technology were enormous. By 1500, presses across Europe had produced millions of books, and prices had fallen steadily. Ideas could now travel faster and reach far larger audiences than before. Historians often link the printing press to major movements such as the Reformation and the scientific revolution, arguing that the rapid circulation of printed material helped new ideas to spread and survive. What had begun as a practical craft in a single workshop had become a force that reshaped European society.

questions.q: Before 1450, most books in Europe were copied by hand in monasteries.

questions.evidence: Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, a slow process carried out mainly by monks in monasteries.

title: Why Finnish Schools Succeed
topic: The Finnish approach to school education
passage:
[A] Over the past two decades, Finland has attracted worldwide attention for the quality of its school system. When the country performed strongly in international assessments of reading, mathematics and science, educators from many nations travelled to Helsinki to understand how such results were achieved. What they discovered was a system built on principles that differ sharply from those found in many other countries. Rather than relying on frequent testing and competition between schools, Finland has placed its trust in well-trained teachers and a relaxed, supportive learning environment.

[B] One of the most striking features of Finnish education is the late start to formal schooling. Children do not begin compulsory education until the age of seven, which is later than in most European countries. Before that age, the emphasis is on play, social skills and physical activity rather than reading and writing. Supporters of this approach argue that young children learn best through exploration and that pushing academic content too early can create anxiety. Once formal schooling begins, the school day remains comparatively short, and pupils are given generous breaks between lessons to rest and move around.

[C] The teaching profession itself is central to the Finnish model. Teaching is regarded as a highly respected career, and competition for places on training courses is intense. All teachers are required to hold a master's degree, and their preparation includes a strong focus on research as well as classroom practice. Because teachers are so thoroughly trained, they are trusted to design their own lessons and to assess their pupils without the pressure of national examinations. Standardised tests are rare, and the single major national examination is taken only at the end of secondary school.

[D] Equality is another guiding principle. Almost all schools are funded by the state, and private schools are unusual. Meals, textbooks and transport are provided free of charge, so that a child's family income has little effect on the education they receive. Schools in wealthy and poorer areas are intended to be of similar quality, and pupils who struggle are given extra help early rather than being separated into lower groups. This commitment to fairness is often cited as one reason why the gap between the strongest and weakest pupils in Finland has traditionally been small.

[E] Nevertheless, the Finnish system is not without its critics. Some argue that recent results in international assessments have declined, and that the relaxed approach may not suit every learner. Others point out that methods which work well in a small, relatively uniform society may be difficult to copy elsewhere. Even so, Finland continues to be studied by policymakers who hope to learn from its emphasis on trust, equality and the professional freedom of teachers.

questions.q: Educators from other countries visited Finland to study its school system after strong international results.
options: True
False
Not Given

title: Bringing Coral Reefs Back to Life
passage:
[A] Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems on the planet, supporting roughly a quarter of all marine species despite covering less than one per cent of the ocean floor. In recent decades, however, these reefs have suffered severe damage. Rising sea temperatures, pollution, and destructive fishing practices have caused widespread coral bleaching, a process in which stressed corals expel the tiny algae that live inside them and provide them with food and colour. Without these algae, the corals turn white and often die. Faced with such losses, scientists have begun developing methods to actively restore damaged reefs rather than simply protecting what remains.

[B] One of the most common restoration techniques is known as coral gardening. In this approach, small fragments of healthy coral are collected and grown in underwater nurseries, often attached to frames or ropes suspended in the water. After several months, when the fragments have matured, divers transplant them onto degraded reefs. Because many coral species can regrow from broken pieces, a single healthy colony can eventually produce hundreds of new corals. This method has been used successfully in the Caribbean and Southeast Asia, where thousands of corals have been returned to the sea.

[C] Other researchers are experimenting with more advanced strategies. Some are breeding corals that are more tolerant of heat, hoping these hardier varieties will survive future warming. Others build artificial structures on the seabed to give young corals a solid surface on which to settle and grow. A few projects even use gentle electrical currents to encourage the growth of the limestone skeletons that corals depend on. While these techniques show promise, they remain expensive and difficult to apply across the vast areas that need repair.

[D] Despite these encouraging developments, scientists agree that restoration alone cannot save the world's reefs. The fundamental threats, particularly climate change, must be addressed if reefs are to have a long-term future. Restoration projects are best understood as a way of buying time, keeping reefs alive until global emissions are reduced. Many experts also stress the importance of involving local communities, whose knowledge and cooperation are often essential to a project's success. For coastal populations that depend on reefs for food and tourism, the survival of these ecosystems is not merely an environmental concern but an economic one.

title: Taming the Wild Horse
passage:
[A] The domestication of the horse marks one of the most significant turning points in human history. Wild horses once roamed across the grasslands of Europe and Asia, and for thousands of years early humans hunted them purely for their meat. The transformation of the horse from prey into a partner was a gradual process, and archaeologists still debate exactly when and where it first occurred. Most scholars, however, now agree that the earliest domestication took place on the vast open steppes of Central Asia, in a region that today lies within modern Kazakhstan.

[B] The most compelling evidence comes from the Botai culture, which flourished around 3500 BCE. Excavations at Botai settlements have uncovered enormous quantities of horse bones, suggesting that these people depended heavily on horses. More revealing still are the marks found on the horses' teeth. Scientists identified a distinctive pattern of wear caused by a bit, the piece of equipment placed in a horse's mouth to control it while riding. This discovery strongly indicates that the Botai people were not merely hunting horses but were riding them. In addition, chemical traces of mare's milk were detected inside ancient pottery vessels, showing that horses were also milked.

[C] Domestication brought profound changes to human societies. A person on horseback could travel far greater distances than one on foot, allowing communities to trade goods, exchange ideas, and maintain contact across immense territories. Horses also revolutionised warfare. Mounted warriors possessed obvious advantages of speed and height over foot soldiers, and later the horse-drawn chariot became a formidable weapon on the battlefields of the ancient world. The animal's strength was harnessed for agriculture too, as horses were eventually used to pull ploughs and carry heavy loads.

[D] Interestingly, recent genetic research has complicated the traditional story. Studies of ancient DNA suggest that the horses kept by the Botai were not, in fact, the direct ancestors of nearly all modern domestic horses. It appears that a second, separate domestication took place later, further to the west, and it was this lineage that ultimately spread across the world. The Botai line, by contrast, survives today only in the rare Przewalski's horse of Mongolia. The full picture of how humans and horses came together therefore remains incomplete, and continues to be rewritten as new techniques emerge.

title: From Promise to Paper: The Rise of Banknotes
topic: The invention and spread of paper money
passage:
[A] For most of human history, money took the form of objects with intrinsic value, such as coins made of gold, silver or bronze. These metals were durable and widely accepted, but they were also heavy and inconvenient to transport in large quantities. The first true paper money appeared in China during the Tang dynasty, more than a thousand years ago, when merchants began depositing their coins with trusted associates in exchange for handwritten certificates. These certificates could be redeemed for the deposited coins at a later date, allowing traders to travel without carrying dangerous amounts of metal.

[B] By the time of the Song dynasty, the practice had become more formal. The government took control of issuing notes and produced them using woodblock printing, which made large numbers of identical notes possible. This official currency, known as jiaozi, is generally regarded as the earliest government-backed paper money in the world. However, the system carried a serious risk. Because the notes themselves had no intrinsic value, their worth depended entirely on public confidence. When rulers printed too many notes to cover their expenses, prices rose sharply and the value of the currency collapsed.

[C] Knowledge of Chinese paper money reached Europe partly through travellers. The Venetian merchant Marco Polo described the Chinese system in the thirteenth century, and his account astonished European readers, who found it difficult to believe that printed paper could be treated as valuable. Despite such reports, paper currency was not adopted in Europe for several centuries. European economies continued to rely on metal coins, supported occasionally by written promises issued by private banks.

[D] Paper money finally became established in Europe in the seventeenth century. In 1661, a bank in Sweden became the first in Europe to issue banknotes, although the experiment ended in failure when the bank printed more notes than it could honour. Other nations gradually followed, and by the eighteenth century banknotes were circulating widely. The notes issued by these early banks were promises to pay the bearer a fixed amount of precious metal on demand, which meant their value was still ultimately linked to gold or silver.

[E] Over the following centuries, the connection between paper money and precious metals weakened. Governments discovered that currency could function even when it was not backed by a physical commodity, provided that people trusted the authority that issued it. Today almost all national currencies are of this type, and their acceptance rests entirely on confidence rather than on any promise of metal. In this sense, modern money has returned to the principle that first troubled European readers of Marco Polo: value can exist in paper alone.

questions.q: Metal coins were difficult to move around in large amounts.

title: The Great Journeys: Understanding Long-Distance Animal Migration
topic: Why some animals migrate thousands of kilometres
passage:
[A] Each year, millions of animals undertake extraordinary journeys across the planet, travelling thousands of kilometres between distant regions. This behaviour, known as migration, is one of the most remarkable phenomena in the natural world. The Arctic tern, a small seabird, holds the record for the longest migration, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back again, a round trip of roughly 70,000 kilometres. Other famous migrants include the monarch butterfly, humpback whales, and the wildebeest of East Africa. Although these species differ enormously in size and habitat, they share a common purpose: survival.

[B] The primary reason animals migrate is the search for food. In many parts of the world, resources are abundant during one season but scarce during another. As winter approaches in northern regions, plants stop growing and insects disappear, forcing animals to move towards warmer areas where food remains plentiful. The wildebeest of the Serengeti, for example, follow the seasonal rains, which trigger the growth of fresh grass. Roughly 1.5 million animals travel in a continuous loop, guided by the availability of vegetation. Without this movement, many would starve during the dry months.

[C] Reproduction provides a second powerful motive for migration. Numerous species travel to specific locations to breed, often returning to the exact place where they were born. Salmon are perhaps the best-known example. After spending several years in the ocean, they swim upstream against powerful currents to lay their eggs in the freshwater rivers of their birth. Sea turtles behave similarly, crossing entire oceans to reach the beaches where they hatched. These breeding grounds typically offer greater safety for the young, away from the predators that fill richer feeding areas.

[D] How animals navigate such vast distances has long puzzled scientists. Research suggests that migrants rely on several methods at once. Many birds use the position of the sun and stars to orient themselves, while others appear to sense the Earth's magnetic field, using it rather like an internal compass. Some species also memorise landmarks such as coastlines and mountain ranges. Interestingly, young animals of certain species complete their first journey alone, without any guidance from adults, which suggests that migration routes may be partly inherited rather than learned.

[E] Migration is not without danger. Travellers face exhaustion, starvation, and predators along the way, and many do not survive the journey. In recent decades, human activity has made these journeys even harder. Climate change is altering the timing of seasons, so that animals may arrive at their destination before or after food is available. The destruction of habitats and the construction of barriers such as dams further threaten migratory species, making conservation an increasingly urgent concern.

questions.evidence: The Arctic tern, a small seabird, holds the record for the longest migration, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back again, a round trip of roughly 70,000 kilometres.

title: The Choices Behind the Display Case
topic: How museums decide what to collect and display
passage:
[A] Museums are often imagined as neutral storehouses where objects simply accumulate over time. In reality, every item on display is the result of deliberate choices made by curators, directors and specialist committees. Because no institution has the space, money or staff to keep everything, selection is unavoidable. A large national museum may hold hundreds of thousands of objects, yet display only a small fraction of them at any one time. The remainder is kept in storage, loaned to other institutions, or, occasionally, removed from the collection altogether.

[B] The first stage in building a collection is known as acquisition. Objects enter a museum through purchase, donation or archaeological fieldwork. Before accepting anything, staff usually consult a document called a collecting policy, which sets out the themes and periods the museum wishes to represent. An object that falls outside this policy may be refused, however valuable it appears, because caring for it would divert resources from the museum's central purpose. Curators also examine an item's provenance, meaning its history of ownership, to ensure it was obtained legally and ethically.

[C] Deciding what to display involves a different set of pressures. Space is limited, so curators must choose objects that tell a clear story to visitors who may know little about the subject. An object that is rare or scientifically important is not always chosen, because it may be difficult for the public to understand. Instead, curators frequently favour items that are visually striking or that connect to familiar themes. Increasingly, museums also test possible displays with focus groups drawn from the general public before a gallery opens.

[D] These decisions are rarely free of controversy. Critics argue that collections reflect the tastes and assumptions of those in power, and that certain cultures or groups have been poorly represented. In response, many museums now invite community members to help interpret objects connected to their own heritage. Some have returned items to the countries from which they were originally taken. Such changes suggest that a museum is not a fixed archive but an institution that continually reconsiders what deserves to be preserved and shown.

questions.q: Every object a museum owns is on display to the public at the same time.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าพิพิธภัณฑ์จัดแสดงเพียงส่วนน้อย (small fraction) ในแต่ละครั้ง ส่วนที่เหลือเก็บไว้ในคลัง จึงขัดแย้งกับประโยคที่ว่าแสดงทุกชิ้นพร้อมกัน = False
questions.evidence: A large national museum may hold hundreds of thousands of objects, yet display only a small fraction of them at any one time.

questions.q: An object may be turned down even if it seems valuable when it does not match the museum's collecting policy.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงๆ ว่าวัตถุที่อยู่นอกนโยบายอาจถูกปฏิเสธได้ ไม่ว่าจะดูมีค่าเพียงใดก็ตาม = True
questions.evidence: An object that falls outside this policy may be refused, however valuable it appears, because caring for it would divert resources from the museum's central purpose.

questions.q: Checking an object's provenance helps confirm that it was acquired in a legal and ethical way.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าภัณฑารักษ์ตรวจสอบ provenance เพื่อให้แน่ใจว่าวัตถุได้มาอย่างถูกกฎหมายและถูกจริยธรรม = True
questions.evidence: Curators also examine an item's provenance, meaning its history of ownership, to ensure it was obtained legally and ethically.

questions.q: Rare or scientifically important objects are always the ones chosen for display.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าวัตถุที่หายากหรือสำคัญทางวิทยาศาสตร์ 'ไม่ได้ถูกเลือกเสมอไป' เพราะอาจเข้าใจยากสำหรับคนทั่วไป จึงขัดแย้งกับคำว่า always = False
questions.evidence: An object that is rare or scientifically important is not always chosen, because it may be difficult for the public to understand.

questions.q: Museums that consult focus groups attract more visitors than those that do not.
questions.why_th: ข้อความกล่าวถึงการทดสอบการจัดแสดงกับ focus groups แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบจำนวนผู้เข้าชมระหว่างพิพิธภัณฑ์ที่ใช้และไม่ใช้ focus groups จึงไม่มีข้อมูลให้ตัดสิน = Not Given

questions.q: According to the passage, why is selection described as unavoidable for museums?
questions.a: No institution has enough space, money or staff to keep everything
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุชัดว่าไม่มีสถาบันใดมีพื้นที่ เงิน หรือบุคลากรเพียงพอที่จะเก็บทุกอย่าง การคัดเลือกจึงเลี่ยงไม่ได้
questions.evidence: Because no institution has the space, money or staff to keep everything, selection is unavoidable.
options: No institution has enough space, money or staff to keep everything
Curators prefer objects that visitors already recognise

questions.evidence: staff usually consult a document called a collecting policy, which sets out the themes and periods the museum wishes to represent.
options: A document setting out the themes and periods the museum wishes to represent
A list of objects that must be returned to other countries
A record of every visitor's opinion about the galleries

questions.q: When choosing objects for display, curators frequently prefer items that are:
questions.a: visually striking or connected to familiar themes
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าภัณฑารักษ์มักเลือกวัตถุที่โดดเด่นทางสายตาหรือเชื่อมโยงกับธีมที่ผู้ชมคุ้นเคย
questions.evidence: Instead, curators frequently favour items that are visually striking or that connect to familiar themes.
options: visually striking or connected to familiar themes
the oldest available in storage
the most scientifically complex
the cheapest to transport

questions.q: How have some museums responded to criticism about poor representation of certain groups?
questions.a: They have invited community members to help interpret objects and returned some items to their countries of origin
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุว่าพิพิธภัณฑ์เชิญสมาชิกชุมชนมาช่วยตีความวัตถุ และส่งคืนวัตถุบางชิ้นให้ประเทศต้นทาง
questions.evidence: In response, many museums now invite community members to help interpret objects connected to their own heritage. Some have returned items to the countries from which they were originally taken.
options: They have invited community members to help interpret objects and returned some items to their countries of origin
They have closed the galleries that caused controversy
They have stopped acquiring any new objects
They have sold the objects to private collectors

questions.q: Objects can enter a museum through purchase, donation or archaeological ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ระบุช่องทางการได้มาสามอย่าง คำที่หายไปหลัง archaeological คือ fieldwork ตรงตามข้อความ
questions.evidence: Objects enter a museum through purchase, donation or archaeological fieldwork.

questions.q: An object's provenance is defined in the passage as its history of ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ให้คำนิยาม provenance ว่าคือ history of ownership คำที่ต้องเติมคือ ownership
questions.evidence: examine an item's provenance, meaning its history of ownership

questions.q: Before a gallery opens, museums increasingly test possible displays with ___ drawn from the general public.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าพิพิธภัณฑ์ทดสอบการจัดแสดงกับ focus groups ที่มาจากประชาชนทั่วไป (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: museums also test possible displays with focus groups drawn from the general public before a gallery opens.

title: How Glaciers Shape Landscapes
passage:
[A] Glaciers are massive bodies of ice that form where snow accumulates faster than it melts over many years. As layers build up, the snow compresses into dense ice, and gravity gradually pulls the whole mass downhill. Although a glacier may move only a few centimetres a day, its enormous weight gives it the power to reshape entire landscapes over thousands of years. Today, glaciers cover roughly ten per cent of the Earth's land surface, mainly in Greenland and Antarctica, but during past ice ages they extended over much of North America and northern Europe.

[B] Glaciers alter the land through two principal processes: erosion and deposition. As a glacier moves, it grinds away the underlying rock in a process known as abrasion, using fragments of stone embedded in its base like sandpaper. At the same time, meltwater seeps into cracks in the bedrock and freezes, loosening blocks of rock that the glacier then carries away; geologists call this plucking. Together, these processes can carve deep, U-shaped valleys with steep sides and flat floors, which are strikingly different from the narrow, V-shaped valleys created by rivers.

[C] Mountain regions display some of the most dramatic glacial features. Bowl-shaped hollows called cirques form high on mountainsides where small glaciers begin. When several cirques develop around a single peak, they can leave behind a sharp, pyramid-shaped summit known as a horn, and the Matterhorn in the Alps is a famous example. Where glaciers once flowed into the sea, they cut deep coastal valleys that later flooded, producing the fjords found along the coasts of Norway and New Zealand.

[D] A glacier does not only remove material; it also deposits it. All the rock, gravel and sand carried by the ice, collectively called till, is dropped when the ice melts. Ridges of till known as moraines mark the edges and the furthest advance of a glacier. Retreating glaciers also leave behind drumlins, which are smooth, elongated hills, and erratics, which are large boulders transported far from their source and left standing in unfamiliar surroundings.

[E] Studying these landforms allows scientists to reconstruct the extent of ancient ice sheets and to understand how climate has changed over long periods. Because glaciers respond sensitively to temperature, their present rapid retreat in many parts of the world is regarded as clear evidence of modern warming. The landscapes they leave behind, from the lakes of Finland to the valleys of the Scottish Highlands, remain a permanent record of the ice that once shaped them.

questions.q: Glaciers currently cover about ten per cent of the Earth's land surface.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงว่าปัจจุบันธารน้ำแข็งปกคลุมพื้นผิวดินของโลกประมาณร้อยละสิบ จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Today, glaciers cover roughly ten per cent of the Earth's land surface, mainly in Greenland and Antarctica, but during past ice ages they extended over much of North America and northern Europe.

questions.q: Valleys carved by glaciers have the same shape as valleys formed by rivers.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าหุบเขารูปตัว U ที่ธารน้ำแข็งกัดเซาะแตกต่างอย่างชัดเจนจากหุบเขารูปตัว V ที่เกิดจากแม่น้ำ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้ เป็น False
questions.evidence: Together, these processes can carve deep, U-shaped valleys with steep sides and flat floors, which are strikingly different from the narrow, V-shaped valleys created by rivers.

questions.q: The Matterhorn is the highest mountain in the Alps.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึง Matterhorn เพียงว่าเป็นตัวอย่างของยอดเขาแบบ horn เท่านั้น ไม่ได้พูดถึงความสูงหรือการเป็นยอดเขาที่สูงที่สุด จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: Glaciers typically move several metres every day.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าธารน้ำแข็งอาจเคลื่อนที่เพียงไม่กี่เซนติเมตรต่อวัน ไม่ใช่หลายเมตร จึงขัดแย้งโดยตรง เป็น False
questions.evidence: Although a glacier may move only a few centimetres a day, its enormous weight gives it the power to reshape entire landscapes over thousands of years.

questions.q: Scientists can use glacial landforms to learn about climate change in the past.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการศึกษาภูมิลักษณ์เหล่านี้ช่วยให้นักวิทยาศาสตร์เข้าใจการเปลี่ยนแปลงของสภาพภูมิอากาศในอดีตอันยาวนาน จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Studying these landforms allows scientists to reconstruct the extent of ancient ice sheets and to understand how climate has changed over long periods.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is 'plucking'?
options: the grinding of rock by stones embedded in a glacier's base
the process in which meltwater freezes in cracks and loosens blocks of rock
the dropping of gravel and sand when the ice melts
the flooding of coastal valleys by the sea
questions.a: the process in which meltwater freezes in cracks and loosens blocks of rock
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่า plucking คือการที่น้ำจากการละลายซึมเข้าไปในรอยแตกของหินแล้วแข็งตัว ทำให้ก้อนหินหลุดออกและถูกธารน้ำแข็งพาไป ส่วนการบดหินคือ abrasion
questions.evidence: At the same time, meltwater seeps into cracks in the bedrock and freezes, loosening blocks of rock that the glacier then carries away; geologists call this plucking.

questions.q: A 'horn' is formed when
options: a river cuts a narrow channel through a mountain.
several cirques develop around a single peak.
a glacier deposits ridges of till at its edges.
a deep coastal valley is flooded by the sea.
questions.a: several cirques develop around a single peak.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าเมื่อ cirque หลายแห่งก่อตัวรอบยอดเขาเดียวกัน จะเหลือยอดแหลมรูปพีระมิดที่เรียกว่า horn
questions.evidence: When several cirques develop around a single peak, they can leave behind a sharp, pyramid-shaped summit known as a horn, and the Matterhorn in the Alps is a famous example.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of glacial deposition mentioned in the passage?
options: fjords
cirques
moraines
U-shaped valleys
questions.why_th: Moraine คือสันของ till ที่ธารน้ำแข็งทับถมไว้ ซึ่งอยู่ในย่อหน้าเรื่องการทับถม (deposition) ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นผลจากการกัดเซาะ
questions.evidence: Ridges of till known as moraines mark the edges and the furthest advance of a glacier.

questions.q: The writer mentions the current retreat of glaciers in order to
options: suggest that glaciers will soon disappear completely.
illustrate clear evidence of modern warming.
explain how fjords were originally formed.
compare the landscapes of Finland and Scotland.
questions.a: illustrate clear evidence of modern warming.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการถอยร่นอย่างรวดเร็วของธารน้ำแข็งในปัจจุบันถูกมองว่าเป็นหลักฐานชัดเจนของภาวะโลกร้อนยุคใหม่
questions.evidence: Because glaciers respond sensitively to temperature, their present rapid retreat in many parts of the world is regarded as clear evidence of modern warming.

questions.q: During abrasion, a glacier grinds away rock using fragments of stone embedded in its base like ___.
questions.why_th: บทความเปรียบเทียบเศษหินที่ฝังอยู่ใต้ฐานธารน้ำแข็งว่าทำหน้าที่เหมือนกระดาษทราย (sandpaper) ในการบดหิน
questions.evidence: As a glacier moves, it grinds away the underlying rock in a process known as abrasion, using fragments of stone embedded in its base like sandpaper.

questions.q: The rock, gravel and sand carried by the ice is collectively called ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าวัสดุทั้งหมดที่ธารน้ำแข็งพาไป ทั้งหิน กรวด และทราย เรียกรวมกันว่า till
questions.evidence: All the rock, gravel and sand carried by the ice, collectively called till, is dropped when the ice melts.

questions.q: Large boulders transported far from their source and left in unfamiliar surroundings are known as ___.
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่า erratics คือก้อนหินขนาดใหญ่ที่ถูกพัดพาไปไกลจากแหล่งกำเนิดและถูกทิ้งไว้ในสภาพแวดล้อมที่ไม่คุ้นเคย
questions.evidence: Retreating glaciers also leave behind drumlins, which are smooth, elongated hills, and erratics, which are large boulders transported far from their source and left standing in unfamiliar surroundings.

title: The Psychology of Habit Formation
passage:
[A] Every day, people carry out hundreds of actions without conscious thought. Brushing teeth, locking the front door, or taking a familiar route to work are all examples of habits: behaviours that have become automatic through repetition. Psychologists estimate that approximately forty per cent of daily human behaviour is habitual rather than the result of deliberate decision-making. Because habits require little mental effort, they free the brain to concentrate on more demanding tasks, which is why researchers regard them as essential to normal functioning rather than as a sign of laziness.

[B] According to many psychologists, habits operate through a three-part process often called the habit loop. First, a cue, such as a particular time, place or emotional state, triggers the behaviour. Second, the routine itself is performed. Finally, a reward follows, which teaches the brain that the loop is worth remembering. Neuroscientists have found that habitual behaviour is controlled largely by the basal ganglia, a region deep inside the brain, while conscious decisions depend on the prefrontal cortex. As a behaviour becomes habitual, activity gradually shifts from the prefrontal cortex to the basal ganglia, allowing the action to run with minimal attention.

[C] A common belief holds that a new habit can be formed in twenty-one days, but scientific evidence does not support this figure. In a well-known study at University College London, the researcher Phillippa Lally followed ninety-six volunteers who attempted to establish a new daily habit, such as drinking a glass of water after breakfast or running before dinner. On average, the participants needed sixty-six days before the behaviour became automatic, and the time required ranged from eighteen days to two hundred and fifty-four days. Simple actions became automatic more quickly than complex ones. Interestingly, missing a single day did not significantly reduce a person's chance of success.

[D] Context plays a powerful role in maintaining habits. Because cues are usually embedded in a person's physical surroundings, habits tend to survive as long as the environment remains stable. This explains why major life changes, such as moving to a new city or starting a new job, often disrupt old routines and provide an unusually good opportunity to adopt new ones. For the same reason, psychologists generally advise against relying on willpower alone to break an unwanted habit. Suppressing a behaviour while its cues remain present rarely succeeds; a more effective strategy is to replace the old routine with a new one that responds to the same cue and delivers a similar reward.

questions.q: According to psychologists, roughly forty per cent of everyday human behaviour is habitual.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงกันว่านักจิตวิทยาประเมินว่าประมาณ 40% ของพฤติกรรมประจำวันเป็นนิสัย ไม่ใช่การตัดสินใจโดยตั้งใจ
questions.evidence: Psychologists estimate that approximately forty per cent of daily human behaviour is habitual rather than the result of deliberate decision-making.

questions.q: Researchers consider having many habits to be a sign of laziness.
questions.why_th: บทความขัดแย้งโดยตรง: นักวิจัยมองว่านิสัยเป็นสิ่งจำเป็นต่อการทำงานปกติของสมอง ไม่ใช่สัญญาณของความขี้เกียจ
questions.evidence: Because habits require little mental effort, they free the brain to concentrate on more demanding tasks, which is why researchers regard them as essential to normal functioning rather than as a sign of laziness.

questions.q: The volunteers in Lally's study were all university students.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกเพียงว่ามีอาสาสมัคร 96 คน แต่ไม่ได้ระบุว่าพวกเขาเป็นนักศึกษาหรือไม่ จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: In Lally's study, missing one day greatly lowered a participant's chance of forming a habit.
questions.why_th: บทความขัดแย้งโดยตรง: การพลาดเพียงวันเดียวไม่ได้ลดโอกาสสำเร็จอย่างมีนัยสำคัญ
questions.evidence: Interestingly, missing a single day did not significantly reduce a person's chance of success.

questions.q: Major life changes such as moving to a new city can create a good opportunity to start new habits.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการเปลี่ยนแปลงครั้งใหญ่ในชีวิต เช่น ย้ายเมือง มักทำลายกิจวัตรเดิมและเป็นโอกาสดีเป็นพิเศษในการสร้างนิสัยใหม่
questions.evidence: This explains why major life changes, such as moving to a new city or starting a new job, often disrupt old routines and provide an unusually good opportunity to adopt new ones.

questions.q: According to the passage, which part of the brain largely controls habitual behaviour?
options: the prefrontal cortex
the basal ganglia
the hippocampus
the cerebellum
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าพฤติกรรมที่เป็นนิสัยถูกควบคุมโดย basal ganglia ส่วน prefrontal cortex ใช้สำหรับการตัดสินใจโดยรู้ตัว
questions.evidence: Neuroscientists have found that habitual behaviour is controlled largely by the basal ganglia, a region deep inside the brain, while conscious decisions depend on the prefrontal cortex.

questions.q: In Lally's study, how long on average did participants need before a new behaviour became automatic?
options: twenty-one days
eighteen days
sixty-six days
two hundred and fifty-four days
questions.why_th: ค่าเฉลี่ยคือ 66 วัน ส่วน 18 และ 254 วันคือช่วงต่ำสุด-สูงสุด และ 21 วันคือความเชื่อที่งานวิจัยไม่สนับสนุน
questions.evidence: On average, the participants needed sixty-six days before the behaviour became automatic, and the time required ranged from eighteen days to two hundred and fifty-four days.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the final stage of the habit loop?
options: a cue
a routine
a reward
a decision
questions.why_th: ลำดับของ habit loop คือ cue → routine → reward โดยรางวัลเป็นขั้นสุดท้ายที่สอนสมองให้จดจำวงจร
questions.evidence: Finally, a reward follows, which teaches the brain that the loop is worth remembering.

questions.q: What does the writer suggest is the most effective way to break an unwanted habit?
options: relying on willpower alone
suppressing the behaviour while its cues remain
avoiding all rewards completely
replacing the old routine with a new one
questions.a: replacing the old routine with a new one
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการกดข่มพฤติกรรมมักล้มเหลว วิธีที่ได้ผลกว่าคือแทนที่กิจวัตรเดิมด้วยกิจวัตรใหม่ที่ตอบสนอง cue เดิม
questions.evidence: Suppressing a behaviour while its cues remain present rarely succeeds; a more effective strategy is to replace the old routine with a new one that responds to the same cue and delivers a similar reward.

questions.q: Because habits require little mental effort, the brain is free to concentrate on more ___ tasks.
questions.why_th: คำตอบมาจากประโยคในย่อหน้า [A] โดยตรง: นิสัยช่วยให้สมองไปจดจ่อกับงานที่ยากหรือต้องใช้ความคิดมากกว่า (demanding)
questions.evidence: Because habits require little mental effort, they free the brain to concentrate on more demanding tasks, which is why researchers regard them as essential to normal functioning rather than as a sign of laziness.

questions.q: A cue that triggers a habit can be a particular time, place or ___ state.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่า cue อาจเป็นเวลา สถานที่ หรือสภาวะทางอารมณ์ (emotional state) คำตอบต้องเป็นคำจากบทความตรงตัว
questions.evidence: First, a cue, such as a particular time, place or emotional state, triggers the behaviour.

questions.q: Habits tend to survive as long as the environment remains ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุว่านิสัยจะคงอยู่ตราบใดที่สภาพแวดล้อมยังคงที่ (stable) เพราะ cue ฝังอยู่ในสิ่งแวดล้อมรอบตัว
questions.evidence: Because cues are usually embedded in a person's physical surroundings, habits tend to survive as long as the environment remains stable.

title: How We Perceive Taste and Flavour
topic: The science of taste and flavour
passage:
[A] People often use the words "taste" and "flavour" as if they mean the same thing, but scientists draw a clear distinction between them. Taste refers strictly to the sensations detected by the tongue and other parts of the mouth. For many years, textbooks listed only four basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty and bitter. A fifth taste, known by its Japanese name umami, describes a savoury quality found in foods such as ripe tomatoes, cheese and cooked meat. Umami was first identified by a Japanese chemist in 1908, but Western scientists did not widely accept it as a genuine basic taste until almost a century later.

[B] The tongue is covered with thousands of small structures called taste buds, each of which contains specialised receptor cells. When molecules from food dissolve in saliva, they interact with these receptors and trigger signals that travel along nerves to the brain. A common belief holds that different regions of the tongue are responsible for different tastes, producing a so-called "tongue map". This idea, however, is now regarded as a myth. In reality, all five basic tastes can be detected across the entire surface of the tongue, although sensitivity to each may vary slightly from one area to another.

[C] Flavour is a much broader and more complex experience than taste alone. It arises from the combination of taste, smell and other sensory information, including texture and temperature. Smell, in particular, plays a surprisingly dominant role. As we chew, aromatic compounds released from food travel from the back of the mouth up to the nasal cavity, where they are detected by receptors responsible for smell. Researchers estimate that the majority of what we describe as flavour actually comes from these aromas rather than from taste itself. This explains why food often seems dull and lacking in flavour when a person has a heavy cold and the nose is blocked.

[D] Individual differences also shape how strongly people experience taste. So-called "supertasters" possess an unusually high number of taste buds and tend to find bitter foods, such as certain vegetables, especially unpleasant. Age is another important factor, as the number of taste buds gradually declines over a lifetime, which is one reason why elderly people sometimes complain that food has become bland. Cultural background and repeated exposure further influence preferences, since foods regarded as delicious in one society may be rejected in another. Understanding these mechanisms is not merely of academic interest; it helps food manufacturers design products and assists doctors in treating patients who have lost their sense of taste.

questions.q: Scientists consider taste and flavour to have exactly the same meaning.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์แยกความแตกต่างระหว่างสองคำนี้อย่างชัดเจน จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์
questions.evidence: People often use the words "taste" and "flavour" as if they mean the same thing, but scientists draw a clear distinction between them.

questions.q: Umami was quickly accepted as a basic taste by Western scientists after 1908.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์ตะวันตกไม่ยอมรับจนเกือบหนึ่งศตวรรษต่อมา จึงไม่ใช่การยอมรับอย่างรวดเร็ว
questions.evidence: Umami was first identified by a Japanese chemist in 1908, but Western scientists did not widely accept it as a genuine basic taste until almost a century later.

questions.q: The idea of a "tongue map" is no longer believed to be accurate.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าแนวคิด tongue map ปัจจุบันถือเป็นความเชื่อที่ผิด จึงตรงกับโจทย์
questions.evidence: This idea, however, is now regarded as a myth.

questions.q: A blocked nose during a cold reduces how much flavour a person perceives in food.
questions.why_th: ข้อความอธิบายว่าเมื่อจมูกตันเพราะเป็นหวัด อาหารจะดูจืดและขาดรสชาติ จึงตรงกับโจทย์
questions.evidence: This explains why food often seems dull and lacking in flavour when a person has a heavy cold and the nose is blocked.

questions.q: Supertasters are more likely than other people to become professional chefs.
questions.why_th: ข้อความกล่าวถึงว่า supertasters มีต่อมรับรสจำนวนมากและไม่ชอบรสขม แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวถึงอาชีพเชฟเลย จึงไม่มีข้อมูล

questions.q: According to the passage, umami is best described as
questions.a: a savoury quality found in foods such as ripe tomatoes and cheese
options: a savoury quality found in foods such as ripe tomatoes and cheese
a sweet quality found mainly in cooked meat
a bitter sensation detected only at the back of the tongue
a sour taste identified by Western scientists in 1908
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่า umami เป็นรสชาติแบบ savoury ที่พบในมะเขือเทศสุก ชีส และเนื้อ
questions.evidence: A fifth taste, known by its Japanese name umami, describes a savoury quality found in foods such as ripe tomatoes, cheese and cooked meat.

questions.q: What does the passage say about the five basic tastes and the tongue?
questions.a: All five can be detected across the whole surface of the tongue
options: All five can be detected across the whole surface of the tongue
Each taste is limited to one specific region of the tongue
Only four of them can be detected by the taste buds
Sensitivity to each taste is identical everywhere on the tongue
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่ารสพื้นฐานทั้งห้าสามารถรับรู้ได้ทั่วทั้งลิ้น แม้ความไวจะต่างกันเล็กน้อยในแต่ละบริเวณ
questions.evidence: In reality, all five basic tastes can be detected across the entire surface of the tongue, although sensitivity to each may vary slightly from one area to another.

questions.q: According to the passage, most of what we call flavour actually comes from
questions.a: aromas rather than from taste itself
options: aromas rather than from taste itself
the texture of the food alone
the taste buds on the tongue
the temperature of the food
questions.why_th: นักวิจัยประเมินว่าสิ่งที่เราเรียกว่ารสชาติส่วนใหญ่มาจากกลิ่น ไม่ใช่จากการรับรสโดยตรง
questions.evidence: Researchers estimate that the majority of what we describe as flavour actually comes from these aromas rather than from taste itself.

questions.q: The passage suggests that a decline in the number of taste buds with age can cause elderly people to feel that food
options: has become bland
tastes too bitter
smells unpleasant
is too salty

questions.evidence: Age is another important factor, as the number of taste buds gradually declines over a lifetime, which is one reason why elderly people sometimes complain that food has become bland.

questions.evidence: The tongue is covered with thousands of small structures called taste buds, each of which contains specialised receptor cells.

questions.q: As we chew, aromatic compounds released from food travel up to the ___ cavity, where smell receptors detect them.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าสารกลิ่นเดินทางไปยัง nasal cavity เพื่อให้ตัวรับกลิ่นตรวจจับ

questions.evidence: It arises from the combination of taste, smell and other sensory information, including texture and temperature.

title: The Rise of Remote Work
passage:
[A] For most of the twentieth century, the office was regarded as the natural home of professional work. Employees travelled to a central building each day, and managers measured commitment partly by physical presence. Although a small number of companies experimented with "telecommuting" as early as the 1970s, the practice remained marginal because communication technology was slow and expensive. By 2019, only around five per cent of employees in most industrialised countries worked mainly from home.

[B] This situation changed dramatically in 2020, when a global health crisis forced organisations across the world to close their offices with little warning. Within weeks, millions of employees converted kitchens and spare bedrooms into workplaces. Technologies that had existed for years, such as video conferencing and cloud-based file sharing, suddenly became essential tools rather than optional extras. Many managers who had previously doubted that staff could be productive at home were surprised to find that output remained stable or, in some cases, actually improved.

[C] Supporters of remote work point to several clear benefits. Employees save time and money by avoiding the daily commute, which in large cities can exceed two hours per day. Companies can reduce spending on expensive office space and can recruit talented staff from a much wider geographical area. There is also an environmental argument: fewer commuters means less traffic congestion and lower carbon emissions.

[D] Nevertheless, the shift has created genuine difficulties. Some employees report feeling isolated and find it hard to separate their professional and private lives. Junior workers, in particular, may miss the informal learning that occurs when they observe experienced colleagues in person. Managers also worry about maintaining a shared company culture when staff rarely meet face to face. Partly for these reasons, many organisations have adopted hybrid arrangements, in which employees divide their week between home and the office.

[E] It remains uncertain how far the trend will continue. Surveys consistently show that most employees want to keep at least some flexibility, and companies that insist on a full-time return to the office risk losing staff to more flexible competitors. At the same time, businesses in city centres that depend on office workers, such as cafés and transport networks, have suffered reduced income. Whatever the final balance, few observers expect a complete return to the pre-2020 pattern; remote work, once a rare privilege, has become a permanent feature of the modern labour market.

questions.q: Before 2020, telecommuting was already a common practice in most industrialised countries.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าก่อนปี 2020 การทำงานทางไกลยังเป็นเรื่อง 'ชายขอบ' (marginal) และมีเพียงราว 5% เท่านั้นที่ทำงานจากบ้านเป็นหลัก จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ → False
questions.evidence: Although a small number of companies experimented with "telecommuting" as early as the 1970s, the practice remained marginal because communication technology was slow and expensive.

questions.q: Video conferencing was invented during the crisis of 2020.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเทคโนโลยีอย่าง video conferencing 'มีอยู่แล้วหลายปี' (had existed for years) ก่อนปี 2020 จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความที่ว่าถูกประดิษฐ์ขึ้นในช่วงวิกฤต → False
questions.evidence: Technologies that had existed for years, such as video conferencing and cloud-based file sharing, suddenly became essential tools rather than optional extras.

questions.q: In large cities, daily commuting can take more than two hours.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรง ๆ ว่าการเดินทางไปทำงานในเมืองใหญ่ 'can exceed two hours per day' ตรงกับข้อความ → True
questions.evidence: Employees save time and money by avoiding the daily commute, which in large cities can exceed two hours per day.

questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวว่าพนักงานรุ่นใหม่อาจพลาด 'การเรียนรู้แบบไม่เป็นทางการ' ที่เกิดจากการสังเกตเพื่อนร่วมงานที่มีประสบการณ์แบบตัวต่อตัว แสดงว่าการเรียนรู้แบบนี้มีอยู่จริง → True
questions.evidence: Junior workers, in particular, may miss the informal learning that occurs when they observe experienced colleagues in person.

questions.evidence: Although a small number of companies experimented with "telecommuting" as early as the 1970s, the practice remained marginal because communication technology was slow and expensive.

questions.evidence: Within weeks, millions of employees converted kitchens and spare bedrooms into workplaces.
