questions.why_th: คำตอบมาจากประโยคสุดท้ายของย่อหน้า [E] ตรงตัวว่า 'the modern labour market' (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: Whatever the final balance, few observers expect a complete return to the pre-2020 pattern; remote work, once a rare privilege, has become a permanent feature of the modern labour market.

title: Healing in the Land of the Pharaohs
passage:
[A] The medical tradition of ancient Egypt is among the oldest documented systems of healing in the world. Much of what is known today comes from medical papyri, the most famous of which are the Edwin Smith Papyrus and the Ebers Papyrus, both written around 1600 BCE. The Edwin Smith Papyrus is remarkable for its rational approach: it describes forty-eight cases of injury, listing symptoms, diagnosis and treatment in a systematic manner, with almost no reference to magic. The Ebers Papyrus, by contrast, contains more than seven hundred remedies alongside numerous spells and incantations.

[B] Egyptian physicians, known as swnw, enjoyed a respected position in society and often specialised in particular areas of the body. One inscription refers to a doctor of the eyes, while another names Hesy-Ra, who served the pharaoh around 2650 BCE and is frequently described as the earliest recorded dentist. Medical training took place in institutions called Houses of Life, which were attached to temples and functioned as centres of learning where texts were copied and studied.

[C] Many Egyptian treatments were surprisingly practical. Honey, which has natural antibacterial properties, was regularly applied to open wounds, and raw meat was sometimes used to stop bleeding. Willow, a plant containing the substance from which modern aspirin was later developed, was prescribed to reduce pain and swelling. Physicians also set broken bones with splints and stitched wounds closed. Archaeologists have even discovered a wooden artificial toe attached to a mummy, which some researchers consider the world's earliest functional prosthetic device.

[D] Nevertheless, medicine in Egypt was never entirely separate from religion. Illness with no obvious external cause was frequently attributed to angry gods or hostile spirits, and treatment therefore combined physical remedies with prayers, amulets and ritual. The goddess Sekhmet was believed both to send disease and to cure it, and her priests played an important role in medical practice.

[E] The reputation of Egyptian medicine spread far beyond the country's borders. Greek writers, including Herodotus, praised the skill of Egyptian doctors, and rulers of neighbouring lands are known to have requested their services. Many historians argue that Egyptian knowledge, transmitted through Greece, contributed to the foundations of later European medicine, although the extent of this influence continues to be debated.

questions.q: The Edwin Smith Papyrus contains a large number of magical spells.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า Edwin Smith Papyrus แทบไม่มีการกล่าวถึงเวทมนตร์เลย (almost no reference to magic) จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: The Edwin Smith Papyrus is remarkable for its rational approach: it describes forty-eight cases of injury, listing symptoms, diagnosis and treatment in a systematic manner, with almost no reference to magic.

questions.q: Hesy-Ra is often referred to as the first dentist in recorded history.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรง ๆ ว่า Hesy-Ra มักถูกกล่าวถึงว่าเป็นทันตแพทย์คนแรกสุดที่มีการบันทึกไว้
questions.evidence: One inscription refers to a doctor of the eyes, while another names Hesy-Ra, who served the pharaoh around 2650 BCE and is frequently described as the earliest recorded dentist.

questions.evidence: Honey, which has natural antibacterial properties, was regularly applied to open wounds, and raw meat was sometimes used to stop bleeding.

questions.evidence: Greek writers, including Herodotus, praised the skill of Egyptian doctors, and rulers of neighbouring lands are known to have requested their services.

questions.evidence: The Ebers Papyrus, by contrast, contains more than seven hundred remedies alongside numerous spells and incantations.

questions.evidence: Medical training took place in institutions called Houses of Life, which were attached to temples and functioned as centres of learning where texts were copied and studied.

options: to reduce pain and swelling
to stop bleeding from wounds

questions.evidence: Willow, a plant containing the substance from which modern aspirin was later developed, was prescribed to reduce pain and swelling.

questions.evidence: Archaeologists have even discovered a wooden artificial toe attached to a mummy, which some researchers consider the world's earliest functional prosthetic device.

questions.evidence: Physicians also set broken bones with splints and stitched wounds closed.

questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงการค้นพบนิ้วเท้าเทียมไม้ (wooden artificial toe) ที่ติดอยู่กับมัมมี่ คำตอบคือสองคำนี้จากบทความ
questions.evidence: Archaeologists have even discovered a wooden artificial toe attached to a mummy, which some researchers consider the world's earliest functional prosthetic device.

questions.q: Illness with no obvious external cause was often attributed to angry gods or hostile ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าความเจ็บป่วยที่ไม่มีสาเหตุภายนอกชัดเจนมักถูกโทษว่าเกิดจากเทพเจ้าที่โกรธหรือวิญญาณร้าย (hostile spirits)
questions.evidence: Illness with no obvious external cause was frequently attributed to angry gods or hostile spirits, and treatment therefore combined physical remedies with prayers, amulets and ritual.

title: The economics of fast fashion
passage:
[A] Fast fashion refers to the rapid production of inexpensive clothing that closely follows the latest trends. Over the past three decades, brands have transformed the industry by shortening the time between a design appearing on the catwalk and a finished garment reaching the shelves. Where traditional retailers once released two collections a year, many fast-fashion companies now introduce new styles every few weeks. This speed is the central feature of the business model, and it depends on flexible supply chains that can respond quickly to consumer demand.

[B] The economics of this approach rest on high volume and low prices. By keeping the cost of each item low, retailers encourage shoppers to buy frequently and to view clothing as almost disposable. Manufacturing is typically located in countries where labour is cheap, which reduces production costs considerably. Large orders allow firms to negotiate lower prices for fabric and other materials, and efficient logistics networks move products across the world at remarkable speed. Together, these factors let companies sell garments at a fraction of the price charged by established fashion houses while still earning a healthy profit.

[C] However, the true cost of cheap clothing is often hidden. Environmental researchers point out that the industry consumes enormous quantities of water and energy, and that textile dyeing is a major source of water pollution. Because garments are worn only a few times before being discarded, vast amounts of waste end up in landfill each year. Critics also argue that low prices are sometimes achieved through poor working conditions and inadequate wages in factories abroad. These consequences are not reflected in the price paid at the till, which is one reason economists describe them as external costs.

[D] In response to growing criticism, some companies have begun to promote more sustainable practices, such as recycling schemes and clothing made from organic materials. Yet many observers remain sceptical, suggesting that these measures are modest compared with the scale of production. Genuine change, they argue, would require consumers to buy fewer items and keep them for longer, a habit that runs against the very logic of fast fashion. Whether the industry can reconcile its commercial success with environmental responsibility is likely to remain a difficult question for years to come.

questions.q: Traditional retailers used to release fewer collections per year than fast-fashion companies do today.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าผู้ค้าปลีกแบบดั้งเดิมออกคอลเลกชันปีละสองครั้ง ส่วนแบรนด์ฟาสต์แฟชั่นออกทุกไม่กี่สัปดาห์ จึงน้อยกว่า
questions.evidence: Where traditional retailers once released two collections a year, many fast-fashion companies now introduce new styles every few weeks.

questions.q: Fast-fashion companies are unable to make a profit while selling clothes at low prices.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าบริษัทยังทำกำไรได้ดีแม้ขายราคาถูก จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: Together, these factors let companies sell garments at a fraction of the price charged by established fashion houses while still earning a healthy profit.

questions.q: Textile dyeing contributes significantly to the pollution of water.

questions.evidence: Environmental researchers point out that the industry consumes enormous quantities of water and energy, and that textile dyeing is a major source of water pollution.

questions.q: Fast-fashion garments are more popular with younger shoppers than with older ones.
questions.why_th: ข้อความไม่ได้กล่าวถึงการเปรียบเทียบอายุของผู้ซื้อเลย

questions.evidence: In response to growing criticism, some companies have begun to promote more sustainable practices, such as recycling schemes and clothing made from organic materials.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the central feature of the fast-fashion business model?
questions.a: The speed of moving from design to shelf
options: The speed of moving from design to shelf
The use of high-quality natural fabrics

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าความเร็วเป็นคุณลักษณะหลักของโมเดลธุรกิจ
questions.evidence: This speed is the central feature of the business model, and it depends on flexible supply chains that can respond quickly to consumer demand.

questions.q: Why is manufacturing often located in certain countries?
questions.a: Because labour there is cheap, reducing production costs
options: Because labour there is cheap, reducing production costs
Because those countries have the best fabric quality

questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าการผลิตอยู่ในประเทศที่แรงงานถูก ซึ่งช่วยลดต้นทุนอย่างมาก
questions.evidence: Manufacturing is typically located in countries where labour is cheap, which reduces production costs considerably.

questions.q: What term do economists use for consequences not reflected in the price paid?
options: External costs
Fixed costs

options: Consumers buying fewer items and keeping them longer
Companies releasing collections more frequently

questions.evidence: Genuine change, they argue, would require consumers to buy fewer items and keep them for longer, a habit that runs against the very logic of fast fashion.

questions.q: By keeping the cost of each item low, retailers encourage shoppers to view clothing as almost ___.

questions.evidence: By keeping the cost of each item low, retailers encourage shoppers to buy frequently and to view clothing as almost disposable.

title: Digging the Divide: The Building of the Panama Canal
topic: The construction of the Panama Canal
passage:
[A] The Panama Canal, a waterway of roughly 80 kilometres that links the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, is widely regarded as one of the greatest engineering achievements of the modern era. Before it opened in 1914, ships travelling between the two oceans had to sail around the southern tip of South America, a long and dangerous route. The idea of cutting a passage through the narrow strip of land in Central America had existed for centuries, but the technical difficulties and the region's harsh climate made the project seem almost impossible.

[B] The first serious attempt began in 1881, led by the French engineer Ferdinand de Lesseps, who had earlier overseen the construction of the Suez Canal in Egypt. De Lesseps insisted on building a sea-level canal without locks, a decision that proved disastrous in the mountainous terrain of Panama. Frequent landslides filled the excavations, and machinery struggled in the heat and rain. Even more devastating was disease: thousands of workers died from malaria and yellow fever, illnesses that were not yet understood to be spread by mosquitoes. By 1889 the French company had collapsed financially, and the effort was abandoned.

[C] In 1904 the United States took control of the project and adopted a different strategy. Rather than a sea-level canal, American engineers built a system of locks that raised and lowered ships across the higher ground. A crucial early step was a campaign against disease led by Dr William Gorgas, who ordered the draining of swamps and the elimination of mosquito breeding grounds. These measures dramatically reduced deaths and allowed work to continue. The construction also required the creation of Gatun Lake, formed by damming the Chagres River, which was at the time the largest artificial lake in the world.

[D] The canal was completed in 1914, several months ahead of schedule. Its impact on global trade was immediate and profound, cutting the sea journey between New York and San Francisco by roughly 13,000 kilometres. The project had come at an enormous human cost, however, with an estimated 25,000 workers dying during the French and American periods combined. Today the canal remains a vital route for international shipping, and a major expansion completed in 2016 added a new set of larger locks to accommodate the enormous container vessels of the modern age.

questions.q: Before the canal opened, ships sailing between the Atlantic and Pacific had to travel around the northern tip of South America.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าเรือต้องอ้อมปลายด้านใต้ (southern tip) ไม่ใช่ด้านเหนือ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Before it opened in 1914, ships travelling between the two oceans had to sail around the southern tip of South America, a long and dangerous route.

questions.q: Ferdinand de Lesseps had been involved in building the Suez Canal before working in Panama.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเดอ เลสเซปส์เคยดูแลการสร้างคลองสุเอซมาก่อน จึงเป็นจริง

title: From Laboratory to Clinic: The Making of a Vaccine
topic: How vaccines are developed and tested
passage:
[A] A vaccine is a biological preparation that trains the immune system to recognise and defend against a particular disease. The development of a new vaccine is a lengthy process that typically takes between ten and fifteen years, although this timeline can be shortened during a health emergency. The work begins in the laboratory, where scientists study the structure of a virus or bacterium and try to identify the part of it, known as the antigen, that will provoke a protective response from the body. This early stage is often called the exploratory phase, and most candidate vaccines never progress beyond it.

[B] Once a promising candidate has been identified, it enters what researchers call the pre-clinical stage. During this period, the vaccine is tested on cells grown in the laboratory and, later, on animals such as mice or monkeys. The main purpose of these tests is to establish whether the vaccine is safe and whether it produces the immune response that scientists expect. Many candidates fail here because they do not generate enough protection or because they cause unwanted side effects. Only a small proportion of vaccines are eventually approved for testing in humans.

[C] Human testing is divided into three phases, known as clinical trials. In Phase I, the vaccine is given to a small group of healthy adult volunteers, usually fewer than a hundred, in order to check its safety and to determine the correct dose. Phase II involves several hundred people and examines the immune response in greater detail. Phase III is the largest and most important stage, sometimes enrolling tens of thousands of participants across many countries. In this phase, half of the volunteers receive the vaccine while the others are given a harmless substance called a placebo, so that researchers can compare the two groups and measure how well the vaccine actually prevents infection.

[D] After the trials are complete, the results are submitted to a national regulatory authority, which examines the evidence and decides whether the vaccine may be sold to the public. Even when a vaccine has been licensed, the monitoring does not stop. Scientists continue to observe how it performs once millions of people have received it, a process sometimes referred to as Phase IV or post-marketing surveillance. This ongoing checking allows rare side effects to be detected that would not have appeared in the smaller trials, and it ensures that the benefits of the vaccine continue to outweigh any risks.

questions.q: The development of a new vaccine usually takes more than a decade.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าใช้เวลา 10 ถึง 15 ปี ซึ่งมากกว่าหนึ่งทศวรรษ จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: The development of a new vaccine is a lengthy process that typically takes between ten and fifteen years, although this timeline can be shortened during a health emergency.

questions.q: Most candidate vaccines successfully move past the exploratory phase.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่า candidate ส่วนใหญ่ไม่ผ่านขั้นนี้ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ เป็น False
questions.evidence: This early stage is often called the exploratory phase, and most candidate vaccines never progress beyond it.

title: The Bilingual Brain: How We Acquire a Second Language
topic: How the brain learns a second language
passage:
[A] Learning a second language is one of the most demanding tasks the human brain can undertake, yet millions of people accomplish it every year. Unlike a first language, which children absorb effortlessly through daily exposure, a second language is often studied deliberately in classrooms and requires conscious effort. Researchers who study this process are interested in what happens inside the brain as an adult moves from knowing only a few isolated words to speaking with reasonable fluency.

[B] Much of the early research focused on the idea of a "critical period", a stage in childhood during which language is thought to be learned most easily. According to this view, the ability to master a new language declines sharply after puberty. However, more recent studies suggest that the picture is less rigid than once believed. While young children usually acquire a native-like accent more readily than adults, older learners often make faster progress with vocabulary and grammar because they can rely on skills such as memory strategies and an existing understanding of how language works.

[C] Brain imaging has transformed the field. Using scanners, researchers can observe which regions become active when a person processes a foreign word. Two areas are especially important: Broca's area, linked to the production of speech, and Wernicke's area, associated with understanding meaning. In beginners, additional regions are recruited as the brain works hard to decode unfamiliar sounds. As learners become more proficient, this activity becomes more efficient and begins to resemble the pattern seen when they use their first language.

[D] One of the brain's most remarkable qualities is its plasticity, the capacity to reorganise itself in response to experience. Studies have found that people who speak two languages tend to have a greater density of grey matter in certain regions than those who speak only one. This suggests that the mental exercise of managing two languages physically shapes the brain. Some scientists argue that this constant switching may even delay the onset of certain age-related cognitive difficulties, although the evidence remains debated.

[E] Ultimately, the research points to a hopeful conclusion for adult learners. Although childhood may offer advantages, the brain remains capable of change throughout life. Regular practice, meaningful exposure, and genuine motivation appear to matter more than age alone. For those willing to persevere, the human brain provides the flexibility needed to make a second language a lasting part of one's mind.

questions.q: Children generally learn their first language without conscious effort.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A บอกว่าเด็กซึมซับภาษาแรกอย่างไม่ต้องใช้ความพยายาม (effortlessly)
questions.evidence: Unlike a first language, which children absorb effortlessly through daily exposure, a second language is often studied deliberately in classrooms and requires conscious effort.

questions.q: Recent studies have fully confirmed that language ability disappears completely after puberty.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าการศึกษาใหม่ ๆ ชี้ว่าภาพนี้ไม่ตายตัวอย่างที่เคยเชื่อ ขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'disappears completely'
questions.evidence: However, more recent studies suggest that the picture is less rigid than once believed.

questions.q: Adult learners often make quicker progress than children in learning vocabulary and grammar.

questions.evidence: While young children usually acquire a native-like accent more readily than adults, older learners often make faster progress with vocabulary and grammar because they can rely on skills such as memory strategies and an existing understanding of how language works.

questions.q: Broca's area is more important for learning a second language than Wernicke's area.
questions.why_th: ข้อความกล่าวถึงทั้งสองบริเวณว่าสำคัญ แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบว่าบริเวณใดสำคัญกว่ากัน

questions.evidence: Although childhood may offer advantages, the brain remains capable of change throughout life.
options: The brain remains capable of change throughout life.

questions.q: The brain's ability to reorganise itself in response to experience is known as its ___.

questions.evidence: One of the brain's most remarkable qualities is its plasticity, the capacity to reorganise itself in response to experience.

questions.q: The idea of a ___ refers to a stage in childhood when language is thought to be learned most easily.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ใช้คำว่า 'critical period' หมายถึงช่วงในวัยเด็กที่เรียนภาษาได้ง่ายที่สุด
questions.evidence: Much of the early research focused on the idea of a "critical period", a stage in childhood during which language is thought to be learned most easily.

questions.q: Wernicke's area is associated with understanding ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่า Wernicke's area เกี่ยวข้องกับการเข้าใจความหมาย (meaning)
questions.evidence: Two areas are especially important: Broca's area, linked to the production of speech, and Wernicke's area, associated with understanding meaning.

title: The Origins of the Olympic Games
topic: The origins of the Olympic Games
passage:
[A] The Olympic Games began in ancient Greece more than 2,700 years ago. According to tradition, the first recorded competition was held in the year 776 BC at Olympia, a religious site in the western part of the country. The Games were staged in honour of Zeus, the chief god of the Greeks, and formed part of a larger festival that combined worship, sport and celebration. From the beginning, the Games were held every four years, and this period of time became known as an Olympiad. The Greeks even used the sequence of Olympiads as a way of measuring and recording their history.

[B] In the earliest Games, the only event was a short running race called the stade, which covered a distance of about 190 metres. Over the following centuries, however, the programme gradually expanded. New contests were introduced, including longer foot races, wrestling, boxing and the pentathlon, a demanding event that combined running, jumping, throwing and wrestling. Chariot racing, which required both wealth and skill, became one of the most popular attractions and drew enormous crowds. Only free-born Greek men were permitted to take part in the competitions; women, slaves and foreigners were excluded from entering.

[C] Competing at Olympia was regarded as a great honour rather than a way of earning money. Victorious athletes did not receive cash prizes at the Games themselves. Instead, the winner of each event was awarded a simple crown made from the leaves of a wild olive tree. Despite the modest nature of this reward, successful athletes often returned home to a hero's welcome, and their cities frequently granted them generous gifts, free meals and other privileges for the rest of their lives.

[D] The Games also had an important political dimension. During the festival, a truce known as the ekecheiria was declared across the Greek world. This agreement allowed athletes and spectators to travel safely to Olympia, even when their home states were at war with one another. Although the truce did not always prevent conflict entirely, it reflected the belief that the Games were more important than ordinary rivalries.

[E] The ancient Olympic Games continued for almost twelve centuries. They were finally brought to an end in AD 393, when the Roman emperor Theodosius I banned them, partly because they were associated with pagan religion. The Games were not revived until 1896, when the first modern Olympics were held in Athens, inspired by the traditions of the ancient world.

questions.q: The first recorded Olympic Games took place at Olympia in 776 BC.
options: True
False

title: The Hidden Costs of a Noisy World
passage:
[A] Noise pollution is often described as an invisible danger. Unlike air or water pollution, it leaves no visible trace, yet researchers now regard it as one of the most widespread environmental threats to human health. The World Health Organization has identified traffic noise as the second most harmful environmental factor in Western Europe, ranking only behind air pollution. As cities continue to grow and transport networks expand, the number of people exposed to high levels of unwanted sound is rising steadily.

[B] The most obvious consequence of prolonged noise exposure is damage to hearing. When people are subjected to loud sounds over many years, the delicate cells inside the inner ear can be permanently harmed. This condition, known as noise-induced hearing loss, is common among workers in factories, construction sites and airports. However, scientists have discovered that the effects of noise extend far beyond the ears. The body appears to treat loud or persistent sound as a form of stress, triggering physical reactions that were originally designed to help humans respond to danger.

[C] These stress reactions can have serious long-term consequences. When a person hears a sudden or continuous noise, the body releases hormones such as adrenaline and cortisol. These hormones raise blood pressure and increase the heart rate. If the exposure happens repeatedly, the cardiovascular system remains under constant pressure, which may eventually contribute to heart disease and stroke. Several studies have found that people living near busy roads or airports have a higher risk of developing high blood pressure than those in quieter areas.

[D] Sleep is another area strongly affected by environmental noise. Even when a sleeper does not fully wake up, background sounds can disturb the natural stages of sleep, reducing its quality. Poor sleep in turn affects concentration, mood and the ability to learn. Research involving children living close to airports has shown that they often perform worse on reading tests than children in quieter neighbourhoods. This suggests that noise may interfere with cognitive development during important early years.

[E] Reducing noise pollution is therefore an important public health goal. Governments can introduce measures such as building barriers along motorways, planting trees, and limiting flights during the night. Quieter road surfaces and stricter regulations on vehicles can also make a difference. While complete silence is neither possible nor desirable in a modern society, experts argue that lowering excessive noise would bring considerable benefits to both physical and mental wellbeing.

questions.q: The World Health Organization considers traffic noise to be the most harmful environmental factor in Western Europe.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าเสียงจราจรเป็นอันดับสอง รองจากมลพิษทางอากาศ ไม่ใช่อันดับหนึ่ง
questions.evidence: The World Health Organization has identified traffic noise as the second most harmful environmental factor in Western Europe, ranking only behind air pollution.

title: Life in the Rainforest Canopy
passage:
[A] The canopy of a tropical rainforest is the upper layer formed where the crowns of tall trees meet, typically between twenty and forty metres above the forest floor. For a long time this region remained one of the least studied habitats on Earth, simply because it was so difficult for researchers to reach. Scientists once relied on studying leaves and branches that had fallen to the ground, which gave only a partial picture. Today, a range of new methods, including construction cranes, walkways suspended between trees, and even hot-air balloons fitted with inflatable platforms, allow biologists to work directly among the treetops.

[B] The canopy is remarkable for its extraordinary biological richness. Although it occupies only a thin band of the forest, it is thought to contain the majority of the species found in the rainforest as a whole. Many of these organisms never descend to the ground during their entire lives. Insects are especially abundant, and some scientists estimate that a single large tree may support several thousand different kinds. Birds, monkeys, frogs and countless plants also make their homes there, taking advantage of the sunlight that the lower layers of the forest rarely receive.

[C] Conditions in the canopy are very different from those below. Because it is fully exposed to the sun, the temperature is higher and the air is drier than on the shaded forest floor. Winds are also stronger. To survive, plants growing on the branches have developed clever ways of collecting and storing water. Some, known as epiphytes, grow on other plants rather than in soil, and gather rainwater in cup-shaped arrangements of leaves. These small pools become tiny habitats in their own right, providing homes for insects and even for frogs that breed in them.

[D] The canopy plays an important part in the wider environment. Its leaves absorb large quantities of carbon dioxide and release oxygen, helping to regulate the composition of the atmosphere. The dense mass of foliage also intercepts rainfall, reducing the force with which water strikes the ground and thereby limiting soil erosion. Despite this importance, the canopy is under threat from logging and clearance for agriculture. Because so many species are concentrated in this layer, its destruction could lead to the loss of plants and animals that have not yet even been described by science.

questions.evidence: Scientists once relied on studying leaves and branches that had fallen to the ground, which gave only a partial picture.

title: The Psychology of Queuing
passage:
[A] Queuing, or waiting in line, is an unavoidable feature of modern life. One British study estimated that the average person spends almost six months of their life waiting in queues of one kind or another. Yet psychologists have discovered that how long a wait feels often matters more than how long it actually is. Two customers may wait for exactly the same number of minutes, but one may leave satisfied while the other leaves angry, depending on how the waiting experience was managed.

[B] Much of what is known about this subject comes from the work of the American academic David Maister, who argued in the 1980s that occupied time feels shorter than unoccupied time. When people have something to do or to watch, they pay less attention to the passing minutes. A famous example concerns a group of office buildings in New York, where tenants complained repeatedly about slow lifts. Instead of installing faster lifts, which would have been extremely expensive, the management placed mirrors beside the lift doors. The complaints almost completely disappeared, because people occupied themselves by checking their appearance. Similarly, one large airport reduced complaints about baggage delays not by delivering luggage faster, but by moving the arrival gates further from the baggage hall, so that passengers spent more time walking and less time standing still.

[C] Uncertainty and unfairness also make waits feel longer. Research shows that a wait of unknown length feels considerably longer than a wait whose end can be predicted, which is why many organisations now display estimated waiting times. Fairness matters just as much. Customers become frustrated when someone who arrived later is served first, a situation that commonly occurs when each counter has its own separate line. For this reason, many banks and fast-food chains have adopted a single winding queue that feeds all counters, ensuring that customers are served strictly in order of arrival.

[D] Modern technology has extended these principles. Numbered tickets and smartphone apps allow people to join a virtual queue and spend the waiting period sitting down or shopping instead of standing in line. Some theme parks deliberately overestimate the published waiting time for popular rides, so that visitors feel pleasantly surprised when they reach the front earlier than expected. The lesson from decades of research is clear: for businesses, changing how a wait feels can be cheaper and more effective than shortening the wait itself.

questions.q: According to a British study, the average person spends nearly six months of their life queuing.
questions.why_th: ตรงกับข้อมูลในย่อหน้า A ที่ระบุว่างานวิจัยของอังกฤษประมาณว่าคนทั่วไปใช้เวลาเกือบหกเดือนของชีวิตไปกับการต่อคิว

to attract new tenants to the buildings
questions.a: to give people something to do while waiting
questions.why_th: กระจกทำให้คนใช้เวลาไปกับการดูตัวเอง (occupied time) จึงรู้สึกว่ารอไม่นานและเลิกบ่น ตามหลักของ Maister
questions.evidence: The complaints almost completely disappeared, because people occupied themselves by checking their appearance.

questions.q: According to the passage, customers become frustrated when
options: someone who arrived later is served first
estimated waiting times are displayed
they have to walk further than expected
staff at the counters work too slowly
questions.a: someone who arrived later is served first
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าความไม่ยุติธรรมทำให้ลูกค้าหงุดหงิด เมื่อคนที่มาทีหลังได้รับบริการก่อน
questions.evidence: Customers become frustrated when someone who arrived later is served first, a situation that commonly occurs when each counter has its own separate line.

questions.q: Why do some theme parks overestimate the published waiting time for popular rides?
options: so that visitors feel pleasantly surprised
so that visitors choose less popular rides
so that the parks can sell more tickets
so that queues become shorter overall
questions.a: so that visitors feel pleasantly surprised
questions.why_th: สวนสนุกตั้งใจบอกเวลารอเกินจริง เพื่อให้ผู้เข้าชมรู้สึกดีใจเมื่อถึงคิวเร็วกว่าที่คาดไว้
questions.evidence: Some theme parks deliberately overestimate the published waiting time for popular rides, so that visitors feel pleasantly surprised when they reach the front earlier than expected.

questions.q: What is the main conclusion of the passage?
options: Businesses should always invest in reducing actual waiting times.
Changing how a wait feels can be cheaper and more effective than shortening the wait itself.
Modern technology has completely eliminated the need to queue.
Customers prefer standing in a single line to sitting down.
questions.a: Changing how a wait feels can be cheaper and more effective than shortening the wait itself.
questions.why_th: ประโยคสรุปท้ายบทความระบุชัดว่า การเปลี่ยนความรู้สึกระหว่างรออาจถูกกว่าและได้ผลกว่าการทำให้เวลารอสั้นลงจริง ๆ
questions.evidence: The lesson from decades of research is clear: for businesses, changing how a wait feels can be cheaper and more effective than shortening the wait itself.

questions.q: David Maister argued that occupied time feels shorter than ___ time.
questions.why_th: คำตอบมาจากประโยคที่สรุปแนวคิดของ Maister ว่าเวลาที่มีอะไรทำรู้สึกสั้นกว่าเวลาที่ว่างเปล่า (unoccupied time)
questions.evidence: Much of what is known about this subject comes from the work of the American academic David Maister, who argued in the 1980s that occupied time feels shorter than unoccupied time.

questions.q: Many banks and fast-food chains use a single winding queue so that customers are served strictly in order of ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าคิวเดี่ยวแบบคดเคี้ยวช่วยให้ลูกค้าได้รับบริการตามลำดับการมาถึง (order of arrival)
questions.evidence: For this reason, many banks and fast-food chains have adopted a single winding queue that feeds all counters, ensuring that customers are served strictly in order of arrival.

questions.q: Numbered tickets and smartphone apps allow people to join a ___ queue instead of standing in line.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D กล่าวว่าเทคโนโลยีช่วยให้คนเข้าคิวเสมือน (virtual queue) แล้วไปนั่งหรือช้อปปิ้งระหว่างรอได้
questions.evidence: Numbered tickets and smartphone apps allow people to join a virtual queue and spend the waiting period sitting down or shopping instead of standing in line.

title: The Evolution of the Alphabet
passage:
[A] For most of human history, people had no means of recording their thoughts beyond memory and speech. The earliest writing systems, which emerged in Mesopotamia and Egypt more than five thousand years ago, changed this permanently. However, these systems were extremely difficult to master. Egyptian hieroglyphs and Mesopotamian cuneiform each contained hundreds of separate symbols, many of which represented whole words or ideas rather than sounds. As a result, reading and writing remained the privilege of a small class of professional scribes, who trained for many years to learn their craft.

[B] The first step towards a simpler system was taken around 1850 BCE by Semitic-speaking workers living in Egypt. Instead of memorising hundreds of signs, they borrowed a small number of Egyptian symbols and used each one to stand for a single consonant sound in their own language. This early script, known today as Proto-Sinaitic, contained fewer than thirty characters. Because each symbol represented a sound rather than a complete word, learners could combine a limited set of letters to write any word they could pronounce.

[C] This principle was later refined by the Phoenicians, a trading people based on the coast of the eastern Mediterranean. By about 1050 BCE they had developed a practical script of just twenty-two letters, all of them consonants. Phoenician merchants carried their writing system to the ports where they did business, and neighbouring cultures gradually adapted it for their own languages. The script's simplicity was central to its success: it could be learned quickly, which made written records available to traders and craftsmen, not only to specialist scribes.

[D] The Greeks, who acquired the Phoenician script around the eighth century BCE, made a modification of lasting importance. Their language could not be written clearly with consonants alone, so they reassigned certain Phoenician letters to represent vowel sounds. The result is generally considered the first true alphabet, in which both consonants and vowels were recorded. From Greece, the alphabet passed to the Etruscans of Italy and then to the Romans, whose Latin version spread across Europe with the expansion of their empire.

[E] The alphabet has continued to evolve since Roman times. Medieval scribes developed lowercase letters, and the invention of printing in the fifteenth century helped to standardise letter shapes across Europe. Today the Latin alphabet is the most widely used writing system in the world, employed by billions of people, although its individual letters still preserve traces of the ancient symbols from which they descend.

questions.q: In early Mesopotamia and Egypt, most people could read and write.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการอ่านเขียนเป็นสิทธิพิเศษของอาลักษณ์มืออาชีพกลุ่มเล็ก ๆ เท่านั้น จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: As a result, reading and writing remained the privilege of a small class of professional scribes, who trained for many years to learn their craft.

questions.q: The Proto-Sinaitic script contained more than thirty characters.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าอักษร Proto-Sinaitic มีตัวอักษรน้อยกว่าสามสิบตัว (fewer than thirty) ซึ่งตรงข้ามกับ more than thirty
questions.evidence: This early script, known today as Proto-Sinaitic, contained fewer than thirty characters.

questions.q: The Phoenician script could be learned in a short time.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุชัดว่าอักษรฟินิเชียนเรียนรู้ได้อย่างรวดเร็ว (it could be learned quickly) จึงสอดคล้องกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: The script's simplicity was central to its success: it could be learned quickly, which made written records available to traders and craftsmen, not only to specialist scribes.

questions.q: The Phoenicians developed their script in order to record religious ceremonies.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงชาวฟินิเชียนในฐานะพ่อค้าและการค้าขายเท่านั้น ไม่ได้พูดถึงจุดประสงค์ทางศาสนาใด ๆ จึงไม่มีข้อมูลยืนยันหรือขัดแย้ง

questions.q: The invention of printing helped to make letter shapes more uniform across Europe.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการประดิษฐ์การพิมพ์ในศตวรรษที่ 15 ช่วยทำให้รูปทรงตัวอักษรเป็นมาตรฐานเดียวกันทั่วยุโรป (standardise = uniform)
questions.evidence: Medieval scribes developed lowercase letters, and the invention of printing in the fifteenth century helped to standardise letter shapes across Europe.

questions.q: According to the passage, why was literacy limited to a small group in early Mesopotamia and Egypt?
options: The writing systems contained hundreds of symbols and took years to master
Writing materials were too expensive for ordinary people
Governments prevented ordinary people from learning to write
The scribes refused to share their knowledge with others
questions.a: The writing systems contained hundreds of symbols and took years to master
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าระบบเขียนยุคแรกมีสัญลักษณ์หลายร้อยตัวและยากมาก อาลักษณ์ต้องฝึกหลายปี ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีกล่าวถึงในบทความ
questions.evidence: Egyptian hieroglyphs and Mesopotamian cuneiform each contained hundreds of separate symbols, many of which represented whole words or ideas rather than sounds.

questions.q: What was the key feature of the Proto-Sinaitic script?
options: Each symbol stood for a single consonant sound
It contained more symbols than Egyptian hieroglyphs
It recorded both consonants and vowels
It was used mainly for trade agreements
questions.a: Each symbol stood for a single consonant sound
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าคนงานเซมิติกยืมสัญลักษณ์อียิปต์มาใช้แทนเสียงพยัญชนะเสียงเดียวต่อหนึ่งสัญลักษณ์ ส่วนสระเพิ่งถูกเพิ่มโดยชาวกรีกภายหลัง
questions.evidence: Instead of memorising hundreds of signs, they borrowed a small number of Egyptian symbols and used each one to stand for a single consonant sound in their own language.

questions.q: Why is the Greek script generally considered the first true alphabet?
options: It recorded both consonants and vowels
It contained exactly twenty-two letters
It was the first script spread by merchants
It was the first script to use lowercase letters
questions.a: It recorded both consonants and vowels
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าชาวกรีกกำหนดตัวอักษรบางตัวให้แทนเสียงสระ ทำให้บันทึกได้ทั้งพยัญชนะและสระ จึงถือเป็นอักษรแท้ระบบแรก
questions.evidence: The result is generally considered the first true alphabet, in which both consonants and vowels were recorded.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did the Latin alphabet spread across Europe?
options: Through the expansion of the Roman Empire
Through the voyages of Phoenician merchants
Through the work of medieval scribes
Through the invention of printing
questions.a: Through the expansion of the Roman Empire
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าอักษรละตินของชาวโรมันแพร่ไปทั่วยุโรปพร้อมกับการขยายตัวของจักรวรรดิโรมัน ตัวเลือกอื่นเกี่ยวกับขั้นตอนอื่นของประวัติศาสตร์
questions.evidence: From Greece, the alphabet passed to the Etruscans of Italy and then to the Romans, whose Latin version spread across Europe with the expansion of their empire.

questions.q: By about 1050 BCE the Phoenicians had developed a practical script of just twenty-two letters, all of them ___.
questions.why_th: ตามบทความ อักษรฟินิเชียนมี 22 ตัว และทั้งหมดเป็นพยัญชนะ (consonants) คำนี้นำมาจากประโยคในบทความโดยตรง
questions.evidence: By about 1050 BCE they had developed a practical script of just twenty-two letters, all of them consonants.

questions.q: The Greeks reassigned certain Phoenician letters to represent ___ sounds.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าชาวกรีกนำตัวอักษรฟินิเชียนบางตัวมาใช้แทนเสียงสระ (vowel sounds) เพราะภาษากรีกเขียนด้วยพยัญชนะอย่างเดียวไม่ชัดเจน
questions.evidence: Their language could not be written clearly with consonants alone, so they reassigned certain Phoenician letters to represent vowel sounds.

questions.q: Today the Latin alphabet is the most widely used ___ in the world.
questions.why_th: คำตอบคือ writing system (ระบบการเขียน) ซึ่งเป็นคำสองคำที่ปรากฏตรงตามประโยคสุดท้ายของบทความ
questions.evidence: Today the Latin alphabet is the most widely used writing system in the world, employed by billions of people, although its individual letters still preserve traces of the ancient symbols from which they descend.

title: The Physics of Sound Insulation
passage:
[A] Sound is a form of energy that travels through air, liquids and solids as a series of pressure waves. When a machine operates or a person speaks, vibrations push against nearby air molecules, which pass the disturbance on to their neighbours. The loudness of a sound is measured in decibels, while its pitch depends on its frequency, measured in hertz. Low-frequency sounds, such as traffic rumble or bass music, have long wavelengths and are notoriously difficult to block, whereas high-frequency sounds are absorbed far more easily.

[B] The most basic principle of sound insulation is known as the mass law: the heavier a barrier is, the more sound energy it reflects. Doubling the mass of a wall reduces the sound passing through it by roughly six decibels. For this reason, dense materials such as concrete, brick and thick glass have traditionally been used in building construction. However, heavy walls are costly and impractical in many modern buildings, which increasingly depend on lightweight steel and timber frames.

[C] Engineers therefore developed a second strategy called decoupling. When two layers of material are separated by an air gap, sound waves lose energy each time they move from a solid into the air and back again. This explains why a cavity wall, made of two thin panels with a space between them, can block more noise than a single solid wall of the same total weight. Double glazing applies the same idea to windows, and the performance improves further when the two panes of glass have different thicknesses, because each pane weakens a different range of frequencies.

[D] A third technique is absorption. Porous materials such as mineral wool and acoustic foam contain millions of tiny air pockets, and when sound waves enter them, the vibrating air is slowed by friction and the energy is converted into small amounts of heat. Builders often place these materials inside wall cavities to stop sound bouncing back and forth between the panels. Absorption alone, however, cannot block noise: a foam panel may improve the sound quality inside a recording studio, but it does little to prevent noise travelling through the wall to the room next door.

[E] Even a carefully insulated wall can be defeated by flanking transmission, in which sound bypasses the barrier by travelling through connected floors, ceilings, pipes or ventilation ducts. For this reason, acoustic engineers treat a building as a complete system rather than a set of separate surfaces. Many countries now set legal minimum standards for sound insulation in new apartments, and these are checked with on-site tests before residents move in.

questions.q: Sound can travel through liquids as well as through air.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุชัดเจนว่าเสียงเดินทางผ่านอากาศ ของเหลว และของแข็งได้ ข้อความนี้ตรงกับบทความ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Sound is a form of energy that travels through air, liquids and solids as a series of pressure waves.

questions.q: High-frequency sounds are more difficult to block than low-frequency sounds.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเสียงความถี่ต่ำบล็อกได้ยาก ส่วนเสียงความถี่สูงถูกดูดซับได้ง่ายกว่า ข้อความในโจทย์กลับด้านกับบทความ จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: Low-frequency sounds, such as traffic rumble or bass music, have long wavelengths and are notoriously difficult to block, whereas high-frequency sounds are absorbed far more easily.

questions.q: A wall consisting of two panels separated by a gap can reduce noise better than a solid wall of equal weight.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าผนังสองชั้นที่มีช่องว่างอากาศ (cavity wall) กันเสียงได้ดีกว่าผนังทึบชั้นเดียวที่มีน้ำหนักรวมเท่ากัน ข้อความนี้ตรงกับบทความ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: This explains why a cavity wall, made of two thin panels with a space between them, can block more noise than a single solid wall of the same total weight.

questions.q: Mineral wool was originally developed for use in recording studios.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึง mineral wool ว่าเป็นวัสดุพรุนที่ใช้ดูดซับเสียง แต่ไม่ได้พูดถึงประวัติหรือจุดประสงค์แรกเริ่มของการพัฒนาวัสดุนี้เลย จึงตอบ Not Given

questions.q: Acoustic engineers consider each wall of a building as a separate surface.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E บอกว่าวิศวกรด้านเสียงมองอาคารเป็นระบบเดียวทั้งหมด ไม่ใช่พื้นผิวแยกกันเป็นชิ้น ๆ ข้อความนี้ขัดแย้งกับบทความโดยตรง จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: For this reason, acoustic engineers treat a building as a complete system rather than a set of separate surfaces.

questions.q: According to the mass law, what happens when the mass of a wall is doubled?
options: The sound passing through it drops by roughly six decibels
The wall reflects all sound frequencies equally
The sound passing through it is completely blocked
The wall absorbs twice as much heat
questions.a: The sound passing through it drops by roughly six decibels
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ระบุว่าการเพิ่มมวลของผนังเป็นสองเท่าจะลดเสียงที่ผ่านได้ประมาณ 6 เดซิเบล ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในบทความ
questions.evidence: Doubling the mass of a wall reduces the sound passing through it by roughly six decibels.

questions.q: Why do many modern buildings avoid heavy walls?
options: Heavy walls block too much useful sound
Heavy walls are costly and impractical
Heavy walls are banned by legal standards
Heavy walls absorb moisture from the air
questions.a: Heavy walls are costly and impractical
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่าผนังหนักมีราคาแพงและไม่สะดวกในอาคารสมัยใหม่ ซึ่งหันไปใช้โครงเหล็กและไม้น้ำหนักเบาแทน
questions.evidence: However, heavy walls are costly and impractical in many modern buildings, which increasingly depend on lightweight steel and timber frames.

questions.q: Why does double glazing perform better when the two panes of glass have different thicknesses?
options: Each pane weakens a different range of frequencies
The air gap between the panes becomes wider
The thicker pane reflects all of the sound
The window becomes heavier than a solid wall
questions.a: Each pane weakens a different range of frequencies
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C อธิบายว่ากระจกสองบานที่หนาไม่เท่ากันจะช่วยลดเสียงคนละช่วงความถี่ ทำให้ประสิทธิภาพรวมดีขึ้น
questions.evidence: Double glazing applies the same idea to windows, and the performance improves further when the two panes of glass have different thicknesses, because each pane weakens a different range of frequencies.

questions.q: What happens to sound energy inside porous materials such as acoustic foam?
options: It is reflected back into the room
It is turned into electrical energy
It is converted into small amounts of heat
It increases in frequency and pitch
questions.a: It is converted into small amounts of heat
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D บอกว่าอากาศที่สั่นในช่องอากาศเล็ก ๆ ของวัสดุพรุนถูกแรงเสียดทานทำให้ช้าลง และพลังงานเสียงถูกเปลี่ยนเป็นความร้อนปริมาณเล็กน้อย
questions.evidence: Porous materials such as mineral wool and acoustic foam contain millions of tiny air pockets, and when sound waves enter them, the vibrating air is slowed by friction and the energy is converted into small amounts of heat.

questions.q: The loudness of a sound is measured in ___, while its pitch depends on its frequency.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุว่าความดังของเสียงวัดเป็นเดซิเบล (decibels) ส่วนระดับเสียงสูงต่ำขึ้นกับความถี่
questions.evidence: The loudness of a sound is measured in decibels, while its pitch depends on its frequency, measured in hertz.

questions.q: Builders often place porous materials inside wall ___ to stop sound bouncing back and forth between the panels.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D บอกว่าช่างก่อสร้างมักใส่วัสดุพรุนไว้ในโพรงผนัง (wall cavities) เพื่อไม่ให้เสียงสะท้อนไปมาระหว่างแผ่นผนัง
questions.evidence: Builders often place these materials inside wall cavities to stop sound bouncing back and forth between the panels.

questions.q: In many countries, legal standards for sound insulation in new apartments are checked with ___ before residents move in.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E ระบุว่ามาตรฐานขั้นต่ำทางกฎหมายจะถูกตรวจสอบด้วยการทดสอบหน้างานจริง (on-site tests) ก่อนผู้อยู่อาศัยย้ายเข้า
questions.evidence: Many countries now set legal minimum standards for sound insulation in new apartments, and these are checked with on-site tests before residents move in.

title: The Invention of Plastic and Its Legacy
topic: The invention of plastic and its legacy
passage:
[A] In 1869, the American inventor John Wesley Hyatt developed celluloid, the first commercially successful plastic, in response to a competition that offered a reward to anyone who could produce a substitute for ivory in billiard balls. At the time, elephant populations were declining, and manufacturers feared that natural materials would soon become scarce. Celluloid, made from plant cellulose, demonstrated that industry no longer had to depend entirely on nature. Nearly four decades later, in 1907, the Belgian-born chemist Leo Baekeland invented Bakelite, the first fully synthetic plastic, which contained no molecules found in the natural world.

[B] Bakelite was cheap to produce, resistant to heat and electricity, and could be moulded into almost any shape, so it was quickly adopted for telephones, radios, and electrical insulation. The Second World War accelerated the growth of the plastics industry enormously, as materials such as nylon and polyethylene were manufactured on a large scale for parachutes, aircraft parts, and military equipment. When the war ended, companies redirected this productive capacity towards consumer goods, and plastic soon entered almost every area of daily life, from kitchen containers to clothing.

[C] The advantages of plastic are difficult to overstate. It is lightweight, inexpensive, and extremely durable, and it can be manufactured in endless varieties. In medicine, plastic made disposable syringes and sterile packaging possible, reducing infection rates in hospitals. In transport, lighter vehicles consume less fuel. Plastic packaging also extends the life of food, which helps to limit waste.

[D] However, the very durability that makes plastic useful has become its greatest problem. Most plastics do not decompose; instead, they break apart into ever smaller fragments known as microplastics, which have been detected in oceans, soil, drinking water, and even human blood. Around eight million tonnes of plastic are estimated to enter the sea every year, harming marine animals that swallow it or become trapped in it. Although recycling is often presented as a solution, only about nine percent of all the plastic ever produced has actually been recycled.

[E] Scientists and governments are now searching for answers. Some researchers are developing bioplastics made from corn starch or algae, which decompose more quickly, while many countries have introduced bans on single-use items such as straws and carrier bags. Yet bioplastics currently cost more to produce than conventional plastics, and global production continues to rise. The invention that once freed manufacturers from the limits of nature has left a legacy that may persist in the environment for centuries.

questions.q: Celluloid was developed as a substitute for ivory in billiard balls.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุชัดเจนว่า celluloid ถูกพัฒนาขึ้นเพื่อตอบโจทย์การประกวดหาวัสดุทดแทนงาช้างในลูกบิลเลียด จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: In 1869, the American inventor John Wesley Hyatt developed celluloid, the first commercially successful plastic, in response to a competition that offered a reward to anyone who could produce a substitute for ivory in billiard balls.

questions.q: Bakelite was made from molecules that exist in nature.
questions.why_th: ข้อความขัดแย้งกับเนื้อเรื่องโดยตรง เพราะบทอ่านบอกว่า Bakelite ไม่มีโมเลกุลที่พบในธรรมชาติเลย จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: Nearly four decades later, in 1907, the Belgian-born chemist Leo Baekeland invented Bakelite, the first fully synthetic plastic, which contained no molecules found in the natural world.

questions.q: John Wesley Hyatt received the prize money offered by the competition.
questions.why_th: บทอ่านบอกเพียงว่ามีการประกวดที่เสนอรางวัล แต่ไม่ได้บอกว่า Hyatt ได้รับเงินรางวัลหรือไม่ จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: The Second World War slowed down the development of the plastics industry.
questions.why_th: บทอ่านระบุว่าสงครามโลกครั้งที่สอง 'เร่ง' การเติบโตของอุตสาหกรรมพลาสติกอย่างมาก ซึ่งตรงข้ามกับคำว่า slowed down จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: The Second World War accelerated the growth of the plastics industry enormously, as materials such as nylon and polyethylene were manufactured on a large scale for parachutes, aircraft parts, and military equipment.

questions.q: Bioplastics are currently more expensive to produce than conventional plastics.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E ระบุตรงกันว่า bioplastics มีต้นทุนการผลิตสูงกว่าพลาสติกทั่วไปในปัจจุบัน จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Yet bioplastics currently cost more to produce than conventional plastics, and global production continues to rise.

questions.q: According to paragraph A, why did manufacturers want a new material in the 1860s?
options: They feared that natural materials would soon become scarce
Elephants had already become extinct
Plant cellulose had become too expensive
Synthetic chemicals were banned at the time
questions.a: They feared that natural materials would soon become scarce
questions.why_th: บทอ่านบอกว่าประชากรช้างลดลงและผู้ผลิตกลัวว่าวัสดุธรรมชาติจะขาดแคลนในไม่ช้า ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึงหรือขัดกับเนื้อเรื่อง (ช้างแค่ลดจำนวน ยังไม่สูญพันธุ์)
questions.evidence: At the time, elephant populations were declining, and manufacturers feared that natural materials would soon become scarce.

questions.q: What happened when the Second World War ended?
options: Companies redirected their productive capacity towards consumer goods
The production of nylon and polyethylene stopped completely
Plastic was removed from kitchen containers and clothing
Governments banned plastic in military equipment
questions.a: Companies redirected their productive capacity towards consumer goods
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่าหลังสงครามจบ บริษัทต่าง ๆ หันกำลังการผลิตไปทำสินค้าอุปโภคบริโภคแทน ทำให้พลาสติกเข้าสู่ชีวิตประจำวัน ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดหรือไม่มีในบทอ่าน
questions.evidence: When the war ended, companies redirected this productive capacity towards consumer goods, and plastic soon entered almost every area of daily life, from kitchen containers to clothing.

questions.q: Which benefit of plastic in medicine does the writer mention?
options: It reduced infection rates in hospitals
It lowered the price of medicines
It shortened the time patients spent in hospital
It replaced all metal medical instruments
questions.a: It reduced infection rates in hospitals
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าพลาสติกทำให้มีเข็มฉีดยาแบบใช้แล้วทิ้งและบรรจุภัณฑ์ปลอดเชื้อ ซึ่งช่วยลดอัตราการติดเชื้อในโรงพยาบาล ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึงเลย
questions.evidence: In medicine, plastic made disposable syringes and sterile packaging possible, reducing infection rates in hospitals.

questions.q: How much of all the plastic ever produced has been recycled, according to the passage?
options: About nine percent
About eight percent
About nineteen percent
About ninety percent
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุตัวเลขชัดเจนว่ามีเพียงประมาณ 9 เปอร์เซ็นต์ของพลาสติกทั้งหมดที่เคยผลิตมาที่ถูกรีไซเคิลจริง (ระวังสับสนกับ 8 ล้านตันที่ลงทะเลต่อปี)
questions.evidence: Although recycling is often presented as a solution, only about nine percent of all the plastic ever produced has actually been recycled.

questions.q: Most plastics do not decompose but break apart into tiny fragments known as ___.
questions.why_th: คำตอบมาจากประโยคในย่อหน้า D ที่เรียกเศษพลาสติกขนาดเล็กเหล่านี้ว่า microplastics ต้องตอบคำเดียวตามบทอ่าน
questions.evidence: Most plastics do not decompose; instead, they break apart into ever smaller fragments known as microplastics, which have been detected in oceans, soil, drinking water, and even human blood.

questions.q: Bakelite was cheap to produce and resistant to heat and ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่า Bakelite ทนทั้งความร้อนและไฟฟ้า คำที่หายไปคือ electricity ตามบทอ่านทุกตัวอักษร
questions.evidence: Bakelite was cheap to produce, resistant to heat and electricity, and could be moulded into almost any shape, so it was quickly adopted for telephones, radios, and electrical insulation.

questions.q: Some researchers are developing bioplastics made from ___ or algae.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E ระบุว่านักวิจัยกำลังพัฒนา bioplastics ที่ทำจาก corn starch หรือสาหร่าย คำตอบคือสองคำนี้ตามบทอ่าน อยู่ในลิมิต 2 คำพอดี
questions.evidence: Some researchers are developing bioplastics made from corn starch or algae, which decompose more quickly, while many countries have introduced bans on single-use items such as straws and carrier bags.

title: From Folklore to Supercomputers: How Weather Forecasting Works
passage:
[A] For most of human history, predicting the weather depended on folklore, proverbs and simple observation of the sky. Farmers and sailors watched cloud shapes, wind direction and the behaviour of animals, and their sayings were sometimes accurate but often misleading. Modern weather forecasting, by contrast, is a scientific process that combines enormous quantities of data with powerful computers. Although forecasts are still not perfect, a prediction for three days ahead is now as reliable as a one-day forecast was in the 1980s.

[B] The process begins with observation. Thousands of surface weather stations around the world record temperature, air pressure, humidity, wind speed and rainfall every hour. Twice a day, weather balloons carrying instruments called radiosondes rise through the atmosphere, measuring conditions up to a height of about 30 kilometres. In addition, satellites photograph cloud systems from space, ocean buoys report the temperature of the sea surface, and commercial aircraft automatically send back data from their flight routes. All of this information is transmitted to national meteorological centres, where it is checked and combined.

[C] The next stage is known as numerical weather prediction. Supercomputers divide the atmosphere into a three-dimensional grid of millions of boxes and use the laws of physics to calculate how the air in each box will change over time. The equations describe how heat, moisture and wind interact, and the computer solves them step by step to produce a picture of the atmosphere hours or days into the future. Because so many calculations are required, forecasting centres operate some of the fastest computers in the world.

[D] However, the atmosphere is a chaotic system, which means that tiny errors in the starting data can grow rapidly into large mistakes in the forecast. To deal with this problem, forecasters use a technique called ensemble forecasting. Instead of running the model once, they run it many times, each time with slightly different starting conditions. If the results are similar, forecasters can be confident in the prediction; if they differ widely, the forecast is uncertain. Even with these methods, most scientists agree that detailed forecasts beyond about ten days are impossible.

[E] Finally, human forecasters remain essential. They compare the output of different computer models, apply their knowledge of local geography, and translate technical data into clear public warnings. When severe weather such as flooding or storms threatens, this human judgement can save lives. Weather forecasting is therefore best understood as a partnership between machines and people rather than a purely automatic process.

questions.q: A modern forecast for three days ahead is about as reliable as a one-day forecast was in the 1980s.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงๆ ว่าการพยากรณ์ล่วงหน้า 3 วันในปัจจุบันเชื่อถือได้เท่ากับการพยากรณ์ล่วงหน้า 1 วันในยุค 1980s
questions.evidence: Although forecasts are still not perfect, a prediction for three days ahead is now as reliable as a one-day forecast was in the 1980s.

questions.q: Traditional weather sayings used by farmers and sailors were never correct.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าคำพยากรณ์แบบพื้นบ้าน 'บางครั้งแม่นยำ' (sometimes accurate) จึงขัดแย้งกับคำว่า never correct
questions.evidence: Farmers and sailors watched cloud shapes, wind direction and the behaviour of animals, and their sayings were sometimes accurate but often misleading.

questions.q: Weather balloons are released twice a day and measure conditions up to about 30 kilometres.
questions.why_th: ตรงกับข้อความในย่อหน้า B ที่ว่าบอลลูนตรวจอากาศถูกปล่อยวันละสองครั้งและวัดสภาพอากาศจนถึงความสูงประมาณ 30 กิโลเมตร
questions.evidence: Twice a day, weather balloons carrying instruments called radiosondes rise through the atmosphere, measuring conditions up to a height of about 30 kilometres.

questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงทั้งดาวเทียมและทุ่นลอยในทะเลว่าเป็นแหล่งข้อมูล แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบว่าอันไหนมีประโยชน์มากกว่ากัน

questions.q: Most scientists believe that detailed forecasts for more than about ten days ahead are possible.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์ส่วนใหญ่เห็นว่าการพยากรณ์แบบละเอียดเกินประมาณ 10 วันเป็นไปไม่ได้ จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: Even with these methods, most scientists agree that detailed forecasts beyond about ten days are impossible.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to the observation data after it has been collected?
options: It is transmitted to national meteorological centres to be checked and combined.
It is published directly to the public without any checking.
It is stored on satellites until it is needed.
It is compared with weather folklore and proverbs.
questions.a: It is transmitted to national meteorological centres to be checked and combined.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่าข้อมูลทั้งหมดถูกส่งไปยังศูนย์อุตุนิยมวิทยาแห่งชาติเพื่อตรวจสอบและรวมเข้าด้วยกัน
questions.evidence: All of this information is transmitted to national meteorological centres, where it is checked and combined.

questions.q: Why do forecasting centres operate some of the fastest computers in the world?
options: Because numerical weather prediction requires an enormous number of calculations.
Because satellites produce photographs that are difficult to store.
Because radiosondes send data only twice a day.
Because human forecasters cannot read technical data.
questions.a: Because numerical weather prediction requires an enormous number of calculations.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าเพราะต้องคำนวณจำนวนมหาศาล ศูนย์พยากรณ์จึงต้องใช้คอมพิวเตอร์ที่เร็วที่สุดในโลก
questions.evidence: Because so many calculations are required, forecasting centres operate some of the fastest computers in the world.

questions.q: In ensemble forecasting, forecasters can be confident in a prediction when
options: the repeated model runs produce similar results.
the model is run only once with perfect data.
the results of the runs differ widely from each other.
the forecast covers a period longer than ten days.
questions.a: the repeated model runs produce similar results.
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าถ้าผลลัพธ์จากการรันโมเดลหลายครั้งคล้ายกัน นักพยากรณ์จะมั่นใจในคำพยากรณ์ได้ แต่ถ้าต่างกันมากแสดงว่าไม่แน่นอน
questions.evidence: If the results are similar, forecasters can be confident in the prediction; if they differ widely, the forecast is uncertain.

questions.q: What is the writer's main point in the final paragraph?
options: Weather forecasting is a partnership between machines and people.
Computers will soon replace human forecasters completely.
Severe weather warnings are usually ignored by the public.
Local geography has little influence on the weather.
questions.a: Weather forecasting is a partnership between machines and people.
questions.why_th: ประโยคสรุปของย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าการพยากรณ์อากาศควรเข้าใจว่าเป็นความร่วมมือระหว่างเครื่องจักรกับมนุษย์ ไม่ใช่กระบวนการอัตโนมัติล้วนๆ
questions.evidence: Weather forecasting is therefore best understood as a partnership between machines and people rather than a purely automatic process.

questions.q: Supercomputers divide the atmosphere into a three-dimensional ___ of millions of boxes.
questions.why_th: คำว่า grid (ตาราง/โครงข่าย) มาจากประโยคในย่อหน้า C โดยตรง ซึ่งอธิบายวิธีที่คอมพิวเตอร์แบ่งชั้นบรรยากาศ
questions.evidence: Supercomputers divide the atmosphere into a three-dimensional grid of millions of boxes and use the laws of physics to calculate how the air in each box will change over time.

questions.q: Small errors in the starting data can grow quickly because the atmosphere is a ___ system.
questions.why_th: คำว่า chaotic (ไร้ระเบียบ/โกลาหล) มาจากย่อหน้า D ซึ่งอธิบายว่าความคลาดเคลื่อนเล็กน้อยในข้อมูลตั้งต้นขยายตัวเป็นความผิดพลาดใหญ่ได้
questions.evidence: However, the atmosphere is a chaotic system, which means that tiny errors in the starting data can grow rapidly into large mistakes in the forecast.

questions.q: Ocean buoys report the temperature of the ___.
questions.why_th: คำตอบ sea surface (ผิวน้ำทะเล) มาจากย่อหน้า B ที่ระบุว่าทุ่นลอยในมหาสมุทรรายงานอุณหภูมิของผิวน้ำทะเล
questions.evidence: In addition, satellites photograph cloud systems from space, ocean buoys report the temperature of the sea surface, and commercial aircraft automatically send back data from their flight routes.

title: The Archaeology of Shipwrecks
passage:
[A] Shipwrecks have fascinated people for centuries, but only in recent decades have they become the subject of serious scientific study. Maritime archaeology, the discipline that investigates wrecks and other submerged remains, emerged as a recognised field in the middle of the twentieth century. Its growth was made possible largely by the invention of the aqualung in 1943, which allowed divers to remain underwater for extended periods and to work directly on sites that had previously been inaccessible. Before this, most objects were recovered by chance, often by fishermen or sponge divers who had no interest in recording where or how items were found.

[B] A sunken ship is valuable to researchers precisely because it represents a single moment in time. When a vessel goes down, its cargo, tools and personal possessions are sealed together on the seabed. Unlike a settlement on land, which may be occupied and altered over many generations, a wreck captures the technology and trade of one particular voyage. For this reason, archaeologists sometimes describe wrecks as "time capsules". The cold, dark conditions of the deep sea can also preserve organic materials such as wood, rope and textiles that would normally decay quickly on land.

[C] Excavating a wreck is nevertheless slow and demanding work. Divers must map the position of every object before anything is lifted, because the arrangement of the remains often reveals how the ship was built and loaded. Photographs, drawings and, increasingly, three-dimensional digital models are made of the site. Once artefacts are removed from the water, they cannot simply be left to dry. Waterlogged wood, for example, will crack and shrink as it loses moisture, so it must be treated with chemicals over a period that may last several years.

[D] Perhaps the most famous example of such conservation is the Mary Rose, an English warship that sank in 1545 and was raised from the seabed in 1982. The recovery and preservation of its hull required decades of continuous spraying with special solutions. Projects on this scale are expensive, and many governments now prefer to protect wrecks where they lie rather than bring them to the surface. This approach, known as preservation in situ, treats the seabed itself as a kind of underwater museum, leaving the remains for future generations who may possess better technology.

questions.q: Maritime archaeology became an established field of study in the 1800s.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าเกิดขึ้นกลางศตวรรษที่ 20 ไม่ใช่ศตวรรษที่ 19 จึงขัดแย้งกัน
questions.evidence: Maritime archaeology, the discipline that investigates wrecks and other submerged remains, emerged as a recognised field in the middle of the twentieth century.

questions.q: The aqualung enabled divers to stay underwater for longer periods.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการประดิษฐ์ aqualung ทำให้นักดำน้ำอยู่ใต้น้ำได้นานขึ้น
questions.evidence: Its growth was made possible largely by the invention of the aqualung in 1943, which allowed divers to remain underwater for extended periods and to work directly on sites that had previously been inaccessible.

questions.evidence: Before this, most objects were recovered by chance, often by fishermen or sponge divers who had no interest in recording where or how items were found.

questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าสภาพเย็นและมืดใต้ทะเลลึกช่วยรักษาวัสดุอินทรีย์อย่างไม้ เชือก และผ้า
questions.evidence: The cold, dark conditions of the deep sea can also preserve organic materials such as wood, rope and textiles that would normally decay quickly on land.

questions.a: Because they capture the technology and trade of a single voyage
options: Because they are occupied and altered over many generations
Because they capture the technology and trade of a single voyage

questions.q: The English warship known as the ___ sank in 1545 and was raised in 1982.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุชื่อเรือรบอังกฤษว่า Mary Rose ที่จมปี 1545 และถูกกู้ขึ้นมาปี 1982
questions.evidence: Perhaps the most famous example of such conservation is the Mary Rose, an English warship that sank in 1545 and was raised from the seabed in 1982.

questions.q: The approach of protecting wrecks where they lie is known as preservation ___.
questions.why_th: บทความเรียกวิธีอนุรักษ์ซากเรือในที่เดิมว่า preservation in situ
questions.evidence: This approach, known as preservation in situ, treats the seabed itself as a kind of underwater museum, leaving the remains for future generations who may possess better technology.

title: A Frozen History: The Story of Ice Cream
passage:
[A] The desire to enjoy cold, sweet treats is far older than the modern dessert we recognise today. Long before refrigeration existed, people in ancient China are believed to have mixed snow with fruit and honey to create a refreshing dish. Written records suggest that similar preparations were served to emperors, who valued them as a luxury. In warmer regions of the ancient world, ice was often transported from mountains and stored in deep pits, where it could be kept frozen for months. These early recipes, however, were closer to flavoured ices than to the smooth, creamy product familiar to us now.

[B] The transformation of ice into a true dessert depended on an important scientific discovery. During the sixteenth century, cooks learned that adding salt to ice lowered its temperature considerably. This technique allowed liquids such as milk and cream to be frozen quickly and evenly, producing a far softer texture. The knowledge spread across Europe, and by the seventeenth century frozen desserts had become fashionable in the royal courts of Italy and France. At this stage, ice cream remained an expensive rarity, prepared by skilled cooks and enjoyed almost exclusively by the wealthy.

[C] Ice cream reached North America with European settlers, and it quickly gained popularity among those who could afford it. Several early American political leaders were known to be enthusiastic admirers of the dessert, and detailed recipes survive from that period. Yet the treat remained a privilege of the rich, largely because harvesting and storing natural ice was difficult and costly. Producing ice cream by hand was also slow and required considerable effort, which kept it beyond the reach of ordinary families.

[D] The nineteenth century brought changes that made ice cream widely available. The invention of the hand-cranked freezer greatly simplified the process, while a growing trade in ice made the raw material cheaper. Later, the development of industrial refrigeration and mechanical production allowed ice cream to be manufactured on a large scale. New inventions such as the edible cone and, eventually, refrigerated transport helped the dessert reach markets far from where it was made. By the twentieth century, ice cream had been transformed from a symbol of luxury into an everyday pleasure enjoyed by millions around the world.
