title: When Crowds Move: The Science of Safety in Public Spaces
passage:
[A] Every year, millions of people gather in stadiums, railway stations and festival grounds, and for most of them the experience passes without incident. Yet under certain conditions a moving crowd can become extremely dangerous. Researchers who study crowd behaviour argue that the greatest risks arise not from panic, as is often assumed, but from density. When too many bodies are pressed into a limited space, individuals lose control over their own movement and are carried along by the pressure of those around them. At this point the crowd begins to behave more like a fluid than a collection of independent people.

[B] Density is usually measured as the number of people per square metre. At four people per square metre, movement remains fairly comfortable, but at six or more the situation changes sharply. Shockwaves can pass through the crowd, and pressure may build to a level that makes breathing difficult. Investigations into major disasters have repeatedly shown that deaths were caused by compression rather than by trampling, a distinction that has important consequences for how public spaces are designed. Understanding this has shifted attention away from controlling the behaviour of individuals and towards controlling the conditions in which crowds form.

[C] Modern safety design therefore concentrates on managing flow before dangerous densities can develop. Wide corridors, clearly marked exits and the removal of sudden bottlenecks all help people to keep moving. Engineers now use computer models that simulate how thousands of individuals will move through a given space, allowing designers to identify problem areas long before a building is completed. Such simulations can reveal, for example, that a staircase which appears generous on paper will nonetheless cause a backlog once a certain number of people attempt to use it at once.

[D] Timing and information also matter. Staggering the arrival and departure of visitors reduces the peak load on any single route, while clear signage and announcements help people choose sensible paths. During an emergency, the way an evacuation is communicated can be as important as the physical layout of the building. People tend to leave by the route through which they entered, even when a nearer exit is available, so designers must anticipate this tendency rather than assume that crowds will act with perfect logic.

[E] Ultimately, crowd safety depends on cooperation between architects, event organisers and the authorities. No single measure guarantees safety, but a combination of good design, careful planning and effective communication greatly reduces the likelihood of disaster. As cities grow and public events attract ever larger audiences, the science of crowd behaviour is likely to become an increasingly essential part of the way our shared spaces are built.

questions.evidence: Every year, millions of people gather in stadiums, railway stations and festival grounds, and for most of them the experience passes without incident.

title: Guardians of Clay: Uncovering and Conserving the Terracotta Army
topic: The rediscovery and preservation of the Terracotta Army
passage:
[A] In March 1974, a group of farmers digging a well near the city of Xi'an in Shaanxi Province, China, struck fragments of hardened clay and pieces of bronze. What they had accidentally uncovered was one of the most remarkable archaeological finds of the twentieth century: the Terracotta Army, a vast collection of life-sized ceramic soldiers buried to guard the tomb of Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China. The emperor, who died in 210 BC, had ordered the construction of this underground force more than two thousand years earlier. Subsequent excavation revealed thousands of figures arranged in military formation across several large pits.

[B] The scale of the project astonished researchers. It is estimated that around 8,000 soldiers, together with horses and wooden chariots, were produced by labourers working under imperial command. Each warrior is strikingly individual: no two faces are identical, and the figures differ in height, uniform and hairstyle according to their military rank. Archaeologists believe the heads, arms and torsos were manufactured separately in workshops and then assembled, a method that allowed for both mass production and variety. Originally, the entire army was painted in bright colours, though most of this pigment has since faded or flaked away.

[C] Preserving the figures has proved as challenging as excavating them. When the coloured lacquer beneath the paint is exposed to the dry air of Xi'an, it can curl and peel within minutes, causing the surface colour to be lost forever. To address this problem, conservation scientists developed chemical treatments that stabilise the lacquer as soon as a figure is unearthed. In addition, some archaeologists now argue that sections of the site should remain buried until better techniques become available, a cautious approach that prioritises long-term protection over immediate discovery.

[D] Today the Terracotta Army attracts millions of visitors annually and has been recognised by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site. Yet a great deal remains unexplored. The central tomb of Qin Shi Huang himself has never been opened, partly out of respect and partly because ancient records warn of rivers of poisonous mercury surrounding the burial chamber. For now, the clay soldiers continue their silent watch, offering historians an extraordinary window into the ambitions and craftsmanship of early imperial China.

questions.evidence: a group of farmers digging a well near the city of Xi'an in Shaanxi Province, China, struck fragments of hardened clay and pieces of bronze.

title: The Fading Colours of the Reef
topic: Why coral reefs are bleaching and how scientists respond
passage:
[A] Coral reefs are among the most biologically diverse ecosystems on the planet, supporting roughly a quarter of all marine species despite covering less than one per cent of the ocean floor. The vivid colours for which reefs are famous do not come from the corals themselves but from tiny algae called zooxanthellae that live inside the coral tissue. These algae photosynthesise and supply the coral with the majority of its energy, while the coral offers the algae shelter and access to sunlight. This close partnership, known as symbiosis, is the foundation on which entire reef communities depend.

[B] Coral bleaching occurs when this partnership breaks down. When seawater becomes too warm, even by only one or two degrees Celsius above the usual summer maximum, the algae begin to produce harmful compounds. In response, the coral expels the algae from its tissue, revealing the white limestone skeleton beneath and giving the phenomenon its name. A bleached coral is not immediately dead, but it is severely weakened and deprived of its main food source. If cooler conditions return quickly, the algae may recolonise and the coral can recover; if the heat persists for weeks, the coral usually starves and dies. Although rising sea temperatures are the principal trigger, pollution, sedimentation and unusually low tides can also contribute to bleaching events.

[C] The scale of the problem has grown markedly in recent decades. The Great Barrier Reef, off the coast of Australia, experienced severe mass bleaching in 2016 and again in 2017, with some surveys reporting that half of its shallow-water corals were lost. Because such events are closely linked to marine heatwaves, scientists regard them as one of the clearest biological signals of a warming climate.

[D] Faced with these losses, researchers have responded on several fronts. Some teams are breeding corals that show a natural tolerance to heat, then transplanting their offspring onto damaged reefs in the hope of building more resilient populations. Others are cultivating young corals in protected nurseries before returning them to the sea. A more experimental approach involves adjusting the microscopic organisms that live alongside corals to improve their resistance to stress. Alongside these interventions, extensive monitoring networks now use satellites and underwater sensors to predict where bleaching is likely to strike, allowing managers to concentrate their limited resources. Most scientists stress, however, that such measures can only buy time; the long-term survival of reefs ultimately depends on reducing the greenhouse gas emissions that are warming the oceans.

questions.q: The bright colours of coral reefs are produced by the coral animals themselves.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุชัดว่าสีสันไม่ได้มาจากตัวปะการังเอง แต่มาจากสาหร่าย zooxanthellae ประโยคคำถามจึงขัดแย้งกับเนื้อเรื่องโดยตรง = False
questions.evidence: The vivid colours for which reefs are famous do not come from the corals themselves but from tiny algae called zooxanthellae that live inside the coral tissue.

questions.q: The algae provide the coral with most of its energy.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่าสาหร่ายสังเคราะห์แสงและให้พลังงานส่วนใหญ่แก่ปะการัง ซึ่งตรงกับประโยคคำถาม = True
questions.evidence: These algae photosynthesise and supply the coral with the majority of its energy, while the coral offers the algae shelter and access to sunlight.

questions.q: A coral dies instantly at the moment it becomes bleached.

False
Not Given

questions.q: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest coral reef system in the world.

False
Not Given

questions.q: Scientists believe that reef restoration techniques alone can guarantee the long-term survival of reefs.

False
Not Given

questions.evidence: Most scientists stress, however, that such measures can only buy time; the long-term survival of reefs ultimately depends on reducing the greenhouse gas emissions that are warming the oceans.

questions.a: The algae begin producing harmful compounds when the water is too warm

questions.q: Which of the following is described as a more experimental scientific response?
questions.a: Adjusting the microscopic organisms that live alongside corals to improve stress resistance

Breeding heat-tolerant corals for transplanting
Growing young corals in protected nurseries

questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องใช้คำว่า 'A more experimental approach' กับวิธีการปรับสิ่งมีชีวิตขนาดเล็กที่อาศัยอยู่รอบปะการัง ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นวิธีที่กล่าวถึงแต่ไม่ได้ระบุว่าเป็นวิธีเชิงทดลองมากกว่า

questions.q: The relationship between corals and the algae that live in their tissue is known as ___.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องเรียกความสัมพันธ์ใกล้ชิดนี้ว่า symbiosis โดยตรง คำที่ต้องเติมในช่องว่างจึงเป็น symbiosis

questions.q: When the coral expels its algae, it reveals the white ___ skeleton underneath.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่าเมื่อขับสาหร่ายออก จะเผยให้เห็นโครงกระดูกหินปูนสีขาว (white limestone skeleton) คำที่หายไปคือ limestone

questions.q: Monitoring networks use satellites and underwater ___ to predict where bleaching is likely to strike.

questions.evidence: extensive monitoring networks now use satellites and underwater sensors to predict where bleaching is likely to strike, allowing managers to concentrate their limited resources.

title: Teaching the Body to Remember: How Vaccines Train the Immune System
topic: How vaccines train the immune system
passage:
[A] The human immune system is a remarkable network of cells and molecules that defends the body against harmful microorganisms known as pathogens. When a virus or bacterium enters the body for the first time, the immune system must identify it as foreign before mounting a response. This initial reaction is often slow, which is why people frequently fall ill during a first encounter with a new pathogen. Vaccines were developed to overcome precisely this delay, allowing the body to prepare its defences in advance rather than during an active infection.

[B] A vaccine works by presenting the immune system with a harmless version or fragment of a pathogen. Traditional vaccines may contain weakened or inactivated microbes, while more recent technologies use only a single protein or a set of genetic instructions that the body can read to produce that protein temporarily. In every case, the material is unable to cause the disease itself. Instead, it acts as a kind of rehearsal, prompting the immune system to recognise the distinctive molecular shapes, called antigens, that mark the surface of the real pathogen.

[C] Once these antigens are detected, specialised white blood cells begin to respond. Certain cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that attach to the antigen and either neutralise the invader or flag it for destruction. Other cells coordinate the wider response or directly destroy infected cells. This coordinated activity takes several days to reach full strength, and during this period the vaccinated person may experience mild symptoms such as a sore arm or slight fever. These effects are a normal sign that the immune system is learning, not evidence that the vaccine has caused the disease.

[D] The most important outcome of vaccination is the creation of memory cells. After the threat has been dealt with, most of the responding cells die away, but a small population of long-lived memory cells remains in the body. Should the genuine pathogen appear later, these cells recognise it immediately and trigger a rapid, powerful response, often defeating the infection before any symptoms develop. This is why some vaccines provide protection for many years. For certain diseases, however, immunity fades over time or the pathogen changes its antigens, so additional doses, known as boosters, are required to maintain protection.

[E] Beyond protecting individuals, widespread vaccination can safeguard entire communities. When a sufficiently large proportion of a population becomes immune, the pathogen struggles to spread from person to person, indirectly shielding those who cannot be vaccinated, such as newborn infants or people with weakened immune systems. This effect, called herd immunity, has helped to control and in some cases eliminate diseases that were once widespread and deadly.

questions.q: The immune system usually responds quickly the first time it meets a new pathogen.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าปฏิกิริยาครั้งแรก 'มักช้า' (often slow) ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'quickly' โดยตรง คำตอบจึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: This initial reaction is often slow, which is why people frequently fall ill during a first encounter with a new pathogen.

questions.q: Vaccines are able to give a person the disease they are designed to prevent.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: บทความระบุชัดเจนว่าสารในวัคซีน 'ไม่สามารถก่อโรคได้ด้วยตัวเอง' (unable to cause the disease itself) จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ คำตอบเป็น False
questions.evidence: In every case, the material is unable to cause the disease itself.

questions.q: Mild symptoms after vaccination indicate that the immune system is responding.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าอาการเล็กน้อยเป็น 'สัญญาณปกติว่าระบบภูมิคุ้มกันกำลังเรียนรู้' ซึ่งตรงกับใจความของประโยคที่ถาม คำตอบจึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: These effects are a normal sign that the immune system is learning, not evidence that the vaccine has caused the disease.

questions.q: mRNA vaccines are more expensive to produce than traditional vaccines.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงเทคโนโลยีที่ใช้คำสั่งพันธุกรรมให้ร่างกายสร้างโปรตีน แต่ไม่ได้เอ่ยถึงต้นทุนหรือราคาในการผลิตเลย จึงไม่มีข้อมูลให้ตัดสิน คำตอบเป็น Not Given

questions.q: Herd immunity can protect people who are unable to receive vaccines themselves.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [E] ระบุว่าภูมิคุ้มกันหมู่ช่วยปกป้องคนที่ฉีดวัคซีนไม่ได้ทางอ้อม เช่น ทารกแรกเกิดหรือผู้มีภูมิคุ้มกันอ่อนแอ คำตอบจึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: the pathogen struggles to spread from person to person, indirectly shielding those who cannot be vaccinated, such as newborn infants or people with weakened immune systems.

questions.q: According to the passage, why were vaccines originally developed?
questions.a: To let the body prepare its defences before an infection occurs
options: To let the body prepare its defences before an infection occurs
To completely replace the natural immune system
To cure people who are already seriously ill
To make first infections less painful
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุว่าวัคซีนถูกพัฒนาขึ้นเพื่อเอาชนะความล่าช้าของภูมิคุ้มกัน โดยให้ร่างกายเตรียมการป้องกันล่วงหน้า จึงตรงกับตัวเลือกแรก ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในบทความ
questions.evidence: Vaccines were developed to overcome precisely this delay, allowing the body to prepare its defences in advance rather than during an active infection.

questions.q: What are antigens described as in the passage?
questions.a: Distinctive molecular shapes on the surface of a pathogen
options: Distinctive molecular shapes on the surface of a pathogen
Proteins produced by white blood cells
Weakened microbes used in traditional vaccines
Long-lived cells that remain after infection
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] นิยาม antigens ว่าเป็นรูปทรงโมเลกุลเฉพาะที่อยู่บนผิวของเชื้อโรค จึงตรงกับตัวเลือกแรก ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นสับสนกับ antibodies เชื้อที่อ่อนแอ หรือ memory cells
questions.evidence: the distinctive molecular shapes, called antigens, that mark the surface of the real pathogen.

questions.q: What is the main function of antibodies according to the passage?
questions.a: To attach to the antigen and neutralise or flag the invader
options: To attach to the antigen and neutralise or flag the invader
To produce genetic instructions for the body
To create memory cells directly
To coordinate the entire immune response
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] อธิบายว่า antibodies เกาะติดกับ antigen แล้วทำให้เป็นกลางหรือทำเครื่องหมายเพื่อให้ถูกทำลาย จึงตรงกับตัวเลือกแรก
questions.evidence: Certain cells produce antibodies, which are proteins that attach to the antigen and either neutralise the invader or flag it for destruction.

questions.q: Why are booster doses needed for some diseases?
questions.a: Because immunity fades over time or the pathogen changes its antigens
options: Because immunity fades over time or the pathogen changes its antigens
Because memory cells never form after the first dose
Because the first vaccine always fails to work
Because antibodies are too strong and must be reduced
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุเหตุผลของการฉีดกระตุ้น (boosters) ว่าเป็นเพราะภูมิคุ้มกันลดลงตามเวลา หรือเชื้อโรคเปลี่ยน antigens จึงตรงกับตัวเลือกแรก
questions.evidence: immunity fades over time or the pathogen changes its antigens, so additional doses, known as boosters, are required to maintain protection.

questions.q: A vaccine acts as a kind of ___, prompting the immune system to recognise antigens.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ใช้คำว่า 'a kind of rehearsal' เปรียบการทำงานของวัคซีนเป็นการซ้อม จึงเติมคำว่า rehearsal (1 คำ)
questions.evidence: Instead, it acts as a kind of rehearsal, prompting the immune system to recognise the distinctive molecular shapes

questions.q: The most important outcome of vaccination is the creation of ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุชัดเจนว่าผลลัพธ์สำคัญที่สุดคือการสร้าง memory cells จึงเติมคำว่า memory cells (2 คำ ไม่เกินขีดจำกัด)
questions.evidence: The most important outcome of vaccination is the creation of memory cells.

questions.q: When enough of a population is immune, the resulting protection is called ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [E] เรียกผลของการที่ประชากรส่วนใหญ่มีภูมิคุ้มกันว่า herd immunity จึงเติมคำว่า herd immunity (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: This effect, called herd immunity, has helped to control and in some cases eliminate diseases that were once widespread and deadly.

title: Bringing Languages Back to Life
passage:
[A] Of the roughly 7,000 languages spoken across the world today, linguists estimate that nearly half are at risk of disappearing before the end of this century. Many of these are Indigenous languages, passed down orally through generations and spoken by relatively small communities. When the last fluent speaker of such a language dies, a unique way of describing the world, including detailed knowledge of local plants, weather patterns and ancestral history, is often lost with them. In response, communities and researchers around the globe have begun ambitious efforts to reverse this decline, a process commonly known as language revitalisation.

[B] One of the most celebrated examples is the revival of Hebrew. For centuries, Hebrew survived mainly as a written language used in religious contexts rather than in everyday conversation. Beginning in the late nineteenth century, dedicated activists worked to transform it into a spoken tongue once more, inventing new vocabulary for modern objects and encouraging families to raise their children using it. Today, Hebrew is the daily language of millions, and it is frequently cited as proof that a dormant language can be brought back into ordinary use. However, experts caution that its unusual circumstances make it difficult to copy elsewhere.

[C] More recently, communities have turned to a range of practical methods. A particularly effective approach is the "language nest", first developed by Maori speakers in New Zealand during the 1980s. In these preschools, elderly fluent speakers care for very young children and speak to them only in the Indigenous language, so that the children absorb it naturally, much as they would a first language. Similar programmes have since been adopted by Indigenous groups in Hawaii and elsewhere. Digital tools, including recording apps and online dictionaries, now help document endangered languages and make lessons available to learners who live far from their traditional homelands.

[D] Despite these encouraging developments, revitalisation remains a demanding undertaking. Success usually depends on sustained funding, supportive government policies and, above all, a genuine desire within the community to keep the language alive. Some critics argue that resources might be better spent on other social needs, yet supporters respond that language is inseparable from identity and cultural survival. What is increasingly clear is that reviving a language is not simply a matter of teaching grammar and words; it requires rebuilding the everyday situations in which people naturally choose to speak it.

questions.q: Linguists believe that about 3,500 of the world's languages could vanish within this century.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่ามีภาษาราว 7,000 ภาษา และเกือบครึ่งเสี่ยงจะสูญหายก่อนสิ้นศตวรรษนี้ ครึ่งหนึ่งของ 7,000 คือประมาณ 3,500 ซึ่งตรงกับข้อความ จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Of the roughly 7,000 languages spoken across the world today, linguists estimate that nearly half are at risk of disappearing before the end of this century.

questions.q: Hebrew was widely used in daily conversation throughout the centuries before its revival.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าก่อนการฟื้นฟู ภาษาฮีบรูอยู่รอดในฐานะภาษาเขียนที่ใช้ในบริบทศาสนา ไม่ใช่ในการสนทนาประจำวัน จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์โดยตรง เป็น False
questions.evidence: For centuries, Hebrew survived mainly as a written language used in religious contexts rather than in everyday conversation.

questions.q: The language nest method was created by Maori speakers in New Zealand.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกชัดเจนว่า language nest ถูกพัฒนาขึ้นครั้งแรกโดยผู้พูดภาษาเมารีในนิวซีแลนด์ในช่วงทศวรรษ 1980 จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: A particularly effective approach is the "language nest", first developed by Maori speakers in New Zealand during the 1980s.

questions.q: Hawaii's language nest programme has produced more fluent speakers than New Zealand's.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องกล่าวเพียงว่ามีโปรแกรมคล้ายกันถูกนำไปใช้ในฮาวาย แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบจำนวนผู้พูดคล่องระหว่างฮาวายกับนิวซีแลนด์ จึงไม่มีข้อมูลให้ตัดสิน เป็น Not Given

questions.q: Experts say that the way Hebrew was revived can easily be repeated with other languages.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าผู้เชี่ยวชาญเตือนว่าสถานการณ์ที่ผิดปกติของฮีบรูทำให้ยากที่จะลอกเลียนแบบที่อื่น ซึ่งขัดกับคำว่า 'easily repeated' โดยตรง จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: However, experts caution that its unusual circumstances make it difficult to copy elsewhere.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is often lost when the last fluent speaker of an Indigenous language dies?
questions.a: detailed knowledge of local plants, weather and ancestral history
options: detailed knowledge of local plants, weather and ancestral history
a large collection of written religious texts
modern vocabulary invented by language activists
government funding for cultural programmes
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุว่าเมื่อผู้พูดคนสุดท้ายเสียชีวิต ความรู้เรื่องพืชท้องถิ่น รูปแบบอากาศ และประวัติบรรพบุรุษมักสูญหายไปด้วย ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: a unique way of describing the world, including detailed knowledge of local plants, weather patterns and ancestral history, is often lost with them.

questions.q: How do children in a language nest learn the Indigenous language?
questions.a: by absorbing it naturally as they would a first language
options: by absorbing it naturally as they would a first language
by memorising grammar rules from textbooks
by using recording apps on their own
by attending religious services regularly
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C อธิบายว่าเด็กเล็กซึมซับภาษาอย่างเป็นธรรมชาติเหมือนกับภาษาแม่ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึงในบริบทของ language nest
questions.evidence: the children absorb it naturally, much as they would a first language.

questions.q: What does the passage say about digital tools such as apps and online dictionaries?
questions.a: They help document languages and reach distant learners
options: They help document languages and reach distant learners
They have replaced elderly fluent speakers
They are only useful for written languages
They guarantee the survival of a language
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าเครื่องมือดิจิทัลช่วยบันทึกภาษาที่ใกล้สูญพันธุ์และทำให้บทเรียนเข้าถึงผู้เรียนที่อยู่ไกลจากถิ่นดั้งเดิม ตัวเลือกอื่นกล่าวเกินจริงหรือไม่ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Digital tools, including recording apps and online dictionaries, now help document endangered languages and make lessons available to learners who live far from their traditional homelands.

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุว่าผู้สนับสนุนโต้แย้งว่าภาษาแยกไม่ออกจากอัตลักษณ์และการอยู่รอดทางวัฒนธรรม ส่วนตัวเลือกที่สองเป็นความเห็นของฝ่ายวิจารณ์ ไม่ใช่ฝ่ายสนับสนุน

questions.q: The process of reversing the decline of endangered languages is commonly known as language ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ปิดท้ายด้วยคำว่า language revitalisation ซึ่งเป็นชื่อเรียกกระบวนการนี้ ตอบคำเดียวคือ revitalisation
questions.evidence: a process commonly known as language revitalisation.

questions.q: In a language nest, ___ fluent speakers look after very young children and speak only the Indigenous language to them.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าผู้พูดคล่องที่สูงอายุ (elderly) เป็นผู้ดูแลเด็กเล็ก คำที่เติมในช่องว่างคือ elderly

questions.q: Successful revitalisation usually depends on sustained ___, supportive government policies and a genuine desire within the community.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุปัจจัยความสำเร็จ ได้แก่ sustained funding, supportive government policies และความต้องการที่แท้จริงในชุมชน คำที่ตอบคือ funding
questions.evidence: Success usually depends on sustained funding, supportive government policies and, above all, a genuine desire within the community to keep the language alive.

title: Growing Up: The Rise of Vertical Farming
topic: Vertical farming and the future of food
passage:
[A] As the global population climbs towards an estimated ten billion people by the middle of this century, agricultural experts face a difficult question: how can enough food be produced without clearing yet more forests or exhausting the world's soils? One increasingly discussed answer is vertical farming, a method in which crops are grown in stacked layers inside carefully controlled indoor environments. Rather than spreading outwards across large fields, these farms grow upwards, often occupying warehouses, disused factories or purpose-built towers located within or close to cities.

[B] The technology behind vertical farming relies heavily on precise control of the growing environment. Instead of sunlight, plants receive light from energy-efficient LED lamps, which can be tuned to particular wavelengths that encourage rapid growth. Temperature, humidity and the concentration of carbon dioxide are all regulated by computer systems. Many vertical farms use hydroponics, a technique in which roots are bathed in a nutrient solution rather than planted in soil. Because conditions never vary with the weather or the seasons, crops such as lettuce and herbs can be harvested throughout the year.

[C] Supporters of the approach point to a number of clear advantages. Vertical farms use up to ninety-five per cent less water than conventional agriculture, since the water that plants do not absorb is captured and reused. Growing food inside sealed buildings also removes the need for chemical pesticides, because insects and weeds are kept out. Furthermore, locating farms near consumers shortens the distance that produce must travel, reducing both transport costs and the spoilage that occurs during long journeys.

[D] Nevertheless, vertical farming faces serious obstacles. The most significant is the enormous amount of electricity required to power the artificial lighting and climate-control equipment. Where this electricity comes from fossil fuels, the environmental benefits are considerably weakened. Building and running such facilities is also expensive, and for this reason vertical farms currently concentrate on high-value leafy greens rather than staple crops like wheat or rice, which remain far cheaper to grow in open fields.

[E] Most researchers therefore see vertical farming not as a replacement for traditional agriculture but as a valuable complement to it. In densely populated regions with limited farmland, or in harsh climates where outdoor cultivation is impractical, growing crops indoors may prove especially useful. As LED technology improves and renewable energy becomes cheaper, the economic case for farming upwards is likely to strengthen in the decades ahead.

questions.q: Vertical farms are always built in newly constructed tower buildings.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าฟาร์มแนวตั้งมักตั้งอยู่ในโกดัง โรงงานที่เลิกใช้ หรือหอที่สร้างขึ้นมาเฉพาะ จึงไม่ได้เป็นตึกสร้างใหม่เสมอไป คำว่า always ขัดกับเนื้อหาโดยตรง จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: these farms grow upwards, often occupying warehouses, disused factories or purpose-built towers located within or close to cities.

questions.q: In vertical farms, plants are given light from LED lamps instead of sunlight.

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าการตั้งฟาร์มใกล้ผู้บริโภคช่วยลดทั้งต้นทุนการขนส่งและการเน่าเสีย (spoilage) ที่เกิดระหว่างการเดินทางไกล คำตอบคือ spoilage (หนึ่งคำ)

questions.evidence: Where this electricity comes from fossil fuels, the environmental benefits are considerably weakened.

title: Reaching for Heaven: The Rise and Function of Gothic Cathedrals
topic: The rise and function of Gothic cathedral architecture
passage:
[A] The Gothic style of architecture emerged in northern France during the middle of the twelfth century and dominated European church building for the next four hundred years. It grew out of an earlier tradition known as Romanesque, which relied on thick walls, small windows and rounded arches to support the enormous weight of stone roofs. Because these buildings needed such solid walls, their interiors were typically dark and heavy in appearance. Gothic builders set out to solve this problem, seeking structures that were taller, lighter and filled with light.

[B] The transformation depended on three key innovations. The first was the pointed arch, which directed the weight of the roof downwards more efficiently than the rounded arch and allowed greater height. The second was the ribbed vault, a framework of stone ribs that carried the load of the ceiling along fixed lines. The third, and perhaps most striking, was the flying buttress: an external support that transferred the outward thrust of the walls to sturdy piers standing away from the building. Together these features meant that walls no longer had to bear the full weight of the roof, so they could be opened up with vast windows.

[C] These windows were commonly filled with stained glass, which flooded the interior with coloured light. For a largely illiterate population, the images depicted in the glass served an important purpose. They told stories from the Bible and the lives of saints, functioning as a kind of visual instruction for worshippers who could not read. In this way, the cathedral was not merely a place of prayer but also a means of teaching religious ideas to ordinary people.

[D] A Gothic cathedral was, however, far more than a religious building. Its construction often took more than a century to complete and required the combined labour of skilled masons, carpenters and glassmakers. The finished structure became a source of civic pride, and towns competed with one another to build ever taller and more magnificent cathedrals. The great height of these buildings was also symbolic, expressing a desire to draw the eyes and thoughts of worshippers upwards towards heaven. Economically, a famous cathedral could attract pilgrims from distant regions, bringing trade and income to the surrounding town.

[E] Although the popularity of the Gothic style eventually faded as new fashions arose during the Renaissance, its influence proved remarkably enduring. In the nineteenth century, architects revived Gothic forms for churches, universities and public buildings across Europe and beyond. Today the surviving medieval cathedrals remain among the most visited monuments in the world, admired as much for their engineering as for their beauty.

questions.q: The Gothic style remained the leading form of European church architecture for around four centuries.
options: True

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าสถาปัตยกรรมโกธิคเกิดขึ้นกลางศตวรรษที่ 12 และครองการสร้างโบสถ์ในยุโรปต่อไปอีกสี่ร้อยปี ซึ่งตรงกับ 'ราวสี่ศตวรรษ' ในคำกล่าว จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: The Gothic style of architecture emerged in northern France during the middle of the twelfth century and dominated European church building for the next four hundred years.

options: True

title: Tiny Fragments, Large Consequences: Microplastics in the Ocean Food Chain
topic: Microplastics in the ocean food chain
passage:
[A] Microplastics are plastic particles smaller than five millimetres in diameter. Some are manufactured at this size, such as the microbeads once added to cosmetics, but the majority form when larger items of plastic waste break apart in the environment. Sunlight, wave action and the physical grinding of sand gradually fragment discarded bottles, packaging and fishing gear into ever smaller pieces. Because these fragments are so light, ocean currents carry them across enormous distances, and researchers have now recorded their presence in every marine region examined, from tropical coastlines to the deep waters of the Arctic.

[B] The reason microplastics matter for marine life lies in their size. To many small organisms, the particles are almost indistinguishable from natural food. Zooplankton, the tiny drifting animals that form the base of the ocean food chain, readily swallow microplastics alongside the algae they normally consume. Once inside an organism, the particles are difficult to expel and may accumulate in the gut. This is significant because zooplankton are eaten by small fish, which are in turn eaten by larger predators. At each stage, plastic can pass from prey to predator, a process scientists refer to as trophic transfer.

[C] Laboratory studies have begun to reveal the biological effects of this ingestion. Fish that consume microplastics often eat less of their natural diet, since the indigestible particles create a false sense of fullness. As a result, some individuals grow more slowly and have reduced energy reserves. There is also concern about the chemicals involved. Plastics frequently contain additives, and their surfaces can attract pollutants already present in seawater. When an animal ingests a particle, these substances may be released into its tissues, although the extent of the harm to wild populations remains uncertain.

[D] Microplastics have also been detected in seafood destined for human consumption, including mussels, oysters and several commercial fish species. This has prompted questions about possible risks to people, yet firm conclusions are still lacking. Much of the plastic in fish is found in the digestive tract, which is usually removed before the fish is eaten, whereas shellfish are typically consumed whole. Scientists caution that far more research is required before the consequences for human health can be properly assessed.

[E] Addressing the problem is challenging because, once microplastics enter the ocean, removing them is virtually impossible. Their small size and wide dispersal place them beyond the reach of any practical cleanup technology. For this reason, most experts argue that the only realistic solution is prevention: reducing the production of unnecessary plastic, improving waste management on land, and stopping litter from reaching rivers and seas in the first place.

title: Two Languages, One Mind: The Cognitive Advantages of Bilingualism
passage:
[A] For much of the twentieth century, growing up with two languages was widely regarded as a disadvantage. Educators feared that a child's limited mental resources would be divided between the two systems, leaving neither fully developed. Early studies appeared to support this view, reporting that bilingual children scored lower on verbal tests than their monolingual peers. However, later researchers pointed out that these early experiments were seriously flawed: they often tested immigrant children in their weaker language and failed to account for differences in social and economic background. Once such factors were controlled, the supposed deficit largely disappeared.

[B] Modern research has, in fact, reversed the earlier conclusion. A central idea is that bilingual speakers must constantly manage two languages that are both active in the mind, even when only one is being used. To speak correctly, the brain has to suppress the language that is not required. Psychologists believe that this continual practice strengthens what is known as the executive control system, a network in the brain responsible for switching attention, ignoring distractions and holding information in mind. Because bilinguals exercise these skills every time they speak, the benefits may extend to tasks that have nothing to do with language at all.

[C] Evidence for this claim comes from a range of experimental tasks. In one well-known test, participants must identify the colour of the ink in which a word is printed while ignoring the word itself. Bilingual participants, on average, respond slightly faster and make fewer errors, suggesting more efficient attention control. Some studies also report that bilingual children are better at tasks requiring them to sort objects by changing rules, a skill linked to mental flexibility.

[D] Perhaps the most striking claims concern later life. Several studies have found that bilingual patients develop the symptoms of dementia around four years later than monolingual patients with similar levels of brain damage. Researchers suggest that a lifetime of managing two languages builds a form of "cognitive reserve" that helps the brain compensate for damage. It is important to note, however, that bilingualism does not prevent the underlying disease; it appears only to delay the point at which symptoms become obvious.

[E] Not all scientists are convinced. Some argue that positive results are more likely to be published than studies showing no effect, which may exaggerate the size of the advantage. Others note that bilinguals differ from monolinguals in many ways besides language, making it hard to isolate a single cause. Despite these debates, most researchers now agree that speaking more than one language shapes the mind in measurable ways.

questions.q: Early twentieth-century studies concluded that bilingual children performed worse than monolingual children on verbal tests.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุชัดเจนว่าการศึกษายุคแรกรายงานว่าเด็กสองภาษาทำคะแนนได้ต่ำกว่าเด็กภาษาเดียวในแบบทดสอบด้านภาษา ตรงกับข้อความจึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Early studies appeared to support this view, reporting that bilingual children scored lower on verbal tests than their monolingual peers.

questions.q: The early experiments on bilingual children were later shown to have methodological weaknesses.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุว่าการทดลองยุคแรกมีข้อบกพร่องร้ายแรง (seriously flawed) เช่น ทดสอบในภาษาที่เด็กอ่อนกว่า และไม่ควบคุมปัจจัยทางสังคม-เศรษฐกิจ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: However, later researchers pointed out that these early experiments were seriously flawed: they often tested immigrant children in their weaker language and failed to account for differences in social and economic background.

questions.q: When a bilingual person speaks one language, the other language is completely switched off in the brain.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่าทั้งสองภาษายังทำงานอยู่ในสมอง (both active) และสมองต้องคอยยับยั้งภาษาที่ไม่ได้ใช้ ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'completely switched off' โดยตรง จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: A central idea is that bilingual speakers must constantly manage two languages that are both active in the mind, even when only one is being used.

questions.q: Bilingual participants complete the ink-colour task with perfect accuracy.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] บอกเพียงว่าคนสองภาษาตอบได้เร็วกว่าเล็กน้อยและผิดพลาดน้อยกว่า แต่ไม่ได้ระบุว่าทำได้ถูกต้องสมบูรณ์ (perfect accuracy) ข้อความจึงไม่ได้ให้ข้อมูลนี้ ตอบ Not Given

questions.q: Speaking two languages can stop the brain disease that causes dementia from developing.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุชัดว่าการพูดสองภาษาไม่ได้ป้องกันตัวโรค เพียงแต่ทำให้อาการปรากฏช้าลงเท่านั้น จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง ตอบ False
questions.evidence: It is important to note, however, that bilingualism does not prevent the underlying disease; it appears only to delay the point at which symptoms become obvious.

questions.q: According to paragraph A, why did the early studies produce misleading results?
questions.a: They tested children in the language they knew less well and ignored social and economic factors.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] อธิบายข้อบกพร่องว่าทดสอบเด็กในภาษาที่อ่อนกว่าและไม่คำนึงถึงปัจจัยทางสังคม-เศรษฐกิจ ตัวเลือกนี้ตรงกับข้อความ ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้ถูกกล่าวถึง
options: They tested children in the language they knew less well and ignored social and economic factors.
They used tasks that were too difficult for young children.
They tested only children from wealthy families.

questions.q: What does the passage say the executive control system is responsible for?
questions.a: Switching attention, ignoring distractions and holding information in mind

options: Switching attention, ignoring distractions and holding information in mind

options: Studies showing a positive effect are more likely to be published than those showing none.

questions.evidence: Some argue that positive results are more likely to be published than studies showing no effect, which may exaggerate the size of the advantage.

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ใช้คำว่า 'suppress' สำหรับการยับยั้งภาษาที่ไม่ต้องการ เป็นคำที่ปรากฏตรงตัวในข้อความ (1 คำ)

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่าการจัดเรียงวัตถุตามกฎที่เปลี่ยนไปเชื่อมโยงกับ 'mental flexibility' คำที่ต้องเติมคือ flexibility (1 คำ)

title: The Waters of Rome
topic: How ancient Rome supplied its cities with water
passage:
[A] The ancient Romans are widely remembered as gifted engineers, and few of their achievements were as vital to daily life as the systems they built to move water. As their cities grew, local springs and wells could no longer meet demand. To solve this problem, Roman engineers constructed aqueducts, long channels that carried fresh water from distant sources to urban centres. The first of these, the Aqua Appia, was completed in 312 BC and ran largely underground. Over the following centuries, the network expanded until the city of Rome alone was served by eleven separate aqueducts.

[B] Contrary to popular belief, most of an aqueduct's length did not consist of the tall stone arches that tourists admire today. The majority of each channel ran beneath the ground, which protected the water from contamination and reduced the risk of damage. Elevated arches were built only where the water had to cross a valley or low-lying ground. The entire system relied on gravity: engineers surveyed the land carefully and gave each channel a gentle, continuous downward slope so that water flowed steadily without the need for pumps. Maintaining this slope over great distances required remarkable precision.

[C] Once the water reached a city, it was collected in a large distribution tank known as a castellum. From there, a network of lead and clay pipes carried it to public fountains, bathhouses and, in some cases, private homes. Public fountains were the most important outlet, since the majority of ordinary citizens did not have water piped directly into their houses and instead collected what they needed from these communal points. Wealthy households could pay for a private connection, though such privileges were taxed and closely regulated by officials.

[D] The scale of this supply was considerable. It has been estimated that the aqueducts delivered enough water for each inhabitant of Rome to enjoy a generous daily amount, far exceeding what many cities managed to provide until modern times. A dedicated body of workers, overseen by an official called the curator aquarum, was responsible for inspecting the channels, clearing blockages and repairing leaks. The writer Frontinus, who held this post in the first century AD, produced a detailed report on the aqueducts that remains a valuable source of information for historians today.

questions.q: The Aqua Appia was mostly built above ground on stone arches.
options: True
False
Not Given

questions.evidence: The first of these, the Aqua Appia, was completed in 312 BC and ran largely underground.

Wherever the water entered a city

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าซุ้มโค้งยกสูงถูกสร้าง "only where the water had to cross a valley or low-lying ground" ตรงกับตัวเลือกที่ถูก ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Elevated arches were built only where the water had to cross a valley or low-lying ground.

questions.q: What was the most important outlet for water once it reached a city?
options: Public fountains
Private homes of the wealthy

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงตัวว่า "Public fountains were the most important outlet" คำตอบจึงเป็นน้ำพุสาธารณะ
questions.evidence: Public fountains were the most important outlet, since the majority of ordinary citizens did not have water piped directly into their houses

questions.q: Who was responsible for overseeing the workers who maintained the aqueducts?
options: The curator aquarum
The Aqua Appia

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่ากลุ่มคนงานถูกกำกับดูแลโดยเจ้าหน้าที่ที่เรียกว่า curator aquarum คำตอบจึงเป็นตัวเลือกนี้ ส่วน Aqua Appia และ castellum เป็นสิ่งของ ไม่ใช่บุคคล
questions.evidence: A dedicated body of workers, overseen by an official called the curator aquarum, was responsible for inspecting the channels, clearing blockages and repairing leaks.

questions.q: The entire aqueduct system relied on ___ to make the water flow.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงตัวว่า "The entire system relied on gravity" คำที่เติมในช่องว่างคือ gravity
questions.evidence: The entire system relied on gravity: engineers surveyed the land carefully and gave each channel a gentle, continuous downward slope

questions.q: When water reached a city it was first collected in a large distribution tank called a ___.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าน้ำถูกเก็บในถังกระจายน้ำขนาดใหญ่ที่เรียกว่า castellum คำที่เติมในช่องว่างคือ castellum
questions.evidence: it was collected in a large distribution tank known as a castellum.

questions.q: Pipes carrying water through the city were made of ___ and clay.

questions.evidence: a network of lead and clay pipes carried it to public fountains, bathhouses and, in some cases, private homes.

title: Green Cities, Healthier Lives
topic: Urban green space and public health
passage:
[A] As the proportion of the world's population living in cities continues to rise, urban planners and public health researchers have turned their attention to the role of green space in supporting the wellbeing of city dwellers. The term "urban green space" refers to parks, gardens, tree-lined streets, sports fields and other vegetated areas within a built environment. For much of the twentieth century such spaces were valued mainly for their recreational appeal, but recent studies suggest that their benefits extend far beyond leisure, touching physical health, mental wellbeing and even the social fabric of neighbourhoods.

[B] A growing body of evidence links access to green space with measurable improvements in physical health. People who live within walking distance of a park tend to be more physically active, which reduces the risk of obesity, cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes. Green areas also help to moderate the urban climate. Trees provide shade and release moisture, lowering local temperatures during heatwaves, a phenomenon that becomes increasingly important as summers grow hotter. In addition, vegetation can filter airborne pollutants, although researchers caution that the extent of this effect depends heavily on the species of plant and the density of surrounding traffic.

[C] The psychological effects of green space have attracted particular interest. Several experiments have found that even brief exposure to natural settings can lower levels of the stress hormone cortisol and improve concentration. One influential study reported that hospital patients whose windows overlooked greenery recovered more quickly than those facing a brick wall. Researchers believe that natural environments allow the mind to rest and recover from the constant demands of urban life, a process sometimes described as attention restoration.

[D] Despite these advantages, access to green space is not distributed equally. In many cities, wealthier districts enjoy larger and better-maintained parks, while poorer neighbourhoods, whose residents may benefit most, are often underserved. Planners now argue that green space should be treated not as a luxury but as essential infrastructure, comparable to roads or water supply. Some municipalities have responded by converting disused industrial land into public gardens and by setting targets for the amount of greenery available per resident. Whether such measures can be sustained amid competing demands for housing and commercial development remains an open question.

questions.q: During the twentieth century, urban green spaces were mainly appreciated for their value as places for recreation.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุตรง ๆ ว่าตลอดช่วงศตวรรษที่ 20 พื้นที่สีเขียวถูกให้คุณค่าหลักในด้านการพักผ่อนหย่อนใจ (recreational appeal) ซึ่งตรงกับโจทย์ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: For much of the twentieth century such spaces were valued mainly for their recreational appeal, but recent studies suggest that their benefits extend far beyond leisure, touching physical health, mental wellbeing and even the social fabric of neighbourhoods.

questions.q: Living close to a park is associated with higher levels of physical activity.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่าคนที่อาศัยอยู่ในระยะเดินถึงสวนสาธารณะมักออกกำลังกายมากกว่า ซึ่งตรงกับโจทย์ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: People who live within walking distance of a park tend to be more physically active, which reduces the risk of obesity, cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes.

questions.q: The ability of plants to remove air pollution is the same regardless of plant type.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องขัดแย้งกับโจทย์โดยตรง เพราะระบุว่าผลของการกรองมลพิษขึ้นอยู่กับชนิดของพืชและความหนาแน่นของการจราจรอย่างมาก จึงไม่เท่ากันเสมอไป จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: In addition, vegetation can filter airborne pollutants, although researchers caution that the extent of this effect depends heavily on the species of plant and the density of surrounding traffic.

questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าการสัมผัสธรรมชาติแม้เพียงช่วงสั้น ๆ สามารถลดระดับฮอร์โมนความเครียด cortisol ได้ ซึ่งตรงกับโจทย์ จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Several experiments have found that even brief exposure to natural settings can lower levels of the stress hormone cortisol and improve concentration.

questions.q: Converting industrial land into gardens is more expensive than building new roads.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องกล่าวถึงการเปลี่ยนที่ดินอุตสาหกรรมเป็นสวน และเปรียบพื้นที่สีเขียวว่าเทียบได้กับถนน แต่ไม่เคยเปรียบเทียบ 'ค่าใช้จ่าย' ของทั้งสองสิ่งนี้เลย จึงไม่มีข้อมูลให้ตัดสิน ตอบ Not Given

questions.a: They lower local temperatures by giving shade and releasing moisture.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าต้นไม้ให้ร่มเงาและปล่อยความชื้น ทำให้อุณหภูมิในพื้นที่ลดลงระหว่างคลื่นความร้อน จึงตรงกับตัวเลือกนี้ ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ปรากฏในเนื้อเรื่อง (บล็อกแสงทั้งหมด, เก็บน้ำฝน, ดูดคาร์บอนไดออกไซด์)

questions.a: Patients with a view of greenery recovered more quickly.

options: Patients with a view of greenery recovered more quickly.

options: As essential infrastructure similar to roads or water supply.

title: From Tally Marks to Zero: The Story of Written Numerals
passage:
[A] Long before people learned to write words, they needed a way to record quantities. The earliest known method was the tally, in which a mark was cut into a bone or a piece of wood for each object being counted. Such tallies did not represent numbers as abstract ideas; they simply matched one scratch to one item. A herder, for example, might carve a notch for every sheep in a flock, so that a missing animal could be detected at a glance. This system worked well for small amounts but became clumsy as the totals grew larger.

[B] The first true written numerals appeared in Mesopotamia around 3400 BCE, when accountants pressed wedge-shaped marks into clay tablets to keep track of grain, cattle and taxes. Crucially, the Mesopotamians grouped their symbols, using one sign for units and another for tens, which meant that a single tablet could record substantial figures without endless repetition. The ancient Egyptians developed a comparable scheme, employing separate hieroglyphs for one, ten, one hundred and so on. Because the value of each symbol was fixed, the order in which they were written did not affect the total.

[C] A very different principle emerged in India. By roughly the sixth century CE, Indian mathematicians were using a system in which the position of a digit determined its value, so that the same symbol could mean one, ten or one hundred depending on where it stood. The genius of this arrangement lay in the invention of a symbol for nothing, a zero, which acted as a placeholder to show that a particular column was empty. This place-value system allowed even enormous numbers to be written with just ten signs.

[D] These Indian numerals travelled westward through the trade and scholarship of the medieval Islamic world, where they were refined and transmitted onward. European merchants and mathematicians gradually adopted them, largely because they made calculation far quicker than the older Roman numerals, which had no zero and relied on repeated letters. Although the new figures met resistance at first, their practical advantages proved decisive. The symbols we now call Arabic numerals are, in fact, the descendants of those Indian forms, and today they are used almost everywhere on Earth.

questions.q: Tally marks treated numbers as abstract concepts rather than as one-to-one matches.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุชัดว่า tally ไม่ได้แทนตัวเลขในเชิงนามธรรม แต่จับคู่หนึ่งรอยขีดต่อหนึ่งสิ่งที่นับ จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์โดยตรง ตอบ False
questions.evidence: Such tallies did not represent numbers as abstract ideas; they simply matched one scratch to one item.

questions.q: The tally system became inconvenient when the numbers being counted grew large.
questions.why_th: ประโยคสุดท้ายของย่อหน้า [A] บอกว่าระบบนี้ใช้ได้ดีกับจำนวนน้อยแต่กลับเทอะทะเมื่อจำนวนมากขึ้น ตรงกับโจทย์ ตอบ True
questions.evidence: This system worked well for small amounts but became clumsy as the totals grew larger.

questions.q: In the Egyptian system, changing the order of the symbols changed the total value.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่าเนื่องจากค่าของแต่ละสัญลักษณ์คงที่ ลำดับการเขียนจึงไม่มีผลต่อผลรวม จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์ ตอบ False
questions.evidence: Because the value of each symbol was fixed, the order in which they were written did not affect the total.

questions.q: The Mesopotamians used clay tablets because they were cheaper than other writing materials.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] กล่าวถึงการกดสัญลักษณ์ลงบนแผ่นดินเหนียว แต่ไม่ได้ให้เหตุผลว่าเลือกใช้เพราะราคาถูกกว่าวัสดุอื่น จึงไม่มีข้อมูลยืนยันหรือขัดแย้ง ตอบ Not Given

questions.q: Roman numerals included a symbol for zero.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] บอกว่าเลขโรมันไม่มีศูนย์ (had no zero) จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์โดยตรง ตอบ False

questions.a: To record quantities by matching one mark to each object
options: To record quantities by matching one mark to each object
To represent numbers as abstract mathematical ideas

To allow enormous numbers to be written with ten signs
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] อธิบายว่า tally คือการขีดหนึ่งรอยต่อหนึ่งสิ่งที่นับ ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นระบบยุคหลัง (Mesopotamia และ India) ตอบข้อแรก

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] เน้นว่าความอัจฉริยะของระบบอินเดียคือการประดิษฐ์ศูนย์ให้ทำหน้าที่บอกตำแหน่งที่ว่างเปล่า ตอบข้อแรก ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นลักษณะของ Mesopotamia
questions.evidence: The genius of this arrangement lay in the invention of a symbol for nothing, a zero, which acted as a placeholder to show that a particular column was empty.

questions.a: They made calculation far quicker than Roman numerals
options: They made calculation far quicker than Roman numerals
They were easier to carve into bone and wood
