questions.evidence: largely because they made calculation far quicker than the older Roman numerals

questions.q: In the Indian system, the ___ of a digit determined its value.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่าตำแหน่ง (position) ของตัวเลขเป็นตัวกำหนดค่า เติมคำตรงตามข้อความ
questions.evidence: a system in which the position of a digit determined its value

questions.q: A zero acted as a ___ to show that a particular column was empty.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ใช้คำว่า placeholder อธิบายหน้าที่ของศูนย์ในการบอกว่าคอลัมน์นั้นว่างเปล่า เติมคำตรงตามข้อความ
questions.evidence: a zero, which acted as a placeholder to show that a particular column was empty

questions.q: Indian numerals travelled westward through the trade and ___ of the medieval Islamic world.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] บอกว่าเลขอินเดียเดินทางไปทางตะวันตกผ่านการค้าและวิชาการ (scholarship) ของโลกอิสลามยุคกลาง เติมคำตรงตามข้อความ
questions.evidence: These Indian numerals travelled westward through the trade and scholarship of the medieval Islamic world

title: Fire and the Forest: How Woodlands Recover from Burning
topic: The ecology of wildfire and forest regeneration
passage:
[A] For much of the twentieth century, wildfire was regarded almost universally as a destructive force to be suppressed wherever possible. Forestry agencies invested heavily in detecting and extinguishing blazes, believing that every fire represented a loss of valuable timber and wildlife habitat. Ecologists have since revised this view considerably. Research across a range of forest types now indicates that periodic burning is a natural and often essential component of many woodland ecosystems, and that decades of aggressive suppression can leave forests more vulnerable rather than less.

[B] The reason lies in the way fire interacts with the accumulation of organic material on the forest floor. When small fires are prevented, dead leaves, fallen branches and dry undergrowth build up over many years. This material, known as fuel load, eventually becomes so dense that any ignition can trigger an unusually intense blaze. Such high-severity fires burn hotter and spread faster than the frequent, low-intensity fires that once cleared the understorey without harming mature trees. In this sense, the policy of suppression can inadvertently create the conditions for the very catastrophes it was designed to prevent.

[C] Many plant species have evolved not merely to survive fire but to depend on it. The seeds of certain pines, for example, are sealed inside cones coated in resin, and only the heat of a passing fire melts this coating and releases them. Other plants regenerate from underground structures that remain protected while the vegetation above is destroyed, sending up fresh shoots within weeks of a burn. Fire also clears the canopy temporarily, allowing sunlight to reach the soil and stimulating seeds that have lain dormant for years. The blackened landscape that follows a fire, though it may appear lifeless, is frequently the starting point for rapid renewal.

[D] Regeneration, however, is not guaranteed. Its success depends on the severity of the fire, the interval between successive burns, and the climate that follows. Where fires now occur more often than the local vegetation can tolerate, some forests are failing to recover and are gradually being replaced by shrubland or grassland. Rising temperatures and prolonged drought in several regions have lengthened fire seasons and increased the area burned each year, raising concern that certain ecosystems may cross a threshold beyond which their historic forest cover cannot return. Managing this balance, many ecologists argue, will increasingly require deliberately setting controlled fires rather than attempting to eliminate them altogether.

questions.q: During the twentieth century, forestry agencies spent large sums trying to put out wildfires.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุชัดเจนว่าหน่วยงานป่าไม้ลงทุนอย่างหนักในการตรวจจับและดับไฟ ซึ่งตรงกับข้อความในคำถาม จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Forestry agencies invested heavily in detecting and extinguishing blazes, believing that every fire represented a loss of valuable timber and wildlife habitat.

questions.q: Modern ecologists still agree with the twentieth-century belief that all fire should be suppressed.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] บอกว่านักนิเวศวิทยาได้ปรับเปลี่ยนมุมมองนี้ไปมากแล้ว จึงขัดแย้งกับคำถามที่ว่ายังคงเห็นด้วยกับความเชื่อเดิม จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: Ecologists have since revised this view considerably.

questions.q: High-severity fires move more quickly than the low-intensity fires that historically cleared the understorey.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่าไฟความรุนแรงสูงลุกลามเร็วกว่าไฟความรุนแรงต่ำที่เคยเผาชั้นไม้พื้นล่างมาก่อน จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Such high-severity fires burn hotter and spread faster than the frequent, low-intensity fires that once cleared the understorey without harming mature trees.

questions.q: Controlled burning is cheaper for governments than suppressing wildfires.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงการจุดไฟแบบควบคุมว่าจำเป็นต่อการจัดการ แต่ไม่เคยเปรียบเทียบเรื่องค่าใช้จ่ายว่าถูกกว่าการดับไฟหรือไม่ จึงตอบ Not Given

questions.q: Some forests affected by frequent fires are turning into shrubland or grassland.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุว่าเมื่อไฟเกิดบ่อยเกินไป ป่าบางแห่งไม่ฟื้นตัวและค่อยๆ ถูกแทนที่ด้วยไม้พุ่มหรือทุ่งหญ้า จึงตอบ True
questions.evidence: Where fires now occur more often than the local vegetation can tolerate, some forests are failing to recover and are gradually being replaced by shrubland or grassland.

questions.q: According to paragraph B, why can a long-standing policy of fire suppression be harmful?
questions.a: It allows fuel to build up until fires become unusually intense.
options: It allows fuel to build up until fires become unusually intense.
It kills mature trees that would otherwise survive small fires.
It prevents seeds from being released from resin-coated cones.
It reduces the amount of sunlight reaching the forest floor.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] อธิบายว่าเมื่อห้ามไฟเล็ก เชื้อเพลิงจะสะสมจนหนาแน่นและทำให้ไฟรุนแรงผิดปกติ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงกับเหตุผลนี้ (เป็นรายละเอียดจากย่อหน้าอื่นหรือไม่ปรากฏในบทความ)
questions.evidence: This material, known as fuel load, eventually becomes so dense that any ignition can trigger an unusually intense blaze.

questions.q: What does the passage say about certain pine seeds?
questions.a: They are released only when fire melts the resin coating on their cones.
options: They are released only when fire melts the resin coating on their cones.
They survive underground while the plant above is destroyed.
They can remain dormant in the soil for many years before sprouting.
They germinate more successfully in the absence of sunlight.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่าเมล็ดสนถูกห่อหุ้มในโคนที่เคลือบด้วยยาง และความร้อนจากไฟทำให้ยางละลายและปล่อยเมล็ดออกมา ตัวเลือกอื่นกล่าวถึงพืชชนิดอื่นในย่อหน้าเดียวกัน
questions.evidence: The seeds of certain pines, for example, are sealed inside cones coated in resin, and only the heat of a passing fire melts this coating and releases them.

questions.q: Which factor is NOT mentioned in paragraph D as affecting whether a forest regenerates successfully?
questions.a: The type of soil in which the trees grow.
options: The type of soil in which the trees grow.
How severe the fire was.
The time between one fire and the next.
The climate that follows the fire.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุปัจจัยสามอย่าง คือ ความรุนแรงของไฟ ช่วงเวลาระหว่างไฟแต่ละครั้ง และสภาพอากาศที่ตามมา แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวถึงชนิดของดิน จึงเป็นคำตอบที่ถูกต้อง
questions.evidence: Its success depends on the severity of the fire, the interval between successive burns, and the climate that follows.

questions.q: What overall change in fire conditions does the passage link to rising temperatures and drought?
questions.a: Longer fire seasons and a greater area burned each year.
options: Longer fire seasons and a greater area burned each year.
A faster recovery of forests after burning.
A reduction in the density of fuel on the forest floor.
An increase in the number of resin-coated pine cones.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุว่าอุณหภูมิที่สูงขึ้นและภัยแล้งทำให้ฤดูไฟยาวนานขึ้นและพื้นที่ถูกเผาไหม้มากขึ้นในแต่ละปี ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ปรากฏในบทความ
questions.evidence: Rising temperatures and prolonged drought in several regions have lengthened fire seasons and increased the area burned each year, raising concern that certain ecosystems may cross a threshold beyond which their historic forest cover cannot return.

questions.q: The build-up of dead leaves, fallen branches and dry undergrowth on the forest floor is known as ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] เรียกวัสดุที่สะสมบนพื้นป่านี้ว่า fuel load ซึ่งตรงกับคำในบทความและอยู่ในขอบเขตสองคำ
questions.evidence: This material, known as fuel load, eventually becomes so dense that any ignition can trigger an unusually intense blaze.

questions.q: Some plants regenerate from ___ structures that stay protected while the vegetation above is destroyed.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ใช้คำว่า underground structures ที่ยังคงได้รับการปกป้องขณะที่ต้นด้านบนถูกทำลาย จึงเติมคำว่า underground
questions.evidence: Other plants regenerate from underground structures that remain protected while the vegetation above is destroyed, sending up fresh shoots within weeks of a burn.

questions.q: By temporarily clearing the canopy, fire allows ___ to reach the soil and stimulates dormant seeds.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่าไฟเปิดเรือนยอดชั่วคราวทำให้แสงแดด (sunlight) ส่องถึงดินและกระตุ้นเมล็ดที่จำศีลอยู่
questions.evidence: Fire also clears the canopy temporarily, allowing sunlight to reach the soil and stimulating seeds that have lain dormant for years.

title: Bearing the Weight: The Physics of Bridges
topic: The physics of how bridges bear load
passage:
[A] Every bridge must solve a single fundamental problem: how to carry loads safely across a gap without collapsing. Engineers distinguish between two broad categories of load. The first is the dead load, which is the constant weight of the structure itself, including the deck, beams and railings. The second is the live load, which varies over time and includes vehicles, pedestrians and environmental forces such as wind and snow. A well-designed bridge must transfer both types of load through its supports and ultimately into the ground, where the earth absorbs the forces. If any part of this pathway is too weak, the structure fails.

[B] When a load is applied, materials within the bridge experience two opposing internal forces: compression and tension. Compression squeezes material together, shortening it, while tension stretches it apart. Consider a simple beam bridge resting on two piers. As a heavy truck moves across the centre, the top surface of the beam is compressed while the underside is placed under tension. Different materials respond differently to these forces. Stone and concrete resist compression extremely well but are relatively weak in tension, which is why unreinforced concrete beams tend to crack along their lower edge. Steel, by contrast, performs strongly under both compression and tension, making it a versatile choice for modern construction.

[C] The shape of a bridge largely determines how these forces are distributed. In an arch bridge, the curved structure converts the downward weight of the deck into compression that travels along the curve and outward to the supports at each end. Because stone is strong in compression, arches allowed ancient builders to span considerable distances using only masonry. A suspension bridge works on an entirely different principle. Here the deck hangs from vertical cables, which are attached to two enormous main cables draped between tall towers. The main cables carry the load in tension and transfer it to massive anchorages buried in the ground at either end. This arrangement enables suspension bridges to cross exceptionally wide gaps, such as river estuaries.

[D] Engineers never design a bridge to carry only the maximum load it is expected to meet. Instead, they build in a safety factor, deliberately making the structure several times stronger than necessary. This precaution accounts for unexpected events, material defects and gradual weakening over decades of use. Bridges must also be able to flex slightly, because a completely rigid structure would crack under repeated stress or sudden movement. For this reason, expansion joints are incorporated to allow the materials to expand and contract as temperatures change, preventing damaging internal stresses from building up.

questions.q: Dead load refers to forces that change over time, such as passing traffic.
questions.why_th: Passage ระบุว่า dead load คือน้ำหนักคงที่ของโครงสร้างเอง ส่วน live load ต่างหากที่เปลี่ยนแปลงตามเวลา เช่น ยานพาหนะ ข้อความนี้จึงขัดแย้งกับ passage โดยตรง = False
questions.evidence: The first is the dead load, which is the constant weight of the structure itself, including the deck, beams and railings.

questions.q: Steel is able to withstand both compression and tension effectively.
questions.why_th: Passage บอกชัดเจนว่าเหล็ก (Steel) ทำงานได้ดีทั้งภายใต้ compression และ tension = True
questions.evidence: Steel, by contrast, performs strongly under both compression and tension, making it a versatile choice for modern construction.

questions.q: Ancient stone arch bridges were more expensive to build than modern steel bridges.
questions.why_th: Passage กล่าวว่า arch หินสามารถ span ระยะไกลได้ แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบค่าใช้จ่ายระหว่างสะพานหินโบราณกับสะพานเหล็กสมัยใหม่เลย จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: In a suspension bridge, the main cables bear the load through tension.
questions.why_th: Passage ระบุว่า main cables รับน้ำหนักในรูปของ tension แล้วส่งต่อไปยัง anchorages = True
questions.evidence: The main cables carry the load in tension and transfer it to massive anchorages buried in the ground at either end.

questions.q: A perfectly rigid bridge would perform better than one that can flex.
questions.why_th: Passage บอกว่าโครงสร้างที่แข็งเกร็งอย่างสมบูรณ์จะแตกร้าวภายใต้แรงเค้นซ้ำ ๆ ข้อความนี้จึงขัดแย้งกับ passage = False
questions.evidence: Bridges must also be able to flex slightly, because a completely rigid structure would crack under repeated stress or sudden movement.

questions.q: According to the passage, why does unreinforced concrete tend to crack along its lower edge in a beam bridge?
questions.a: Because concrete is weak in tension, which occurs on the underside
options: Because concrete is weak in tension, which occurs on the underside
Because concrete is weak in compression, which occurs on the underside
Because the top surface of the beam is stretched apart
Because concrete cannot withstand any compression at all
questions.why_th: Passage บอกว่าใต้คานเกิด tension และคอนกรีตอ่อนแอต่อ tension จึงร้าวที่ขอบล่าง ตัวเลือกอื่นสลับ compression/tension หรือผิดข้อเท็จจริง
questions.evidence: Stone and concrete resist compression extremely well but are relatively weak in tension, which is why unreinforced concrete beams tend to crack along their lower edge.

questions.q: How does an arch bridge handle the downward weight of the deck?
questions.a: It converts the weight into compression that travels along the curve to the supports
options: It converts the weight into compression that travels along the curve to the supports
It converts the weight into tension carried by hanging cables
It transfers the weight directly to anchorages buried in the ground
It relies on expansion joints to absorb the downward force
questions.why_th: Passage อธิบายว่า arch เปลี่ยนน้ำหนักที่กดลงเป็น compression วิ่งไปตามส่วนโค้งสู่จุดรองรับ ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นหลักการของ suspension bridge หรือไม่ตรง
questions.evidence: In an arch bridge, the curved structure converts the downward weight of the deck into compression that travels along the curve and outward to the supports at each end.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of building in a safety factor?
questions.a: To make the structure several times stronger than the maximum expected load requires
options: To make the structure several times stronger than the maximum expected load requires
To reduce the overall cost of construction materials
To allow the bridge to carry exactly its maximum expected load
To eliminate the need for expansion joints
questions.why_th: Passage บอกว่า safety factor คือการทำให้โครงสร้างแข็งแรงกว่าที่จำเป็นหลายเท่า เผื่อเหตุไม่คาดฝัน ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับเนื้อหา
questions.evidence: Instead, they build in a safety factor, deliberately making the structure several times stronger than necessary.

questions.q: According to the passage, what ultimately absorbs the forces carried through a bridge's supports?
options: The ground
The main cables
The dead load
The expansion joints
questions.why_th: Passage ระบุว่าแรงถูกส่งผ่านจุดรองรับลงสู่พื้นดิน ซึ่งดินเป็นตัวดูดซับแรงไว้ = The ground
questions.evidence: A well-designed bridge must transfer both types of load through its supports and ultimately into the ground, where the earth absorbs the forces.

questions.q: Compression squeezes material together and shortens it, whereas ___ stretches it apart.
questions.why_th: Passage อธิบายว่า tension ยืดวัสดุออกจากกัน ตรงข้ามกับ compression คำตอบคือ tension ตามคำในเนื้อเรื่อง
questions.evidence: Compression squeezes material together, shortening it, while tension stretches it apart.

questions.q: In a suspension bridge, the deck hangs from vertical cables attached to two enormous ___ that are draped between tall towers.
questions.why_th: Passage บอกว่า cables แนวตั้งติดกับ main cables ขนาดใหญ่ที่พาดระหว่างเสาสูง คำตอบคือ main cables (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: Here the deck hangs from vertical cables, which are attached to two enormous main cables draped between tall towers.

questions.q: ___ are incorporated into bridges to allow materials to expand and contract as temperatures change.
questions.why_th: Passage ระบุว่า expansion joints ถูกใส่เข้าไปเพื่อให้วัสดุขยายและหดตัวได้เมื่ออุณหภูมิเปลี่ยน คำตอบคือ expansion joints (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: For this reason, expansion joints are incorporated to allow the materials to expand and contract as temperatures change, preventing damaging internal stresses from building up.

title: The Silent Retreat: Why Some Languages Are Disappearing
passage:
[A] Of the roughly 7,000 languages spoken in the world today, linguists estimate that nearly half may vanish by the end of this century. On average, a language falls silent every few months when its last fluent speaker dies. This process, known as language death, is not new — languages have always risen and declined — but researchers agree that the current rate of loss is unprecedented in human history.

[B] The most common cause of language disappearance is not the physical extinction of its speakers but a gradual shift to a more dominant tongue. Parents often stop passing their mother tongue to their children because they believe a dominant language offers better prospects for education and employment. Within two or three generations, a community's original language can be reduced to a handful of elderly speakers. Economic pressure is therefore a powerful driver: in many regions, a minority language is associated with poverty and rural life, while the dominant language is linked to modernity and social advancement.

[C] Government policy has also played a significant role. During the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, many states promoted a single official language in schools and public institutions, sometimes punishing children for using their home language in the classroom. Urbanisation intensifies the trend, as migrants from different linguistic backgrounds settle in cities where only the dominant language allows communication. More recently, mass media and the internet have strengthened the position of a few global languages, since most online content is produced in only a small fraction of the world's languages.

[D] The consequences of this loss extend far beyond communication. Each language encodes unique knowledge about local plants, medicines, and ecosystems, accumulated over centuries. When a language dies, this knowledge frequently dies with it, because much of it has never been written down. Linguists also point out that every language offers evidence about how the human mind organises ideas, so each loss narrows the scientific record.

[E] Efforts to reverse the decline have achieved some success. Welsh, once in steady retreat, has grown thanks to bilingual education and television broadcasting, while Hebrew was famously revived as an everyday spoken language in the twentieth century. Similar programmes now support Maori in New Zealand and Hawaiian in the United States. Technology, often blamed for spreading dominant languages, is increasingly used to record endangered ones, with smartphone apps and online dictionaries helping young people reconnect with the speech of their grandparents. Whether such measures can outpace the forces of globalisation, however, remains uncertain.

questions.q: Languages never disappeared before the modern era.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการตายของภาษาไม่ใช่เรื่องใหม่ ภาษาเกิดขึ้นและเสื่อมถอยมาโดยตลอด จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: This process, known as language death, is not new — languages have always risen and declined — but researchers agree that the current rate of loss is unprecedented in human history.

questions.q: Languages usually disappear because all of their speakers die out.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าสาเหตุที่พบบ่อยที่สุดไม่ใช่การที่ผู้พูดเสียชีวิตทั้งหมด แต่เป็นการค่อย ๆ เปลี่ยนไปใช้ภาษาที่มีอิทธิพลมากกว่า
questions.evidence: The most common cause of language disappearance is not the physical extinction of its speakers but a gradual shift to a more dominant tongue.

questions.q: Some children were punished at school for using their home language.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C กล่าวชัดเจนว่าบางรัฐลงโทษเด็กที่ใช้ภาษาบ้านเกิดในห้องเรียน
questions.evidence: During the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, many states promoted a single official language in schools and public institutions, sometimes punishing children for using their home language in the classroom.

questions.q: Most linguists learn to speak fluently the endangered languages they study.
questions.why_th: บทความพูดถึงนักภาษาศาสตร์ที่ประเมินและชี้ให้เห็นคุณค่าของภาษา แต่ไม่ได้บอกเลยว่าพวกเขาเรียนพูดภาษาที่ใกล้สูญหรือไม่

questions.q: Hebrew was brought back into everyday spoken use in the twentieth century.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E ระบุว่าภาษาฮีบรูถูกฟื้นฟูให้เป็นภาษาพูดในชีวิตประจำวันในศตวรรษที่ 20
questions.evidence: Welsh, once in steady retreat, has grown thanks to bilingual education and television broadcasting, while Hebrew was famously revived as an everyday spoken language in the twentieth century.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do parents often stop passing their mother tongue to their children?
options: They believe a dominant language offers better prospects for education and employment
They are legally forbidden to use the mother tongue at home
They find the mother tongue too difficult to teach
They want their children to move to rural areas
questions.a: They believe a dominant language offers better prospects for education and employment
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกตรง ๆ ว่าพ่อแม่เชื่อว่าภาษาหลักให้โอกาสด้านการศึกษาและการทำงานที่ดีกว่า ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในบทความ
questions.evidence: Parents often stop passing their mother tongue to their children because they believe a dominant language offers better prospects for education and employment.

questions.q: What does the writer say about content on the internet?
options: Most of it is produced in only a small fraction of the world's languages
It is equally divided among all major languages
It has weakened the position of global languages
It is mainly created by government institutions
questions.a: Most of it is produced in only a small fraction of the world's languages
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าเนื้อหาออนไลน์ส่วนใหญ่ผลิตในภาษาเพียงส่วนน้อยของภาษาทั้งหมดในโลก ซึ่งยิ่งเสริมอำนาจภาษาหลัก
questions.evidence: More recently, mass media and the internet have strengthened the position of a few global languages, since most online content is produced in only a small fraction of the world's languages.

questions.q: According to paragraph D, what often happens when a language dies?
options: Knowledge about local plants, medicines, and ecosystems is frequently lost with it
Its speakers quickly write down their traditional knowledge
Scientists immediately record how its speakers organised ideas
Neighbouring communities adopt its vocabulary
questions.a: Knowledge about local plants, medicines, and ecosystems is frequently lost with it
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D อธิบายว่าเมื่อภาษาตาย ความรู้เฉพาะถิ่นมักตายตามไปด้วย เพราะส่วนใหญ่ไม่เคยถูกบันทึกเป็นลายลักษณ์อักษร
questions.evidence: When a language dies, this knowledge frequently dies with it, because much of it has never been written down.

questions.q: Which language does the writer say has grown thanks to bilingual education and television broadcasting?
options: Welsh
Hebrew
Maori
Hawaiian
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าภาษาเวลส์เติบโตขึ้นเพราะการศึกษาสองภาษาและการออกอากาศทางโทรทัศน์ ส่วนฮีบรูคือตัวอย่างการฟื้นฟู และเมารี/ฮาวายเป็นโครงการที่คล้ายกัน
questions.evidence: Welsh, once in steady retreat, has grown thanks to bilingual education and television broadcasting, while Hebrew was famously revived as an everyday spoken language in the twentieth century.

questions.q: On average, a language falls silent every few months when its last ___ speaker dies.
questions.why_th: คำที่หายไปคือ 'fluent' ตามประโยคในย่อหน้า A ที่ว่าภาษาเงียบหายไปเมื่อผู้พูดที่พูดคล่องคนสุดท้ายเสียชีวิต
questions.evidence: On average, a language falls silent every few months when its last fluent speaker dies.

questions.q: In many regions, a minority language is associated with poverty and ___ life.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่าภาษาชนกลุ่มน้อยถูกเชื่อมโยงกับความยากจนและชีวิตชนบท (rural life) ตรงข้ามกับภาษาหลักที่สื่อถึงความทันสมัย
questions.evidence: Economic pressure is therefore a powerful driver: in many regions, a minority language is associated with poverty and rural life, while the dominant language is linked to modernity and social advancement.

questions.q: Smartphone apps and ___ are helping young people reconnect with the speech of their grandparents.
questions.why_th: คำตอบคือ 'online dictionaries' ตามย่อหน้า E ซึ่งกล่าวถึงเทคโนโลยีที่ใช้บันทึกภาษาใกล้สูญและช่วยคนรุ่นใหม่กลับมาเชื่อมโยงกับภาษาของปู่ย่าตายาย
questions.evidence: Technology, often blamed for spreading dominant languages, is increasingly used to record endangered ones, with smartphone apps and online dictionaries helping young people reconnect with the speech of their grandparents.

title: Reaching for the Sky: The Engineering of Skyscrapers
passage:
[A] The skyscraper is perhaps the most visible symbol of modern engineering ambition. Before the late nineteenth century, the height of a building was restricted by the load-bearing masonry walls that supported it: the taller the structure, the thicker its lower walls had to be. This constraint disappeared in 1885, when the Home Insurance Building in Chicago introduced an internal skeleton of iron and steel that carried the weight of the floors and walls. Because the frame, rather than the walls, bore the load, exterior surfaces could be made of light materials such as glass, and architects were suddenly free to design buildings far taller than anything previously imagined.

[B] Height, however, creates difficulties of its own, the most serious of which is wind. A tall tower behaves rather like a vertical beam fixed at one end, and strong gusts can cause its upper floors to sway by a metre or more. Although this movement rarely threatens the structure itself, it can produce discomfort and even nausea among occupants. Engineers therefore install devices to limit the motion. The best known is the tuned mass damper, a heavy weight suspended near the top of a tower that swings in the opposite direction to the building's movement, absorbing much of its energy. In Taipei 101 in Taiwan, the damper is a steel sphere weighing 660 tonnes.

[C] What lies below ground is equally important. A skyscraper's enormous weight must be transferred safely into the earth, and the method chosen depends chiefly on local geology. In Manhattan, towers can rest directly on solid bedrock, which lies close to the surface. In Dubai, by contrast, firm rock is buried deep beneath layers of sand, so engineers drive concrete piles dozens of metres into the ground, relying on friction between the piles and the surrounding soil, rather than contact with rock, to hold the building in place. The Burj Khalifa, currently the world's tallest building, stands on 192 such piles.

[D] Vertical transport presents a final challenge, for without fast and safe lifts no tall office building would be practical. The weight of conventional steel cable limits the distance a single lift can travel, so the tallest towers are divided into zones, with passengers changing lifts at intermediate floors known as sky lobbies. Recent developments, including lightweight carbon-fibre ropes and cabins that move both vertically and horizontally, may eventually remove this limit. Whatever the technology, the underlying principle of skyscraper engineering remains unchanged: every gain in height must be matched by an equally careful response to the forces that height creates.

questions.q: Before the late nineteenth century, taller buildings required thicker walls at their base.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุชัดว่า ยิ่งอาคารสูงเท่าไร ผนังส่วนล่างก็ยิ่งต้องหนาขึ้นเท่านั้น จึงตรงกับข้อความ (True)
questions.evidence: Before the late nineteenth century, the height of a building was restricted by the load-bearing masonry walls that supported it: the taller the structure, the thicker its lower walls had to be.

questions.q: The swaying of tall buildings in strong wind often causes serious damage to the structure.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าการโยกตัว 'แทบไม่เป็นอันตรายต่อตัวโครงสร้าง' (rarely threatens the structure) จึงขัดแย้งโดยตรงกับคำถามที่ว่าเกิดความเสียหายบ่อยครั้ง (False)
questions.evidence: Although this movement rarely threatens the structure itself, it can produce discomfort and even nausea among occupants.

questions.q: Taipei 101 was the first building in the world to use a tuned mass damper.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวเพียงว่า Taipei 101 มี damper เป็นลูกเหล็กหนัก 660 ตัน แต่ไม่ได้ระบุว่าเป็นอาคารแรกของโลกที่ใช้อุปกรณ์นี้ จึงไม่มีข้อมูลยืนยันหรือขัดแย้ง (Not Given)

questions.q: In Manhattan, solid rock is located near ground level.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าในแมนแฮตตัน หินแข็ง (bedrock) อยู่ใกล้ผิวดิน อาคารจึงตั้งบนหินได้โดยตรง (True)
questions.evidence: In Manhattan, towers can rest directly on solid bedrock, which lies close to the surface.

questions.q: The piles beneath the Burj Khalifa are held in place by direct contact with rock.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเสาเข็มในดูไบอาศัยแรงเสียดทานระหว่างเสาเข็มกับดินโดยรอบ 'ไม่ใช่การสัมผัสกับหิน' จึงขัดแย้งกับคำถาม (False)
questions.evidence: In Dubai, by contrast, firm rock is buried deep beneath layers of sand, so engineers drive concrete piles dozens of metres into the ground, relying on friction between the piles and the surrounding soil, rather than contact with rock, to hold the building in place.

questions.q: According to the passage, why did the steel frame allow buildings to become much taller?
options: It carried the building's weight instead of the walls
It made buildings lighter than glass
It protected buildings from strong winds
It reduced the cost of masonry walls
questions.a: It carried the building's weight instead of the walls
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าเมื่อโครงเหล็ก (frame) รับน้ำหนักแทนผนัง ผนังภายนอกจึงใช้วัสดุเบาได้ และสถาปนิกออกแบบอาคารให้สูงขึ้นได้มาก
questions.evidence: Because the frame, rather than the walls, bore the load, exterior surfaces could be made of light materials such as glass, and architects were suddenly free to design buildings far taller than anything previously imagined.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of a tuned mass damper?
options: To absorb the energy of a building's movement
To strengthen the foundations of a tower
To reduce the weight of the upper floors
To generate electricity from wind
questions.a: To absorb the energy of a building's movement
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า tuned mass damper คือน้ำหนักถ่วงที่แกว่งสวนทางกับการเคลื่อนไหวของอาคารเพื่อดูดซับพลังงานส่วนใหญ่ของการโยกตัว
questions.evidence: The best known is the tuned mass damper, a heavy weight suspended near the top of a tower that swings in the opposite direction to the building's movement, absorbing much of its energy.

questions.q: According to the passage, the choice of foundation method depends mainly on
options: the local geology
the height of the building
the cost of concrete piles
the age of the surrounding buildings
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงตัวว่า วิธีการทำฐานรากที่เลือกใช้ 'depends chiefly on local geology' คือขึ้นอยู่กับสภาพธรณีวิทยาในพื้นที่เป็นหลัก
questions.evidence: A skyscraper's enormous weight must be transferred safely into the earth, and the method chosen depends chiefly on local geology.

questions.q: Why are the tallest towers divided into lift zones?
options: Because cable weight limits how far a single lift can travel
Because passengers prefer shorter journeys
Because carbon-fibre ropes are too expensive
Because lifts must also move horizontally
questions.a: Because cable weight limits how far a single lift can travel
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าน้ำหนักของสายเคเบิลเหล็กแบบเดิมจำกัดระยะทางที่ลิฟต์ตัวเดียวจะวิ่งได้ อาคารสูงมากจึงต้องแบ่งเป็นโซน
questions.evidence: The weight of conventional steel cable limits the distance a single lift can travel, so the tallest towers are divided into zones, with passengers changing lifts at intermediate floors known as sky lobbies.

questions.q: Because the steel frame carried the load, exterior surfaces could be made of light materials such as ___.
questions.why_th: คำตอบคือ 'glass' ตามข้อความในย่อหน้า A ที่ยกตัวอย่างวัสดุเบาที่ใช้ทำผิวภายนอกอาคาร
questions.evidence: Because the frame, rather than the walls, bore the load, exterior surfaces could be made of light materials such as glass, and architects were suddenly free to design buildings far taller than anything previously imagined.

questions.q: The damper in Taipei 101 is a steel ___ weighing 660 tonnes.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า damper ของ Taipei 101 เป็น 'steel sphere' (ลูกกลมเหล็ก) หนัก 660 ตัน คำที่หายไปคือ 'sphere'
questions.evidence: In Taipei 101 in Taiwan, the damper is a steel sphere weighing 660 tonnes.

questions.q: In the tallest towers, passengers change lifts at intermediate floors known as ___.
questions.why_th: บทความเรียกชั้นเปลี่ยนลิฟต์ระหว่างทางว่า 'sky lobbies' คำตอบต้องคัดลอกตรงตัวสองคำนี้จากย่อหน้า D
questions.evidence: The weight of conventional steel cable limits the distance a single lift can travel, so the tallest towers are divided into zones, with passengers changing lifts at intermediate floors known as sky lobbies.

title: Seeing in Colour: How the Human Eye Perceives Colour
topic: How the human eye perceives colour
passage:
[A] Colour is not a property that exists in objects themselves; rather, it is a sensation produced inside the human brain in response to light. When light strikes an object, the surface absorbs certain wavelengths and reflects others. The reflected wavelengths enter the eye, where they are converted into electrical signals that the brain interprets as colour. Visible light occupies only a narrow band of the electromagnetic spectrum, with wavelengths ranging from roughly 380 to 700 nanometres. Shorter wavelengths are perceived as blue and violet, while longer wavelengths appear red.

[B] The detection of colour depends on specialised cells in the retina, the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye. Two main types of photoreceptor exist: rods and cones. Rods are extremely sensitive to light and allow us to see in dim conditions, but they do not distinguish colour. Cones, by contrast, are responsible for colour vision and function best in bright light. The human retina contains approximately six million cones, concentrated in a central region called the fovea. This concentration explains why colours appear most vivid when we look directly at an object.

[C] Human colour vision is described as trichromatic because it relies on three kinds of cone, each containing a different pigment that responds most strongly to a particular range of wavelengths. These are often labelled short, medium and long cones, corresponding broadly to blue, green and red light. No single cone type can identify a colour on its own. Instead, the brain compares the relative strength of the signals arriving from all three types and combines them to generate the enormous variety of hues that people can perceive.

[D] When one or more cone types are absent or faulty, colour vision is impaired, a condition commonly known as colour blindness. The most frequent form makes it difficult to distinguish red from green, and it affects men far more often than women because the relevant genes are carried on the X chromosome. Such individuals are rarely unable to see colour entirely; they simply confuse certain shades that people with normal vision separate easily.

[E] Colour perception is also shaped by context and by the brain's expectations. A phenomenon called colour constancy allows us to recognise an object as having the same colour under very different lighting conditions, so a white shirt still looks white in both sunlight and artificial light. The brain achieves this by taking into account the colour of the surrounding illumination and adjusting its interpretation accordingly. Perception of colour is therefore an active process, not a simple recording of the light that reaches the eye.

questions.q: Colour exists as a fixed physical property within objects themselves.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุชัดว่าสีไม่ใช่คุณสมบัติที่มีอยู่ในวัตถุ แต่เป็นความรู้สึกที่เกิดในสมอง จึงขัดแย้งโดยตรง
questions.evidence: Colour is not a property that exists in objects themselves; rather, it is a sensation produced inside the human brain in response to light.

questions.q: Rods enable people to see colours in dim lighting.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่า rods ไวต่อแสงและช่วยมองเห็นในที่มืด แต่ไม่แยกแยะสี จึงขัดแย้ง
questions.evidence: Rods are extremely sensitive to light and allow us to see in dim conditions, but they do not distinguish colour.

questions.q: A single cone type is able to identify a colour by itself.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าไม่มี cone ชนิดใดระบุสีได้เพียงลำพัง จึงขัดแย้งโดยตรง
questions.evidence: No single cone type can identify a colour on its own.

questions.q: Colour blindness usually results in a complete inability to see any colour.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าผู้ที่มีภาวะนี้ไม่ค่อยมองไม่เห็นสีเลย เพียงสับสนบางเฉดสี จึงขัดแย้ง
questions.evidence: Such individuals are rarely unable to see colour entirely; they simply confuse certain shades that people with normal vision separate easily.

questions.q: Women are more likely than men to have red-green colour blindness.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าภาวะนี้พบในผู้ชายบ่อยกว่าผู้หญิงมาก จึงขัดแย้ง
questions.evidence: The most frequent form makes it difficult to distinguish red from green, and it affects men far more often than women because the relevant genes are carried on the X chromosome.

questions.q: Eating certain foods can improve the function of cones in the retina.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องไม่ได้กล่าวถึงอาหารกับการทำงานของ cone เลย จึงไม่มีข้อมูล

questions.q: According to the passage, why do colours appear most vivid when we look directly at an object?
questions.a: Because cones are concentrated in the central region called the fovea
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่า cone กระจุกตัวใน fovea ทำให้สีดูสดที่สุดเมื่อมองตรง ๆ
questions.evidence: This concentration explains why colours appear most vivid when we look directly at an object.
options: Because cones are concentrated in the central region called the fovea
Because rods send stronger signals in bright light
Because direct light contains more wavelengths
Because the fovea contains six million rods

questions.q: Human colour vision is described as trichromatic because it relies on:
questions.a: three kinds of cone, each containing a different pigment
questions.why_th: ข้อความอธิบายว่า trichromatic เพราะอาศัย cone สามชนิดที่มี pigment ต่างกัน
questions.evidence: Human colour vision is described as trichromatic because it relies on three kinds of cone, each containing a different pigment that responds most strongly to a particular range of wavelengths.
options: three kinds of cone, each containing a different pigment
three types of rod in the fovea
a single pigment sensitive to all wavelengths
the absorption of light by the object's surface

questions.q: How does the brain generate the wide variety of hues that people perceive?
questions.a: By comparing the relative strength of signals from all three cone types
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าสมองเปรียบเทียบความแรงของสัญญาณจาก cone ทั้งสามชนิดแล้วรวมกัน
questions.evidence: Instead, the brain compares the relative strength of the signals arriving from all three types and combines them to generate the enormous variety of hues that people can perceive.
options: By comparing the relative strength of signals from all three cone types
By relying only on the long cones for red light
By using rods to add brightness to each hue
By measuring the wavelength absorbed by the object

questions.q: What does colour constancy allow people to do?
questions.a: Recognise an object as the same colour under different lighting conditions
questions.why_th: ข้อความอธิบายว่า colour constancy ทำให้จำสีของวัตถุได้เหมือนเดิมภายใต้แสงต่างกัน
questions.evidence: A phenomenon called colour constancy allows us to recognise an object as having the same colour under very different lighting conditions, so a white shirt still looks white in both sunlight and artificial light.
options: Recognise an object as the same colour under different lighting conditions
See colours clearly in complete darkness
Distinguish red from green without cones
Detect wavelengths outside the visible spectrum

questions.q: Visible light forms only a narrow band of the ___ spectrum.
questions.why_th: ข้อความใช้คำว่า electromagnetic spectrum โดยตรง
questions.evidence: Visible light occupies only a narrow band of the electromagnetic spectrum, with wavelengths ranging from roughly 380 to 700 nanometres.

questions.q: The photoreceptors responsible for colour vision are called ___.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่า cones เป็นตัวรับผิดชอบการมองเห็นสี
questions.evidence: Cones, by contrast, are responsible for colour vision and function best in bright light.

questions.q: To achieve colour constancy, the brain takes into account the colour of the surrounding ___.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าสมองพิจารณาสีของ illumination ที่อยู่รอบ ๆ
questions.evidence: The brain achieves this by taking into account the colour of the surrounding illumination and adjusting its interpretation accordingly.

title: From Nebula to Black Hole: The Lifecycle of a Star
passage:
[A] Stars begin their existence within vast clouds of gas and dust known as nebulae. These clouds consist primarily of hydrogen, the most abundant element in the universe. When a region of a nebula becomes sufficiently dense, often triggered by the shockwave from a nearby exploding star, gravity causes the material to collapse inward. As the collapsing cloud contracts, its core heats up, forming a protostar. This early stage may last several hundred thousand years. Once the core temperature reaches approximately 15 million degrees Celsius, nuclear fusion begins: hydrogen atoms combine to form helium, releasing enormous quantities of energy.

[B] The onset of fusion marks the beginning of a star's longest phase, known as the main sequence. During this period, the outward pressure generated by fusion balances the inward pull of gravity, keeping the star stable. The Sun, a medium-sized star, has been in its main sequence for roughly 4.6 billion years and is expected to remain there for a similar length of time. The duration of this phase depends chiefly on a star's mass. Contrary to what might be expected, massive stars exhaust their fuel far more quickly than smaller ones, because they burn hydrogen at a much greater rate. A star many times heavier than the Sun may survive only a few million years, whereas the smallest stars, known as red dwarfs, can persist for hundreds of billions of years.

[C] When a star's hydrogen supply runs low, its core contracts and its outer layers expand dramatically, transforming it into a red giant. What happens next depends again on mass. A medium-sized star such as the Sun will eventually shed its outer layers, creating a glowing shell of gas called a planetary nebula. The remaining core, no longer producing energy, becomes a white dwarf, an extremely dense object roughly the size of the Earth that gradually cools over billions of years.

[D] Massive stars meet a far more violent end. When fusion ceases, the core collapses in less than a second, and the resulting explosion, known as a supernova, can briefly outshine an entire galaxy. Supernovae scatter heavy elements such as iron and gold into space, where they may later be incorporated into new stars and planets. Depending on the mass of the surviving core, the remnant becomes either a neutron star, in which a teaspoon of material would weigh billions of tonnes, or a black hole, whose gravity is so strong that not even light can escape. In this way, the death of one generation of stars provides the raw material for the next.

questions.q: The collapse of material in a nebula can be set off by the shockwave from a nearby exploding star.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงๆ ว่าการยุบตัวของกลุ่มก๊าซมักถูกกระตุ้นโดยคลื่นกระแทก (shockwave) จากดาวที่ระเบิดอยู่ใกล้เคียง จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: When a region of a nebula becomes sufficiently dense, often triggered by the shockwave from a nearby exploding star, gravity causes the material to collapse inward.

questions.q: Massive stars remain in the main sequence for longer than smaller stars.
questions.why_th: บทความขัดแย้งโดยตรง: ดาวมวลมากใช้เชื้อเพลิงหมดเร็วกว่าดาวเล็กมาก เพราะเผาไฮโดรเจนในอัตราที่สูงกว่า ดังนั้นข้อความนี้เป็น False
questions.evidence: Contrary to what might be expected, massive stars exhaust their fuel far more quickly than smaller ones, because they burn hydrogen at a much greater rate.

questions.q: Red dwarfs are the most common type of star in the universe.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกเพียงว่า red dwarfs เป็นดาวขนาดเล็กที่สุดและอยู่ได้นานหลายแสนล้านปี แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวถึงความ 'พบมากที่สุด' เลย จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: A white dwarf continues to generate energy after it forms.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าแกนที่เหลือ 'ไม่ผลิตพลังงานอีกต่อไป' (no longer producing energy) แล้วจึงกลายเป็น white dwarf ข้อความนี้จึงขัดแย้งกับบทความ
questions.evidence: The remaining core, no longer producing energy, becomes a white dwarf, an extremely dense object roughly the size of the Earth that gradually cools over billions of years.

questions.q: Light is unable to escape from a black hole.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวว่าแรงโน้มถ่วงของหลุมดำแรงมากจนแม้แต่แสงก็หนีออกมาไม่ได้ ตรงกับข้อความนี้ จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Depending on the mass of the surviving core, the remnant becomes either a neutron star, in which a teaspoon of material would weigh billions of tonnes, or a black hole, whose gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.

questions.q: According to the passage, nuclear fusion in a young star begins when
options: the core temperature reaches approximately 15 million degrees Celsius.
the protostar collides with a nearby star.
the nebula has lost most of its dust.
the star sheds its outer layers of gas.
questions.a: the core temperature reaches approximately 15 million degrees Celsius.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าฟิวชันเริ่มขึ้นเมื่ออุณหภูมิแกนกลางถึงประมาณ 15 ล้านองศาเซลเซียส ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ปรากฏในบทความ
questions.evidence: Once the core temperature reaches approximately 15 million degrees Celsius, nuclear fusion begins: hydrogen atoms combine to form helium, releasing enormous quantities of energy.

questions.q: What chiefly determines how long a star stays in its main sequence?
options: its mass
its distance from other stars
its colour
the size of the nebula it formed in
questions.why_th: บทความบอกชัดว่า 'The duration of this phase depends chiefly on a star's mass' คือระยะเวลาช่วง main sequence ขึ้นอยู่กับมวลของดาวเป็นหลัก
questions.evidence: The duration of this phase depends chiefly on a star's mass.

questions.q: In the passage, a planetary nebula is described as
options: a glowing shell of gas formed when a medium-sized star sheds its outer layers.
a cloud of dust in which new planets always develop.
the explosion that ends the life of a massive star.
an extremely dense object roughly the size of the Earth.
questions.a: a glowing shell of gas formed when a medium-sized star sheds its outer layers.
questions.why_th: บทความนิยาม planetary nebula ว่าเป็นเปลือกก๊าซเรืองแสงที่เกิดเมื่อดาวขนาดกลางสลัดชั้นนอกออก ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็น supernova และ white dwarf ซึ่งเป็นคนละสิ่ง
questions.evidence: A medium-sized star such as the Sun will eventually shed its outer layers, creating a glowing shell of gas called a planetary nebula.

questions.q: According to paragraph D, supernovae are important partly because they
options: scatter heavy elements that may be incorporated into new stars and planets.
prevent black holes from forming in a galaxy.
cause nearby stars to cool down more quickly.
occur only once in the lifetime of each galaxy.
questions.a: scatter heavy elements that may be incorporated into new stars and planets.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า supernova กระจายธาตุหนัก เช่น เหล็กและทอง สู่อวกาศ ซึ่งอาจกลายเป็นวัตถุดิบของดาวและดาวเคราะห์รุ่นใหม่ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในบทความ
questions.evidence: Supernovae scatter heavy elements such as iron and gold into space, where they may later be incorporated into new stars and planets.

questions.q: During the main sequence, the outward pressure generated by fusion balances the inward pull of ___, keeping the star stable.
questions.why_th: ประโยคในบทความบอกว่าแรงดันออกจากฟิวชันสมดุลกับแรงดึงเข้าของ 'gravity' (แรงโน้มถ่วง) คำตอบต้องคัดลอกจากบทความคำเดียว
questions.evidence: During this period, the outward pressure generated by fusion balances the inward pull of gravity, keeping the star stable.

questions.q: When a star's hydrogen supply runs low, its outer layers expand dramatically and it becomes a ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าเมื่อไฮโดรเจนใกล้หมด ชั้นนอกของดาวขยายตัวจนกลายเป็น 'red giant' (ดาวยักษ์แดง) ต้องตอบ 2 คำตามบทความ
questions.evidence: When a star's hydrogen supply runs low, its core contracts and its outer layers expand dramatically, transforming it into a red giant.

questions.q: A teaspoon of material from a ___ would weigh billions of tonnes.
questions.why_th: บทความบรรยายว่า neutron star (ดาวนิวตรอน) หนาแน่นมากจนวัสดุเพียงหนึ่งช้อนชาหนักหลายพันล้านตัน คำตอบคือ 2 คำจากบทความ
questions.evidence: Depending on the mass of the surviving core, the remnant becomes either a neutron star, in which a teaspoon of material would weigh billions of tonnes, or a black hole, whose gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.

title: The Puzzle of Dreaming
passage:
[A] For centuries, people have wondered why the human mind produces vivid stories during sleep. Dreams occur most frequently during a stage of sleep known as rapid eye movement, or REM, when the brain becomes almost as active as it is during waking hours. Although everyone dreams several times each night, most people forget the majority of their dreams within minutes of waking. This tendency to forget has made scientific study difficult, and even today researchers do not fully agree on why we dream at all.

[B] One influential explanation is the "activation-synthesis" theory, first proposed in the 1970s. According to this view, dreams are essentially the mind's attempt to make sense of random electrical signals generated by the lower brain during sleep. The higher regions of the brain receive these meaningless bursts of activity and weave them into a loose narrative. Supporters argue that this is why dreams often seem strange or illogical. Critics, however, point out that many dreams contain coherent emotional themes, which would be surprising if their raw material were truly random.

[C] A second group of theories focuses on the role of dreaming in memory. Experiments have shown that people who sleep after learning a new task perform better than those who stay awake. Some scientists believe that during REM sleep the brain replays and strengthens important memories, while discarding trivial information. Dreams, in this view, may be a by-product of this nightly filing process. This idea is supported by studies in which sleeping animals appeared to repeat patterns of brain activity recorded earlier while they were learning.

[D] Other researchers emphasise emotion rather than memory. They suggest that dreaming allows the mind to process difficult feelings in a safe environment, rehearsing responses to threats without real danger. This might explain why anxiety and fear are so common in dreams. Supporters of this theory note that people deprived of REM sleep often become irritable and struggle to regulate their moods, although the exact mechanism remains unclear.

[E] Despite decades of research, no single theory has been proven correct, and it is possible that dreams serve several purposes at once. What is certain is that dreaming is a universal human experience, shared across every culture and historical period. As brain-imaging technology continues to improve, scientists hope to observe the dreaming brain more directly and finally solve one of the oldest mysteries of the mind.

title: Fixing the Light: The Invention of Photography
passage:
[A] The desire to preserve images of the world is far older than photography itself. As early as the fifth century BCE, Chinese and Greek scholars described the principle of the camera obscura, a darkened chamber in which light passing through a small opening projects an inverted image of the scene outside onto the opposite wall. By the Renaissance, artists were using portable versions of the device as an aid to accurate drawing. The image it produced, however, was temporary: the moment the light changed, the picture vanished. The central challenge for early experimenters was therefore not creating an image but finding a chemical means of fixing it permanently.

[B] The first person to solve this problem was Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, a French inventor. In 1826 or 1827, he coated a pewter plate with bitumen of Judea, a light-sensitive substance, and placed it inside a camera obscura pointed at the courtyard of his country estate. After an exposure believed to have lasted at least eight hours, the plate retained a faint but permanent image, now regarded as the world's oldest surviving photograph. Niépce called his technique heliography, meaning "sun drawing".

[C] Niépce later formed a partnership with Louis Daguerre, a Parisian theatre designer, but died in 1833 before their work was complete. Daguerre continued the research alone and eventually developed a far more practical process using silver-coated copper plates treated with iodine vapour. The daguerreotype, announced publicly in 1839, reduced exposure times from hours to minutes and produced images of remarkable sharpness. The French government purchased the rights to the invention and, rather than restricting its use, presented it "free to the world", awarding Daguerre a lifetime pension in return.

[D] Meanwhile, in England, William Henry Fox Talbot had independently been experimenting with light-sensitive paper. His calotype process, patented in 1841, produced a paper negative from which unlimited positive copies could be printed. Although calotype images were softer and less detailed than daguerreotypes, the negative-positive principle proved far more significant in the long term, since it made the reproduction of photographs possible and remains the conceptual basis of film photography.

[E] The two rival processes were eventually displaced in 1851, when Frederick Scott Archer introduced the wet collodion method, which combined the sharpness of the daguerreotype with the reproducibility of the calotype. Photography rapidly became a commercial industry: portrait studios opened in cities across Europe and America, and having one's likeness recorded, once a privilege of the wealthy who could afford painted portraits, became available to the middle classes within a single generation.

questions.evidence: By the Renaissance, artists were using portable versions of the device as an aid to accurate drawing.

title: The Psychology of Colour in Marketing
topic: The psychology of colour in marketing
passage:
[A] Colour is one of the most powerful tools available to marketers, yet its influence often goes unnoticed by the very consumers it affects. Research suggests that people make a subconscious judgement about a product within ninety seconds of viewing it, and that a substantial proportion of that assessment is based on colour alone. Because colour can communicate a message far more quickly than words, companies invest considerable resources in selecting the shades that appear on their packaging, logos and advertisements.

[B] Different colours are widely believed to trigger different emotional responses. Red, for example, is frequently associated with energy, urgency and excitement, which is why it is often used in clearance sales and fast-food branding. Blue, by contrast, tends to convey trust, calmness and reliability, making it a popular choice among banks and technology firms. Green is commonly linked to nature, health and environmental responsibility, while black is often used to suggest luxury and sophistication. Marketers exploit these associations in order to shape how a brand is perceived before a single word has been read.

[C] However, the relationship between colour and consumer behaviour is more complicated than these simple pairings imply. Studies indicate that the appropriateness of a colour for a particular brand matters more than the colour itself. A shade that seems suitable for the product being sold is likely to be received more favourably than one that clashes with expectations, regardless of whether the colour is conventionally viewed as attractive. In other words, consumers respond to whether a colour fits the personality that a brand is attempting to project.

[D] Cultural background further complicates matters. The meanings attached to colours are not universal but vary considerably from one society to another. White, which signifies purity and cleanliness in many Western countries, is traditionally connected with mourning in parts of Asia. Such differences mean that a colour scheme which succeeds in one market may fail, or even cause offence, in another. Consequently, international companies must research local associations carefully before launching a product abroad.

[E] Despite the growing body of evidence, experts caution against treating colour psychology as an exact science. Individual preferences, personal experiences and even gender can all affect how a particular colour is interpreted, and no single shade produces the same reaction in everyone. What remains clear is that colour is never a neutral choice. Whether the effect is intended or accidental, the colours a company selects will inevitably influence the way its products are judged.

questions.q: Most consumers are consciously aware of how colour influences their opinion of a product.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าอิทธิพลของสีมักไม่ถูกสังเกตโดยผู้บริโภค ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'อย่างมีสติ (consciously aware)'
questions.evidence: Colour is one of the most powerful tools available to marketers, yet its influence often goes unnoticed by the very consumers it affects.

questions.evidence: Individual preferences, personal experiences and even gender can all affect how a particular colour is interpreted, and no single shade produces the same reaction in everyone.

questions.evidence: Research suggests that people make a subconscious judgement about a product within ninety seconds of viewing it, and that a substantial proportion of that assessment is based on colour alone.

questions.evidence: The meanings attached to colours are not universal but vary considerably from one society to another.

title: Threads Across Continents: The Silk Road Trade Network
passage:
[A] The Silk Road was not a single paved highway but a vast web of overland and maritime routes that connected East Asia with the Mediterranean world for more than fifteen centuries. The German geographer Ferdinand von Richthofen coined the term "Silk Road" in 1877, yet the routes themselves were active long before this label existed. Caravans of camels and horses carried goods across deserts, mountains, and grasslands, passing from one merchant to the next. Few traders travelled the entire distance; instead, goods changed hands many times, so that a single bolt of silk might pass through dozens of intermediaries before reaching its final buyer.

[B] Although the network took its name from Chinese silk, which was highly prized in Rome, silk was only one of countless commodities that moved along these routes. Spices, precious stones, glassware, wool, gold, and ceramics all travelled in both directions. Perhaps more significant than the physical goods, however, were the ideas that accompanied them. Religions such as Buddhism, Christianity, and later Islam spread along the trade routes, while technologies including papermaking and printing gradually moved westward from China. In this sense, the Silk Road functioned as a channel for cultural exchange as much as commerce.

[C] The oasis cities that punctuated the routes played an essential role in sustaining trade. Settlements such as Samarkand, Bukhara, and Kashgar provided food, water, and rest for exhausted travellers, and they grew wealthy by taxing the goods that passed through their gates. These cities became cosmopolitan centres where merchants of many languages and faiths mingled. Markets in such towns offered not only local produce but also exotic articles from distant lands, and the resulting prosperity funded impressive mosques, libraries, and palaces.

[D] The Silk Road did not decline for a single reason. From the fifteenth century onwards, the growth of maritime trade routes offered a cheaper and faster alternative to the dangerous overland journey. European ships could carry far larger cargoes than camel caravans, and sea transport avoided the numerous tolls demanded by inland states. Political fragmentation across Central Asia further disrupted the old paths. Nevertheless, the legacy of the Silk Road endured, for it had already reshaped the diets, beliefs, and technologies of societies across three continents.

questions.q: The Silk Road consisted of one main road linking East Asia and the Mediterranean.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุชัดว่าไม่ใช่ถนนเส้นเดียว แต่เป็นเครือข่ายเส้นทางจำนวนมาก จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ = False

title: The Making of Honey
passage:
[A] Honey is one of the oldest natural sweeteners known to humankind, yet the process by which honeybees produce it is remarkably complex. It begins when forager bees leave the hive in search of flowering plants. These bees are attracted to the bright colours and fragrances of flowers, from which they collect a sugary liquid called nectar. Using a long, tube-like tongue known as a proboscis, a forager draws the nectar out of the flower and stores it in a special organ called the honey stomach, which is separate from the stomach used for digestion. A single bee may visit more than a thousand flowers before this organ is full.

[B] While the nectar is held in the honey stomach, the transformation into honey has already started. Enzymes secreted by the bee begin to break down the complex sugars in the nectar into simpler sugars, mainly glucose and fructose. When the forager returns to the hive, it passes the partially processed nectar to younger house bees. These bees repeatedly swallow the liquid and bring it back up, mixing it with more enzymes each time. This exchange, which may be repeated for around twenty minutes, gradually converts the nectar into a substance closer to honey.

[C] At this stage the liquid is still too watery to be stored safely, because it contains a high proportion of water and could ferment. To solve this problem, the house bees spread the nectar in thin layers across the cells of the honeycomb. They then fan their wings vigorously over the open cells, creating a current of warm air that speeds up evaporation. This activity lowers the water content from roughly seventy per cent to under twenty per cent, producing the thick, sticky texture that is characteristic of honey.

[D] Once the honey has reached the correct consistency, the bees seal each cell with a thin cap of wax. This wax cap keeps the honey clean and prevents it from absorbing moisture from the air. Sealed in this way, honey can be kept for years without spoiling, which is why it serves as a vital food store for the colony during winter, when flowers are scarce and foraging is impossible. A strong colony may produce far more honey than it needs to survive, and it is this surplus that beekeepers are able to harvest.

questions.q: A forager bee may visit over a thousand flowers before its honey stomach is full.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงว่าผึ้งอาจไปเยือนดอกไม้มากกว่าพันดอกก่อนที่อวัยวะนี้จะเต็ม
questions.evidence: A single bee may visit more than a thousand flowers before this organ is full.

questions.q: The honey stomach is the same organ that the bee uses to digest its food.

options: To speed up the evaporation of water from the nectar

questions.evidence: This wax cap keeps the honey clean and prevents it from absorbing moisture from the air.

questions.evidence: it serves as a vital food store for the colony during winter, when flowers are scarce and foraging is impossible.

title: Finding Our Way: The Development of GPS
passage:
[A] The Global Positioning System, universally known as GPS, is a network of satellites that allows a receiver anywhere on Earth to calculate its precise location. Although it is now taken for granted in cars and mobile telephones, the system originated as a military project in the United States. Its roots lie in the launch of the Soviet satellite Sputnik in 1957. American scientists discovered that they could track the satellite by measuring the way its radio signal changed frequency as it moved. They soon realised that the reverse was also possible: if the position of a satellite were known, a listener on the ground could work out where they themselves were standing.

[B] Building on this insight, the United States Department of Defense began developing a satellite navigation system in the 1970s. The first satellite in the modern network was launched in 1978, and the full constellation of twenty-four satellites was completed in 1993. Each satellite carries an extremely accurate atomic clock and continuously broadcasts a signal containing the time and its own position. A receiver on the ground detects signals from several satellites at once and measures the tiny delay before each signal arrives. Because radio waves travel at a known speed, these delays can be converted into distances. Using the distances from at least four satellites, the receiver calculates its latitude, longitude and altitude.

[C] For many years the most accurate signals were reserved for the military, while civilian users received a deliberately degraded version. This policy, which limited ordinary accuracy to around one hundred metres, was ended in 2000, when the American government switched off the intentional error. Overnight, civilian receivers became roughly ten times more precise. This decision transformed GPS from a specialist tool into a technology for everyone, encouraging manufacturers to build cheap receivers into a huge range of consumer products.

[D] Since then, the applications of GPS have multiplied far beyond navigation. Farmers use it to guide tractors along exact lines, reducing waste of seed and fuel. Scientists rely on it to monitor the slow movement of continents and to provide early warning of earthquakes. Perhaps surprisingly, one of its most important roles is timing: banks and power networks depend on the atomic clocks aboard the satellites to keep their operations synchronised. Other countries have since built rival systems, including Europe's Galileo and China's BeiDou, yet the American network remains the standard against which the others are measured.

questions.q: GPS was first created for use by ordinary drivers and travellers.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า GPS เริ่มต้นเป็นโครงการทางทหารของสหรัฐฯ ซึ่งขัดแย้งโดยตรงกับข้อความที่ว่าสร้างมาเพื่อคนขับรถและนักเดินทางทั่วไป
questions.evidence: Although it is now taken for granted in cars and mobile telephones, the system originated as a military project in the United States.

questions.q: American scientists were able to track Sputnik by observing changes in the frequency of its radio signal.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกตรงกันว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์อเมริกันติดตามดาวเทียมได้โดยวัดการเปลี่ยนความถี่ของสัญญาณวิทยุขณะที่มันเคลื่อนที่
questions.evidence: American scientists discovered that they could track the satellite by measuring the way its radio signal changed frequency as it moved.

questions.evidence: The first satellite in the modern network was launched in 1978, and the full constellation of twenty-four satellites was completed in 1993.

questions.why_th: บทความบอกเพียงว่านาฬิกาอะตอมแม่นยำมาก แต่ไม่มีการเปรียบเทียบกับนาฬิกาบนโลกเลย จึงตอบว่าไม่มีข้อมูล

questions.why_th: บทความใช้คำว่า since (หลังจากนั้น) แสดงว่าประเทศอื่นสร้างระบบคู่แข่งขึ้นภายหลัง GPS ของอเมริกา ขัดแย้งกับคำว่า before ในโจทย์
questions.evidence: Other countries have since built rival systems, including Europe's Galileo and China's BeiDou, yet the American network remains the standard against which the others are measured.

questions.evidence: This policy, which limited ordinary accuracy to around one hundred metres, was ended in 2000, when the American government switched off the intentional error.

questions.q: Which of the following is mentioned as a use of GPS beyond navigation?
options: Providing early warning of earthquakes
Controlling the weather over farmland

questions.evidence: Scientists rely on it to monitor the slow movement of continents and to provide early warning of earthquakes.

options: It remains the standard against which the others are measured

questions.evidence: Its roots lie in the launch of the Soviet satellite Sputnik in 1957.
