questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าต้องใช้ระยะทางจากดาวเทียมอย่างน้อยสี่ดวง (four) คำนี้ปรากฏตรงตามตัวอักษรในบทความ
questions.evidence: Using the distances from at least four satellites, the receiver calculates its latitude, longitude and altitude.

questions.q: One of the most important roles of GPS is ___, which keeps banks and power networks synchronised.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าหนึ่งในบทบาทสำคัญที่สุดคือการจับเวลา (timing) ซึ่งช่วยให้ธนาคารและเครือข่ายไฟฟ้าทำงานสอดคล้องกัน
questions.evidence: Perhaps surprisingly, one of its most important roles is timing: banks and power networks depend on the atomic clocks aboard the satellites to keep their operations synchronised.

title: The Warming of Our Cities
passage:
[A] Anyone who has travelled from the countryside into a large city on a warm summer evening may have noticed that the urban air feels distinctly hotter. This difference is not merely an impression. Scientists have long documented a phenomenon known as the urban heat island, in which built-up areas record temperatures several degrees higher than the surrounding rural land. In some large cities, the gap between the city centre and nearby fields can reach as much as ten degrees Celsius after sunset. Understanding why this happens has become increasingly important as the world's population grows more urban.

[B] The principal cause of the effect lies in the materials from which cities are constructed. Concrete, brick, asphalt and stone absorb and store large quantities of solar energy during daylight hours. Unlike vegetation and soil, these hard surfaces do not lose heat quickly. Instead, they release the stored warmth slowly throughout the night, keeping the air temperature elevated long after the sun has set. Dark surfaces, such as tarred roads and roofing, absorb even more radiation than lighter ones, which intensifies the problem in densely paved districts.

[C] The shape of the city itself also plays a significant role. Tall buildings standing close together form narrow spaces sometimes called urban canyons. These structures trap heat by reducing the flow of cooling air and by reflecting radiation back and forth between their walls rather than allowing it to escape into the sky. In addition, cities generate their own heat directly. Vehicles, air-conditioning units, factories and millions of people all produce warmth, a contribution scientists refer to as anthropogenic heat.

[D] A further factor is the relative absence of vegetation. In rural areas, plants and trees cool the surrounding air through a process called evapotranspiration, in which water evaporates from leaves and soil, drawing heat away. Cities, with their limited greenery, lose this natural cooling mechanism. Rainwater, moreover, is quickly channelled away through drains rather than being retained to evaporate later, further reducing the moderating effect that moisture would normally provide.

[E] The consequences of urban heat islands are considerable. Higher temperatures increase the demand for air conditioning, which in turn consumes more electricity and releases yet more heat, creating a damaging cycle. During heatwaves, the effect can pose serious health risks, particularly for the elderly. Fortunately, planners have identified several remedies. Planting trees, creating parks, installing reflective or "green" roofs covered with vegetation, and using lighter-coloured building materials can all help to lower urban temperatures and make cities more comfortable places to live.

questions.q: After sunset, the temperature in some city centres can be up to ten degrees Celsius higher than in the surrounding countryside.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงว่าในเมืองใหญ่บางแห่ง ช่องว่างอุณหภูมิระหว่างใจกลางเมืองกับทุ่งนาใกล้เคียงอาจสูงถึงสิบองศาเซลเซียส 'หลังพระอาทิตย์ตก' ตรงกับโจทย์ทุกประเด็น จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: In some large cities, the gap between the city centre and nearby fields can reach as much as ten degrees Celsius after sunset.

questions.q: Vegetation and soil retain heat for longer than hard urban surfaces do.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าพื้นผิวแข็งของเมืองไม่ปล่อยความร้อนเร็ว 'ต่างจาก' พืชและดิน หมายความว่าพืชและดินสูญเสียความร้อนเร็วกว่า ไม่ใช่เก็บไว้นานกว่า โจทย์จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความโดยตรง เป็น False
questions.evidence: Unlike vegetation and soil, these hard surfaces do not lose heat quickly.

questions.q: Dark surfaces absorb more solar radiation than light-coloured ones.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงว่าพื้นผิวสีเข้ม เช่น ถนนลาดยางและหลังคา ดูดซับรังสีมากกว่าพื้นผิวสีอ่อน จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Dark surfaces, such as tarred roads and roofing, absorb even more radiation than lighter ones, which intensifies the problem in densely paved districts.

questions.q: Urban heat islands cause more deaths among the elderly than among any other group.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกเพียงว่าคลื่นความร้อนเสี่ยงต่อสุขภาพ 'โดยเฉพาะผู้สูงอายุ' แต่ไม่ได้พูดถึงจำนวนการเสียชีวิต และไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบระหว่างกลุ่มคนเลย จึงไม่มีข้อมูลเพียงพอ เป็น Not Given

questions.q: Air conditioning helps to reduce the overall amount of heat in cities.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าเครื่องปรับอากาศใช้ไฟฟ้ามากขึ้นและ 'ปล่อยความร้อนออกมามากขึ้น' จนเกิดวงจรที่เลวร้าย จึงขัดแย้งกับโจทย์ที่ว่าช่วยลดความร้อนโดยรวม เป็น False
questions.evidence: Higher temperatures increase the demand for air conditioning, which in turn consumes more electricity and releases yet more heat, creating a damaging cycle.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the main reason cities stay warm at night?
questions.a: Building materials release stored heat slowly during the night.
options: Building materials release stored heat slowly during the night.
People continue to use vehicles throughout the night.
Rural areas send warm air towards the city.
The sky reflects heat back down onto the city.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่า 'สาเหตุหลัก' คือวัสดุก่อสร้างที่ดูดซับพลังงานตอนกลางวันแล้วปล่อยความร้อนออกมาช้า ๆ ตลอดคืน ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้ถูกระบุว่าเป็นสาเหตุหลักหรือไม่ปรากฏในข้อความเลย
questions.evidence: Instead, they release the stored warmth slowly throughout the night, keeping the air temperature elevated long after the sun has set.

questions.q: What effect do tall buildings standing close together have?
questions.a: They reduce the flow of cooling air and reflect radiation between their walls.
options: They reduce the flow of cooling air and reflect radiation between their walls.
They provide shade that lowers street-level temperatures.
They channel rainwater more efficiently into drains.
They increase evapotranspiration in the surrounding area.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] อธิบายว่าอาคารสูงที่ตั้งชิดกันดักความร้อนโดยลดการไหลของอากาศเย็นและสะท้อนรังสีไปมาระหว่างผนัง ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ปรากฏหรือขัดกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: These structures trap heat by reducing the flow of cooling air and by reflecting radiation back and forth between their walls rather than allowing it to escape into the sky.

questions.q: Why do rural areas benefit from a cooling effect that cities lack?
questions.a: Plants and trees draw heat away as water evaporates from leaves and soil.
options: Plants and trees draw heat away as water evaporates from leaves and soil.
Rural buildings are made from lighter-coloured materials.
The countryside has fewer vehicles producing heat.
Rural drains retain rainwater for longer periods.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุว่าในชนบท พืชและต้นไม้ทำให้อากาศเย็นผ่านกระบวนการคายระเหย (evapotranspiration) ซึ่งน้ำระเหยจากใบและดินแล้วดึงความร้อนออกไป ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้ถูกกล่าวถึงว่าเป็นเหตุผลนี้
questions.evidence: In rural areas, plants and trees cool the surrounding air through a process called evapotranspiration, in which water evaporates from leaves and soil, drawing heat away.

questions.q: Which of the following is mentioned as a remedy for urban heat islands?
questions.a: Installing reflective or vegetated roofs on buildings.
options: Installing reflective or vegetated roofs on buildings.
Widening urban canyons between tall buildings.
Reducing the population of city centres.
Banning air-conditioning units in summer.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [E] ระบุวิธีแก้ไขไว้ชัดเจน ได้แก่ ปลูกต้นไม้ สร้างสวน ติดตั้งหลังคาสะท้อนแสงหรือหลังคาเขียวที่ปกคลุมด้วยพืช ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ปรากฏในข้อความ
questions.evidence: Planting trees, creating parks, installing reflective or "green" roofs covered with vegetation, and using lighter-coloured building materials can all help to lower urban temperatures and make cities more comfortable places to live.

questions.q: Heat produced directly by vehicles, factories and people is called ___ heat.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์เรียกความร้อนที่เมืองสร้างขึ้นเองจากยานพาหนะ โรงงาน และผู้คนว่า anthropogenic heat คำตอบปรากฏตรงตัวในข้อความ
questions.evidence: Vehicles, air-conditioning units, factories and millions of people all produce warmth, a contribution scientists refer to as anthropogenic heat.

questions.q: The narrow spaces formed between tall buildings standing close together are sometimes called urban ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] เรียกช่องว่างแคบระหว่างอาคารสูงที่ตั้งชิดกันว่า urban canyons คำตอบปรากฏตรงตัวในข้อความ
questions.evidence: Tall buildings standing close together form narrow spaces sometimes called urban canyons.

questions.q: In rural areas, plants cool the air through a process known as ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] เรียกกระบวนการที่พืชและต้นไม้ทำให้อากาศเย็นลงว่า evapotranspiration คำตอบปรากฏตรงตัวในข้อความ
questions.evidence: In rural areas, plants and trees cool the surrounding air through a process called evapotranspiration, in which water evaporates from leaves and soil, drawing heat away.

title: From Bitter Bean to Sweet Bar: The History of Chocolate
passage:
[A] Chocolate is derived from the seeds of the cacao tree, which grows naturally in the tropical forests of Central and South America. Long before it reached Europe, the peoples of Mesoamerica valued these seeds highly. The Maya and, later, the Aztecs roasted and ground the beans to produce a thick, bitter drink that was flavoured with spices such as chilli and vanilla. This beverage was reserved mainly for rulers, priests and warriors, and it was consumed during important religious ceremonies. The Aztecs also used cacao beans as a form of currency, exchanging them for goods in local markets.

[B] European contact with chocolate began in the sixteenth century, when Spanish explorers encountered the drink in the Aztec empire. At first, many Europeans found the bitter taste unpleasant. However, once sugar and honey were added, the drink became increasingly popular among the wealthy classes of Spain. For almost a century, Spain managed to keep chocolate a closely guarded secret, but eventually knowledge of it spread to other European courts. By the seventeenth century, fashionable chocolate houses had opened in cities such as London, where people gathered to drink chocolate and discuss the news of the day.

[C] For most of its history, chocolate remained a drink rather than a solid food. This changed dramatically during the Industrial Revolution. In 1828, a Dutch chemist named Coenraad van Houten invented a press that could separate cocoa butter from the roasted beans, producing a fine powder that mixed easily with water. This process, later known as "Dutching", made chocolate cheaper and smoother. A few decades afterwards, manufacturers learned to combine cocoa butter, cocoa powder and sugar to create the first solid eating chocolate.

[D] The final major development was the invention of milk chocolate. In 1875, the Swiss manufacturer Daniel Peter, working with his neighbour Henri Nestlé, succeeded in adding condensed milk to chocolate, creating a milder and creamier product. Swiss firms soon dominated the industry, and chocolate was transformed from a luxury enjoyed by the few into an affordable treat available to ordinary consumers across the world.

questions.q: The cacao tree grows naturally in the tropical forests of Central and South America.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงว่าต้นคาเคาเติบโตตามธรรมชาติในป่าเขตร้อนของอเมริกากลางและอเมริกาใต้

questions.evidence: This beverage was reserved mainly for rulers, priests and warriors, and it was consumed during important religious ceremonies.

options: True

questions.evidence: For almost a century, Spain managed to keep chocolate a closely guarded secret, but eventually knowledge of it spread to other European courts.

questions.a: It separated cocoa butter from the roasted beans to make a fine powder.
options: It added condensed milk to chocolate for the first time.
It produced the first bar of solid eating chocolate.

questions.evidence: In 1828, a Dutch chemist named Coenraad van Houten invented a press that could separate cocoa butter from the roasted beans, producing a fine powder that mixed easily with water.

questions.evidence: Swiss firms soon dominated the industry, and chocolate was transformed from a luxury enjoyed by the few into an affordable treat available to ordinary consumers across the world.

questions.evidence: The Aztecs also used cacao beans as a form of currency, exchanging them for goods in local markets.

questions.evidence: In 1875, the Swiss manufacturer Daniel Peter, working with his neighbour Henri Nestlé, succeeded in adding condensed milk to chocolate, creating a milder and creamier product.

title: How Animals Sleep
passage:
[A] Sleep is a behaviour observed across almost the entire animal kingdom, yet the way different species sleep varies enormously. While humans typically rest in a single block of around eight hours during the night, many animals divide their sleep into numerous short episodes spread throughout the day and night. The total amount of sleep also differs dramatically from one species to another. A brown bat, for example, may sleep for nearly twenty hours in a single day, whereas a giraffe often manages with less than two. Scientists believe these differences are shaped largely by an animal's diet, its size, and the constant threat of predators.

[B] One of the most remarkable adaptations is the ability of certain animals to sleep with only half of the brain at a time, a phenomenon known as unihemispheric sleep. Dolphins and some whales rely on this strategy because they must return to the water's surface to breathe. By resting one hemisphere while keeping the other alert, these marine mammals can continue swimming and remain aware of danger. Many migratory birds use a similar technique, allowing them to remain airborne for days without landing. The eye connected to the active half of the brain stays open, watching for obstacles and threats.

[C] The risk of being eaten strongly influences how and where animals sleep. Prey species that live in open habitats tend to sleep for shorter periods and wake frequently, ready to flee at the first sign of danger. Large predators, by contrast, face few threats and can afford to sleep deeply for long stretches. A lion may rest for up to twenty hours after a large meal. Location matters too: many birds sleep while perched high in trees, and some primates build fresh nests each evening in the safety of the branches.

[D] Environmental conditions add a further layer of variation. During periods of extreme cold or food shortage, some mammals enter hibernation, a prolonged state of reduced activity that is not identical to ordinary sleep but shares certain features with it. Body temperature falls sharply and the heartbeat slows to conserve energy. Researchers continue to debate the precise purpose of sleep, but most agree it plays an essential role in restoring the body and consolidating memory. What remains clear is that, despite the countless forms it takes, sleep is far too widespread among animals to be dismissed as unimportant.

questions.why_th: ค้างคาวนอนเกือบ 20 ชม. ส่วนยีราฟน้อยกว่า 2 ชม. จึงเป็นจริง

questions.evidence: During periods of extreme cold or food shortage, some mammals enter hibernation, a prolonged state of reduced activity that is not identical to ordinary sleep but shares certain features with it.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do dolphins use unihemispheric sleep?
questions.a: They need to keep swimming and surface to breathe.

They cannot close both of their eyes at once.

questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่านักล่าใหญ่เผชิญภัยน้อยจึงนอนลึกได้นาน
questions.evidence: Large predators, by contrast, face few threats and can afford to sleep deeply for long stretches.
options: They can sleep deeply for long periods because they face few threats.
They sleep in short episodes to stay alert for prey.

questions.a: Diet, body size, and the threat of predators.

questions.evidence: Scientists believe these differences are shaped largely by an animal's diet, its size, and the constant threat of predators.
options: Diet, body size, and the threat of predators.
Temperature, water depth, and daylight.

questions.evidence: Researchers continue to debate the precise purpose of sleep, but most agree it plays an essential role in restoring the body and consolidating memory.
options: It helps restore the body and consolidate memory.
It is mainly a way of avoiding predators.
It exists only in large mammals.

title: The Economics of Tipping
passage:
[A] Few economic behaviours puzzle researchers as much as tipping. Standard economic theory assumes that consumers act in their own financial interest, yet every year millions of people voluntarily hand over money to waiters, taxi drivers and hotel staff whom they may never meet again. In the United States, tips paid in restaurants alone are estimated to exceed forty billion dollars annually. The custom, however, is far from universal: while American diners typically add fifteen to twenty per cent to their bills, tipping is modest in much of Europe and can even be regarded as insulting in Japan.

[B] The practice has aristocratic origins. Historians trace tipping to sixteenth-century England, where guests staying in private homes were expected to give small sums, known as vails, to their hosts' servants. The habit crossed the Atlantic after the American Civil War, when wealthy travellers returning from Europe imitated the custom to display their sophistication. Ironically, most European countries later moved away from heavy tipping by including service charges in published prices, whereas the United States retained and expanded the practice. Several American states banned tipping in the early twentieth century, but the laws proved impossible to enforce and were repealed.

[C] Economists have proposed several explanations for why the custom survives. The most common argument is that tipping functions as a monitoring device: because customers observe the quality of service directly, they are better placed than managers to reward good performance. The evidence for this is weak, however. Research shows that the connection between service quality and tip size is small, and that the amount left depends far more on the size of the bill. Nor can self-interest fully explain the behaviour, since many people tip generously in restaurants and taxis in cities they are visiting for the first and only time.

[D] Many researchers therefore conclude that tipping is driven less by economics than by psychology. Diners who leave without tipping commonly report feelings of guilt and embarrassment, suggesting that the custom is maintained by social pressure rather than by calculation. In this view, a tip is the price people pay to avoid disapproval — from waiters, from dining companions and even from themselves.

[E] The system nonetheless has serious critics. In parts of the United States, employers may pay tipped staff a basic wage of just $2.13 an hour, leaving workers dependent on the generosity of strangers and exposed to unpredictable incomes. Studies have also found that tips are influenced by a server's appearance, raising concerns about fairness. A number of restaurants have experimented with abolishing tips and raising menu prices instead, yet several returned to the traditional model after resistance from both customers and staff, a sign of how deeply the habit is embedded in commercial culture.

questions.q: American diners generally add between fifteen and twenty per cent to their restaurant bills.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงกันว่าลูกค้าอเมริกันมักให้ทิป 15-20 เปอร์เซ็นต์ของบิล (typically add fifteen to twenty per cent) จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: The custom, however, is far from universal: while American diners typically add fifteen to twenty per cent to their bills, tipping is modest in much of Europe and can even be regarded as insulting in Japan.

title: Power from Falling Water: How Dams Generate Electricity
passage:
[A] Hydroelectric power is one of the oldest and most widely used forms of renewable energy. The world's first hydroelectric power station began operating in 1882 on the Fox River in Wisconsin, in the United States, and today hydropower supplies roughly sixteen per cent of the world's electricity. Unlike coal or gas plants, hydroelectric dams produce power without burning fuel, which means they release very little carbon dioxide during operation.

[B] The principle behind hydroelectric generation is relatively straightforward. A dam is built across a river, creating a large artificial lake known as a reservoir. Water held in the reservoir possesses potential energy because of its height above the river below. When electricity is needed, gates in the dam open and water flows through a long, sloping pipe called a penstock. As the water descends, its potential energy is converted into kinetic energy, and it strikes the blades of a turbine, causing them to spin. The turbine is connected by a shaft to a generator, where rotating magnets inside coils of copper wire produce an electric current. The electricity then passes through a transformer, which raises its voltage so that it can travel efficiently along power lines to homes and factories.

[C] The amount of power a dam can generate depends on two main factors: the volume of water flowing through the turbines and the vertical distance the water falls, which is known as the head. A dam with a high head can produce considerable power even from a modest flow of water. Some facilities, called pumped-storage plants, take this idea further. During the night, when demand for electricity is low, they use cheap surplus power to pump water from a lower reservoir back up to a higher one. During the day, when demand rises, the water is released again to generate electricity, effectively storing energy like an enormous battery.

[D] Hydroelectric dams offer several advantages beyond clean generation. Because operators can start and stop the flow of water within minutes, hydropower can respond quickly to sudden increases in demand. Reservoirs also provide water for irrigation and help to control flooding downstream. However, the technology is not without drawbacks. Building a dam floods large areas of land, forcing communities to relocate and destroying natural habitats. Dams also block the migration routes of fish such as salmon, and the quality of water released downstream can be altered, affecting river ecosystems for many kilometres.

questions.q: The world's first hydroelectric power station was located in the United States.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าโรงไฟฟ้าพลังน้ำแห่งแรกของโลกเริ่มเดินเครื่องในปี 1882 ที่แม่น้ำ Fox รัฐ Wisconsin ประเทศสหรัฐอเมริกา จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: The world's first hydroelectric power station began operating in 1882 on the Fox River in Wisconsin, in the United States, and today hydropower supplies roughly sixteen per cent of the world's electricity.

questions.q: Hydroelectric dams release large amounts of carbon dioxide while they operate.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าเขื่อนปล่อยคาร์บอนไดออกไซด์ 'น้อยมาก' ระหว่างการทำงาน ซึ่งขัดแย้งโดยตรงกับคำว่า 'ปริมาณมาก' ในโจทย์ จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: Unlike coal or gas plants, hydroelectric dams produce power without burning fuel, which means they release very little carbon dioxide during operation.

questions.q: Pumped-storage plants move water to a higher reservoir when demand for electricity is low.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าในเวลากลางคืนซึ่งความต้องการไฟฟ้าต่ำ โรงไฟฟ้าแบบสูบกลับจะใช้ไฟฟ้าส่วนเกินราคาถูกสูบน้ำขึ้นไปยังอ่างเก็บน้ำด้านบน ตรงกับโจทย์ จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: During the night, when demand for electricity is low, they use cheap surplus power to pump water from a lower reservoir back up to a higher one.

questions.q: The Fox River power station is still producing electricity today.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกเพียงว่าโรงไฟฟ้าที่แม่น้ำ Fox เริ่มเดินเครื่องในปี 1882 แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวว่าปัจจุบันยังผลิตไฟฟ้าอยู่หรือไม่ จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: Hydropower stations need several hours to respond to a sudden rise in electricity demand.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าผู้ควบคุมสามารถเปิด-ปิดการไหลของน้ำได้ภายในไม่กี่นาที ทำให้ตอบสนองความต้องการที่เพิ่มขึ้นได้อย่างรวดเร็ว ขัดแย้งกับ 'หลายชั่วโมง' จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: Because operators can start and stop the flow of water within minutes, hydropower can respond quickly to sudden increases in demand.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is a penstock?
options: a long, sloping pipe that water flows through
a device that raises the voltage of electricity
a wall that blocks the migration routes of fish
a machine that stores surplus electricity at night
questions.a: a long, sloping pipe that water flows through
questions.why_th: บทความนิยาม penstock ว่าเป็นท่อยาวลาดเอียงที่น้ำไหลผ่านเมื่อประตูเขื่อนเปิด ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นหมายถึงหม้อแปลง เขื่อน และโรงไฟฟ้าสูบกลับตามลำดับ
questions.evidence: When electricity is needed, gates in the dam open and water flows through a long, sloping pipe called a penstock.

questions.q: Which piece of equipment raises the voltage of the electricity before it travels along power lines?
options: the turbine
the generator

questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าไฟฟ้าผ่าน transformer ซึ่งเพิ่มแรงดันไฟฟ้าเพื่อให้ส่งไปตามสายไฟได้อย่างมีประสิทธิภาพ ไม่ใช่กังหัน เครื่องกำเนิดไฟฟ้า หรือท่อ penstock
questions.evidence: The electricity then passes through a transformer, which raises its voltage so that it can travel efficiently along power lines to homes and factories.

questions.q: The amount of power a dam can generate depends on the volume of water and
options: the vertical distance the water falls
the temperature of the water in the reservoir

questions.a: the vertical distance the water falls
questions.why_th: บทความระบุปัจจัยหลักสองอย่างคือปริมาตรน้ำที่ไหลผ่านกังหันและระยะทางแนวดิ่งที่น้ำตกลงมา (เรียกว่า head) ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้ถูกกล่าวถึงว่าเป็นปัจจัย
questions.evidence: The amount of power a dam can generate depends on two main factors: the volume of water flowing through the turbines and the vertical distance the water falls, which is known as the head.

questions.q: Why do pumped-storage plants release water from the higher reservoir during the day?
options: because demand for electricity rises
because the reservoir would otherwise overflow
to provide water for irrigation downstream
to allow fish such as salmon to migrate
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าในเวลากลางวันเมื่อความต้องการไฟฟ้าเพิ่มขึ้น น้ำจะถูกปล่อยลงมาเพื่อผลิตไฟฟ้าอีกครั้ง ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่เกี่ยวข้องกับโรงไฟฟ้าสูบกลับ
questions.evidence: During the day, when demand rises, the water is released again to generate electricity, effectively storing energy like an enormous battery.

questions.q: Water held in the reservoir possesses ___ energy because of its height above the river below.
questions.why_th: ประโยคในบทความระบุตรงตัวว่าน้ำในอ่างเก็บน้ำมี 'potential energy' เพราะอยู่สูงกว่าแม่น้ำด้านล่าง คำตอบคือ potential
questions.evidence: Water held in the reservoir possesses potential energy because of its height above the river below.

questions.q: The vertical distance the water falls is known as the ___.
questions.why_th: บทความให้คำจำกัดความว่าระยะทางแนวดิ่งที่น้ำตกลงมาเรียกว่า 'the head' คำตอบคือ head
questions.evidence: The amount of power a dam can generate depends on two main factors: the volume of water flowing through the turbines and the vertical distance the water falls, which is known as the head.

questions.q: Inside the generator, rotating magnets inside coils of ___ produce an electric current.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าแม่เหล็กที่หมุนอยู่ภายในขดลวด 'copper wire' เป็นตัวสร้างกระแสไฟฟ้าในเครื่องกำเนิดไฟฟ้า คำตอบคือ copper wire (2 คำ)
questions.evidence: The turbine is connected by a shaft to a generator, where rotating magnets inside coils of copper wire produce an electric current.

title: The Culture of Tea Ceremonies
passage:
[A] Few beverages have inspired as much ritual and reverence as tea. Although tea drinking began in China several thousand years ago, the practice of preparing and serving it according to formal rules developed gradually. During the Tang dynasty, the scholar Lu Yu wrote The Classic of Tea, an eighth-century manual that described the correct methods of cultivation and preparation. His work transformed tea from a simple drink into a subject of aesthetic and philosophical interest, and it laid the foundations for ceremonial tea culture across East Asia.

[B] The most famous tea ceremony is arguably the Japanese chanoyu, which was carried to Japan by Buddhist monks and later refined in the sixteenth century by the tea master Sen no Rikyu. Rikyu emphasised four principles: harmony, respect, purity and tranquillity. Every element of the ceremony, from the arrangement of flowers to the cleaning of utensils, is designed to express these values. Guests traditionally enter the tea room through a deliberately low doorway, which obliges them to bow and thus reminds them that all participants are equal, regardless of social rank.

[C] China developed a rather different tradition known as gongfu cha, which is particularly associated with the provinces of Fujian and Guangdong. In this practice, tea is brewed in small clay pots and poured into tiny cups, and the same leaves are infused many times. Unlike the Japanese ceremony, gongfu cha places greater emphasis on the taste and aroma of the tea itself than on strict ritual procedure, although hosts still take considerable pride in their skill.

[D] Formal tea customs are not confined to Asia. In Britain, afternoon tea emerged in the 1840s, popularly credited to Anna, the Duchess of Bedford, who wished to bridge the long gap between lunch and a late evening dinner. In Morocco, sweet mint tea is poured from a height into small glasses and offered to guests three times; refusing a glass is widely considered impolite.

[E] Today, tea ceremonies face an uncertain future. Younger generations in Japan and China often regard the traditional forms as time-consuming, and the number of qualified instructors has fallen. At the same time, however, international interest has grown: in 2022, UNESCO added Chinese traditional tea processing to its list of intangible cultural heritage, and tea schools now attract students from around the world who value the ceremonies as a form of mindfulness in a hurried age.

questions.q: Lu Yu's book explained how tea should be grown and prepared.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าหนังสือ The Classic of Tea อธิบายวิธีการเพาะปลูกและการชงชาที่ถูกต้อง จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: During the Tang dynasty, the scholar Lu Yu wrote The Classic of Tea, an eighth-century manual that described the correct methods of cultivation and preparation.

questions.q: In gongfu cha, tea leaves are used only once before being thrown away.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าใบชาเดิมถูกชงซ้ำหลายครั้ง (infused many times) ซึ่งขัดแย้งโดยตรงกับข้อความนี้ จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: In this practice, tea is brewed in small clay pots and poured into tiny cups, and the same leaves are infused many times.

questions.evidence: In Morocco, sweet mint tea is poured from a height into small glasses and offered to guests three times; refusing a glass is widely considered impolite.

questions.q: The number of qualified tea ceremony instructors in Japan and China has risen in recent years.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าจำนวนผู้สอนที่มีคุณวุฒิ 'ลดลง' (has fallen) ตรงข้ามกับคำว่าเพิ่มขึ้นในโจทย์ จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: Younger generations in Japan and China often regard the traditional forms as time-consuming, and the number of qualified instructors has fallen.

questions.q: According to the passage, why is the doorway of the Japanese tea room deliberately low?

It keeps the warmth of the tea room inside

questions.a: It obliges guests to bow, reminding them that all participants are equal
questions.why_th: ประตูเตี้ยบังคับให้แขกต้องก้มตัว เพื่อเตือนว่าทุกคนเท่าเทียมกันไม่ว่าจะมีฐานะทางสังคมอย่างไร
questions.evidence: Guests traditionally enter the tea room through a deliberately low doorway, which obliges them to bow and thus reminds them that all participants are equal, regardless of social rank.

questions.q: How does gongfu cha differ from the Japanese tea ceremony, according to the passage?
options: It focuses more on the taste and aroma of the tea than on strict ritual
It follows more rigid procedures than chanoyu

questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่า gongfu cha ให้ความสำคัญกับรสชาติและกลิ่นของชามากกว่าขั้นตอนพิธีที่เคร่งครัด ต่างจากพิธีของญี่ปุ่น
questions.evidence: Unlike the Japanese ceremony, gongfu cha places greater emphasis on the taste and aroma of the tea itself than on strict ritual procedure, although hosts still take considerable pride in their skill.

questions.q: Why, according to the passage, did Anna, the Duchess of Bedford, introduce afternoon tea?
options: She wished to fill the long gap between lunch and a late evening dinner
She wanted to imitate Asian tea ceremonies
She hoped to promote British tea plantations
She intended to impress foreign visitors
questions.a: She wished to fill the long gap between lunch and a late evening dinner
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าดัชเชสแห่งเบดฟอร์ดต้องการเชื่อมช่องว่างยาวระหว่างมื้อกลางวันกับมื้อเย็นที่กินช้า
questions.evidence: In Britain, afternoon tea emerged in the 1840s, popularly credited to Anna, the Duchess of Bedford, who wished to bridge the long gap between lunch and a late evening dinner.

questions.q: What does the passage say happened in 2022?
options: Chinese traditional tea processing was added to UNESCO's intangible cultural heritage list
Japan opened its first international tea school
The number of tea instructors began to rise again

title: Living Light in the Deep Sea
passage:
[A] In the darkest regions of the ocean, where sunlight never reaches, many creatures produce their own light through a process known as bioluminescence. This ability is remarkably common in the deep sea. Scientists estimate that more than three-quarters of the animals living below a depth of 200 metres are capable of generating light. The glow is created by a chemical reaction in which a molecule called luciferin reacts with oxygen. Unlike the light produced by an ordinary bulb, this reaction gives off almost no heat, and for this reason it is often described as "cold light".

[B] The colours that deep-sea animals produce are limited. The vast majority emit blue or blue-green light, which travels furthest through seawater. A small number of species, however, generate red light. Because most deep-sea predators cannot see red, an animal that produces it can illuminate its surroundings without being noticed. Some organisms make their own light directly, while others rely on colonies of luminous bacteria that live inside special organs. In return for a safe home and a steady supply of nutrients, these bacteria provide their host with a reliable source of light.

[C] Bioluminescence serves a variety of purposes. One of the most important is defence. When threatened, certain squid release a cloud of glowing fluid that confuses an attacker, allowing the squid to escape into the darkness. Other animals use their light to attract prey. The anglerfish, for instance, dangles a glowing lure in front of its mouth, drawing curious smaller fish towards it. Light is also used to communicate, particularly to locate a mate in the vast emptiness of the deep, where individuals may rarely encounter one another.

[D] Studying these organisms is difficult and expensive. The deep sea is cold, completely dark, and subject to crushing pressure, and specimens frequently lose their ability to glow when brought to the surface. To overcome this, researchers increasingly use remotely operated vehicles fitted with sensitive cameras to observe the animals in their natural environment. Such research has practical value beyond biology. The molecules responsible for bioluminescence have been adapted by medical scientists as markers to track the spread of disease within living tissue, demonstrating that the study of these remote creatures can yield unexpected benefits for human health.

questions.q: Bioluminescence is found in the majority of animals living below 200 metres.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่ามากกว่าสามในสี่ของสัตว์ที่อยู่ลึกกว่า 200 เมตรสามารถสร้างแสงได้ ซึ่งคือส่วนใหญ่
questions.evidence: Scientists estimate that more than three-quarters of the animals living below a depth of 200 metres are capable of generating light.

title: Carrying the Message: A History of the Postal Service
topic: The history of the postal service
passage:
[A] The desire to send written messages across distances is almost as old as writing itself. Some of the earliest organised systems appeared in ancient empires, where rulers needed reliable ways to govern territories that stretched far beyond their capitals. The Persian Empire, around the fifth century BCE, established a network of mounted couriers who carried royal correspondence between stations positioned a day's ride apart. Fresh horses and riders waited at each station, allowing messages to travel with remarkable speed. The Roman Empire later developed a comparable service known as the cursus publicus, which used a system of roads and relay posts to move official documents throughout its vast provinces.

[B] For many centuries, however, such services were reserved almost exclusively for governments and the wealthy. Ordinary people had little access to formal postal routes, and letters were often carried privately by travelling merchants, pilgrims or servants. During the medieval period, universities and trading associations created their own messenger systems to serve their particular needs. It was not until the growth of towns and commerce in the later Middle Ages that the demand for a more general postal service began to increase significantly across Europe.

[C] A major turning point came in nineteenth-century Britain. Before 1840, the cost of sending a letter was calculated according to the distance travelled and the number of sheets used, and, crucially, it was usually paid by the recipient rather than the sender. This arrangement was expensive and discouraged many people from using the post at all. A reformer named Rowland Hill argued that a single low charge, paid in advance by the sender, would make the system fairer and ultimately more profitable. His ideas led to the introduction of the Penny Post and the world's first adhesive postage stamp, the Penny Black, in 1840.

[D] The reform had dramatic consequences. The volume of letters posted in Britain rose sharply within a few years, and other nations soon adopted similar systems of prepaid postage. As international mail expanded, the need for cooperation between countries became obvious. In 1874, representatives of several states signed an agreement that created what would become the Universal Postal Union, standardising the rules for exchanging mail across borders. This organisation helped transform the post from a collection of separate national services into a genuinely global network, laying the foundations for the interconnected communications systems that followed.

questions.q: The Persian courier system used the same horses for the entire length of a journey.

questions.evidence: Fresh horses and riders waited at each station, allowing messages to travel with remarkable speed.

questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกชัดว่าใช้ระบบถนนและสถานีถ่ายทอด

title: The Science of Fingerprints
passage:
[A] Fingerprints are the fine ridges of skin that cover the tips of human fingers. They begin to form before birth, developing during the early months of pregnancy as the outer layer of skin folds against the tissues beneath it. The exact pattern that results depends on a combination of genetic instructions and small, unpredictable pressures inside the womb. For this reason, even identical twins, who share the same genes, are born with fingerprints that differ in detail. Once formed, a person's ridge patterns remain essentially unchanged throughout life, growing only in size as the individual matures.

[B] Scientists group fingerprint patterns into three broad types. The most common is the loop, in which the ridges enter from one side, curve around, and exit on the same side. Whorls form circular or spiral shapes, while arches rise in a wave-like curve across the finger without looping back. Although these categories are useful for sorting large collections of prints, identification does not rely on the general pattern alone. Instead, examiners study tiny features known as minutiae, such as the points where a ridge divides in two or comes to a sudden end. It is the arrangement of these details that makes each print unique.

[C] The use of fingerprints for identification has a long history. Ancient societies sometimes pressed a finger into clay or wax to mark ownership of goods, though they may not have fully understood why the marks were reliable. Systematic study began in the nineteenth century. In the 1890s, the British official Edward Henry developed a classification system that allowed prints to be filed and retrieved efficiently, and versions of this method were soon adopted by police forces around the world. For decades afterwards, matching prints remained a slow task carried out entirely by hand.

[D] Modern technology has transformed the process. Computerised databases can now compare a print against millions of stored records in a matter of seconds, greatly reducing the time needed to find a possible match. Nevertheless, the final decision is still made by a trained human examiner, because automated systems occasionally suggest matches that turn out to be incorrect. Fingerprints have also found uses far beyond criminal investigation. Many smartphones and secure buildings now use fingerprint scanners to control access, relying on the same basic principle that no two people share exactly the same ridges.

questions.q: Fingerprints continue to develop new patterns after a person is born.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าลายนิ้วมือคงที่ตลอดชีวิต โตขึ้นเพียงขนาดเท่านั้น ไม่เกิดลายใหม่ จึงขัดแย้ง = False

title: Building for a Warming World
passage:
[A] Sustainable architecture is an approach to design that seeks to reduce the environmental damage caused by buildings throughout their lifetime. Construction and the operation of buildings together account for a substantial share of global energy use and greenhouse gas emissions. In response, architects increasingly aim to minimise the resources a structure consumes, from the materials used to erect it to the electricity required to heat, cool and light it once occupied. The central goal is to create buildings that remain comfortable for their users while placing a far lighter burden on the planet.

[B] One of the oldest and most reliable strategies is known as passive design. Rather than depending on mechanical systems, passive design uses the natural features of a site to regulate temperature. In cooler climates, large windows are positioned on the sunlit side of a building so that winter sunshine warms the interior, a technique called passive solar heating. In hot regions, by contrast, deep roof overhangs and shaded courtyards keep the strongest sunlight out. Thick walls made from materials such as stone or rammed earth absorb heat during the day and release it slowly at night, smoothing out temperature swings without any energy input at all.

[C] The choice of materials also matters a great deal. Every product carries what is termed embodied energy, meaning the total energy consumed in extracting, processing and transporting it before it reaches the building site. Concrete and steel, though strong and widely available, have a very high embodied energy, whereas timber from responsibly managed forests stores carbon and requires comparatively little processing. For this reason, many designers now favour locally sourced and recycled materials, which cut transport distances and reduce the demand for newly manufactured products.

[D] Technology has added further options. Green roofs, planted with grasses and other vegetation, insulate the structure beneath them and absorb rainwater that would otherwise overwhelm urban drains. Solar panels generate electricity on site, and advanced glazing allows daylight to enter while limiting heat loss. A small number of ambitious projects now aim to be entirely self-sufficient in energy, producing as much power as they consume across a year. Such buildings are sometimes described as net-zero, and although they remain expensive to construct, their running costs are extremely low.

[E] Critics point out that sustainable buildings can cost more at the outset, and that some environmental claims are exaggerated for marketing purposes, a practice known as greenwashing. Nevertheless, supporters argue that the long-term savings and reduced emissions justify the initial investment. As cities continue to expand and the climate warms, the principles of sustainable architecture are likely to move from the margins to the mainstream of the profession.

questions.q: Buildings are responsible for a significant proportion of the world's energy consumption.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าการก่อสร้างและการใช้งานอาคารรวมกันคิดเป็นสัดส่วนมากของการใช้พลังงานทั่วโลก

questions.q: Passive design relies mainly on mechanical systems to control indoor temperature.

questions.evidence: Rather than depending on mechanical systems, passive design uses the natural features of a site to regulate temperature.

questions.q: Timber from well-managed forests requires less processing than steel.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าไม้ต้องการการแปรรูปน้อยเมื่อเทียบกับเหล็กและคอนกรีตที่มี embodied energy สูง
questions.evidence: Concrete and steel, though strong and widely available, have a very high embodied energy, whereas timber from responsibly managed forests stores carbon and requires comparatively little processing.

questions.q: What is the central goal of sustainable architecture according to the passage?

To replace all traditional building materials with steel

questions.q: In hot regions, which feature is used to keep the strongest sunlight out?

options: The electricity a building uses for heating and cooling

questions.evidence: Critics point out that sustainable buildings can cost more at the outset, and that some environmental claims are exaggerated for marketing purposes, a practice known as greenwashing.

title: The Domestication of Cats
passage:
[A] The domestic cat, known scientifically as Felis catus, is one of the most popular companion animals in the world today. Yet its relationship with humans is comparatively recent when measured against that of the dog, which was tamed many thousands of years earlier. For a long time, scholars believed that cats were first domesticated in ancient Egypt around 4,000 years ago. However, more recent genetic and archaeological research has pushed this date considerably further back and shifted the location westward, to the Near East.

[B] The wild ancestor of every modern house cat is the African wildcat, Felis silvestris lybica, a solitary hunter native to the deserts and grasslands of the Middle East and North Africa. Unlike many other wildcats, this subspecies was unusually tolerant of human presence. The turning point came with the rise of agriculture. As people in the Fertile Crescent began to store surplus grain in permanent settlements roughly 10,000 years ago, these stores attracted large numbers of mice and rats. The wildcats, in turn, were drawn to this abundant supply of prey. In this way, cats were not deliberately captured and bred by humans; instead, they effectively domesticated themselves by moving closer to human communities.

[C] Compelling evidence for this early association was uncovered on the Mediterranean island of Cyprus. Archaeologists discovered the remains of a cat buried alongside a human in a grave dating back around 9,500 years. Because Cyprus had no native wildcats, the animal must have been brought there by boat, suggesting that a bond between the two species already existed. Egyptian civilisation later elevated the cat to a position of great importance. Cats were associated with the goddess Bastet, protected by law, and sometimes mummified after death.

[D] Despite thousands of years of living beside people, cats have changed remarkably little compared with other domesticated animals. Their bodies remain close in size and shape to those of their wild relatives, and they have retained much of their independent, self-reliant behaviour. Scientists note that domestication has altered the temperament of cats far more than their physical appearance. Modern selective breeding, which has produced the many distinctive pedigree breeds now seen at cat shows, is a very recent development, dating back only about 150 years. For most of their shared history with humans, cats were valued not as ornaments but for the practical service of controlling pests.

questions.q: Dogs were domesticated by humans before cats were.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าความสัมพันธ์ของแมวกับมนุษย์เกิดขึ้นทีหลังสุนัข ซึ่งถูกทำให้เชื่องก่อนหลายพันปี
questions.evidence: Yet its relationship with humans is comparatively recent when measured against that of the dog, which was tamed many thousands of years earlier.

questions.q: Recent research confirms that cats were first domesticated in ancient Egypt.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่างานวิจัยใหม่ผลักวันเวลาให้เก่ากว่าเดิมและย้ายสถานที่ไปทางตะวันตกสู่ตะวันออกใกล้ ขัดกับความเชื่อเรื่องอียิปต์
questions.evidence: However, more recent genetic and archaeological research has pushed this date considerably further back and shifted the location westward, to the Near East.

questions.q: The African wildcat was more accepting of humans than many other wildcats.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าสายพันธุ์ย่อยนี้ทนต่อการมีมนุษย์อยู่ใกล้ได้อย่างผิดปกติเมื่อเทียบกับแมวป่าอื่น
questions.evidence: Unlike many other wildcats, this subspecies was unusually tolerant of human presence.

questions.q: The cat found in the grave on Cyprus was larger than a typical wildcat.

questions.q: The physical appearance of cats has changed more than their temperament through domestication.
questions.why_th: ข้อความกล่าวตรงกันข้ามว่า การทำให้เชื่องเปลี่ยนนิสัยของแมวมากกว่ารูปลักษณ์ภายนอก
questions.evidence: Scientists note that domestication has altered the temperament of cats far more than their physical appearance.

questions.why_th: ข้อความอธิบายว่ายุ้งเก็บธัญพืชดึงดูดหนู และแมวป่าถูกดึงดูดด้วยเหยื่อที่อุดมสมบูรณ์นี้
questions.evidence: The wildcats, in turn, were drawn to this abundant supply of prey.

questions.a: Cyprus had no native wildcats, so the cat must have been transported there
options: Cyprus had no native wildcats, so the cat must have been transported there
The cat had been carefully mummified before burial

questions.evidence: Because Cyprus had no native wildcats, the animal must have been brought there by boat, suggesting that a bond between the two species already existed.

considered unimportant working animals

questions.evidence: Cats were associated with the goddess Bastet, protected by law, and sometimes mummified after death.

questions.q: What does the passage say about modern pedigree cat breeds?
questions.a: They result from selective breeding that is only about 150 years old
options: They result from selective breeding that is only about 150 years old
They differ greatly in body size from wild cats

questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าการผสมพันธุ์แบบคัดเลือกที่สร้างสายพันธุ์แท้เป็นสิ่งใหม่มาก ย้อนไปเพียงราว 150 ปี
questions.evidence: Modern selective breeding, which has produced the many distinctive pedigree breeds now seen at cat shows, is a very recent development, dating back only about 150 years.

questions.q: The wild ancestor of the house cat is the African wildcat, a ___ hunter of deserts and grasslands.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบรรยายบรรพบุรุษของแมวบ้านว่าเป็นนักล่าที่อยู่ตามลำพัง (solitary)
questions.evidence: The wild ancestor of every modern house cat is the African wildcat, Felis silvestris lybica, a solitary hunter native to the deserts and grasslands of the Middle East and North Africa.

questions.q: Because they moved closer to human communities on their own, cats effectively ___ themselves.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าแมวทำให้ตัวเองเชื่อง (domesticated themselves) โดยเข้าใกล้ชุมชนมนุษย์
questions.evidence: instead, they effectively domesticated themselves by moving closer to human communities.

questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าแมวมีคุณค่าเพราะบริการเชิงปฏิบัติในการควบคุมสัตว์รบกวน (controlling pests)
questions.evidence: For most of their shared history with humans, cats were valued not as ornaments but for the practical service of controlling pests.

title: Why Choosing Becomes Harder as the Day Wears On
passage:
[A] Every day, an adult makes thousands of decisions, ranging from the trivial to the profoundly consequential. Psychologists have long assumed that the quality of these decisions depends mainly on a person's intelligence and knowledge. However, research over the past two decades has revealed a more troubling pattern. The mental capacity required to make sound choices appears to be a limited resource that becomes depleted through use. This phenomenon, known as decision fatigue, suggests that the more decisions a person makes, the poorer their subsequent choices tend to be.

[B] One of the most frequently cited studies was conducted in Israel and examined the rulings of parole judges. Researchers found that prisoners who appeared before a judge early in the morning were granted parole in roughly 65 per cent of cases. As the morning progressed, the approval rate fell steadily, approaching zero just before the lunch break. After the judges had eaten and rested, the rate returned to its original level. The study's authors argued that a tired mind gravitates towards the safest and least demanding option, which in this context meant simply denying the request and leaving the prisoner in custody.

[C] The mechanism behind decision fatigue is thought to involve the brain's consumption of glucose, the sugar that fuels mental activity. When people are required to make repeated choices, their self-control weakens, and they become increasingly likely to act impulsively or to avoid deciding altogether. Retailers understand this tendency well. Supermarkets deliberately position sweets and magazines beside the checkout, knowing that shoppers who have already made numerous decisions while walking through the aisles are more prone to unplanned purchases.

[D] Some prominent figures have attempted to minimise the effect by reducing the number of trivial decisions they face. Certain business leaders famously wear almost identical clothing every day, reserving their mental energy for matters they consider important. While such strategies may seem extreme, the underlying principle is supported by evidence. Establishing routines, making significant decisions earlier in the day, and taking regular breaks can all help preserve the mental resources on which good judgement depends. Understanding decision fatigue does not eliminate it, but it does allow individuals to arrange their lives so that important choices are made when the mind is at its sharpest.

questions.q: Psychologists have traditionally believed that decision quality is primarily determined by intelligence and knowledge.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุชัดเจนว่านักจิตวิทยาเชื่อกันมานานว่าคุณภาพของการตัดสินใจขึ้นอยู่กับสติปัญญาและความรู้เป็นหลัก ซึ่งตรงกับข้อความในคำถาม จึงเป็น True
questions.evidence: Psychologists have long assumed that the quality of these decisions depends mainly on a person's intelligence and knowledge.

questions.q: In the Israeli study, the parole approval rate stayed constant throughout the entire morning.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่าอัตราการอนุมัติค่อย ๆ ลดลงจนเกือบเป็นศูนย์ก่อนพักกลางวัน ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'คงที่' โดยตรง จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: As the morning progressed, the approval rate fell steadily, approaching zero just before the lunch break.

questions.q: The parole judges in the study were aware that the time of day was influencing their decisions.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความบรรยายเพียงพฤติกรรมการตัดสินของผู้พิพากษา แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวเลยว่าพวกเขารู้ตัวหรือไม่ว่าถูกอิทธิพลของช่วงเวลาส่งผล จึงไม่มีข้อมูลเพียงพอที่จะสรุป เป็น Not Given

questions.q: Making repeated choices tends to strengthen a person's self-control.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุว่าเมื่อคนต้องตัดสินใจซ้ำ ๆ การควบคุมตนเองจะ 'อ่อนลง' ไม่ใช่แข็งแรงขึ้น จึงขัดแย้งกับคำถามโดยตรง เป็น False
questions.evidence: When people are required to make repeated choices, their self-control weakens, and they become increasingly likely to act impulsively or to avoid deciding altogether.

questions.q: Understanding decision fatigue can completely remove its effects.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุชัดว่าการเข้าใจ decision fatigue 'ไม่ได้ขจัดมันให้หมดไป' เพียงแต่ช่วยให้จัดชีวิตได้ดีขึ้น จึงขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'completely remove' เป็น False
questions.evidence: Understanding decision fatigue does not eliminate it, but it does allow individuals to arrange their lives so that important choices are made when the mind is at its sharpest.

questions.q: According to the passage, decision fatigue is best described as:
questions.a: a limited mental resource that becomes depleted through use
options: a limited mental resource that becomes depleted through use
a permanent decline in intelligence caused by ageing
a lack of knowledge about the choices being made
an emotional reaction to making difficult decisions
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] อธิบายว่าความสามารถทางจิตใจในการตัดสินใจเป็นทรัพยากรที่มีจำกัดและถูกใช้จนหมดไป ตรงกับตัวเลือกที่ถูก ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นเรื่องอายุ ความรู้ และอารมณ์ ไม่ได้ถูกกล่าวถึง
questions.evidence: The mental capacity required to make sound choices appears to be a limited resource that becomes depleted through use.

questions.q: What did the authors of the parole study conclude about a tired mind?
questions.a: It tends to choose the safest and least demanding option
options: It tends to choose the safest and least demanding option
It becomes more generous towards prisoners
It makes decisions more quickly but more fairly
It relies more heavily on written evidence
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่าจิตใจที่เหนื่อยล้าจะโน้มไปเลือกทางที่ปลอดภัยและใช้ความพยายามน้อยที่สุด ตรงกับตัวเลือกที่ถูก ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับเนื้อความที่ว่าผู้พิพากษามักปฏิเสธคำร้อง
questions.evidence: The study's authors argued that a tired mind gravitates towards the safest and least demanding option, which in this context meant simply denying the request and leaving the prisoner in custody.

questions.q: Why do supermarkets place sweets and magazines near the checkout?
questions.a: Because shoppers who have made many decisions are more likely to buy on impulse
options: Because shoppers who have made many decisions are more likely to buy on impulse
Because these items are the most profitable products in the store
Because customers prefer to see them before entering the aisles
Because they help customers make healthier food choices
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] อธิบายว่าผู้ซื้อที่ตัดสินใจมามากแล้วมีแนวโน้มซื้อของแบบไม่ได้วางแผน ร้านจึงวางสินค้าเหล่านี้ไว้ตรงจุดชำระเงิน ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นข้อมูลที่ไม่มีในบทความ
questions.evidence: Supermarkets deliberately position sweets and magazines beside the checkout, knowing that shoppers who have already made numerous decisions while walking through the aisles are more prone to unplanned purchases.

questions.q: According to the final paragraph, which strategy is NOT mentioned as a way to preserve mental resources?
questions.a: Eating foods that are high in glucose
options: Eating foods that are high in glucose
Establishing routines
Making significant decisions earlier in the day
Taking regular breaks
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] กล่าวถึงสามกลยุทธ์ ได้แก่ การสร้างกิจวัตร การตัดสินใจสำคัญตอนต้นวัน และการพักเป็นระยะ แต่ไม่ได้กล่าวถึงการกินอาหารที่มีน้ำตาลสูงเป็นกลยุทธ์ จึงเป็นคำตอบที่ถูก (สิ่งที่ไม่ได้ระบุ)
questions.evidence: Establishing routines, making significant decisions earlier in the day, and taking regular breaks can all help preserve the mental resources on which good judgement depends.

questions.q: The mechanism behind decision fatigue is believed to involve the brain's consumption of ___, the sugar that fuels mental activity.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ระบุคำว่า glucose ตรง ๆ ว่าเป็นน้ำตาลที่หล่อเลี้ยงกิจกรรมของสมอง คำตอบปรากฏตามตัวอักษรและอยู่ในขอบเขตคำที่กำหนด (1 คำ)
questions.evidence: The mechanism behind decision fatigue is thought to involve the brain's consumption of glucose, the sugar that fuels mental activity.

questions.q: Some business leaders wear almost identical clothing every day in order to reserve their mental ___ for important matters.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ใช้วลี mental energy เมื่อกล่าวถึงผู้นำธุรกิจที่แต่งกายเหมือนเดิมทุกวัน คำที่เติมคือ energy ปรากฏตามตัวอักษรและอยู่ในขอบเขต 1 คำ

title: Owning Less, Sharing More: The Economics of Access
topic: The economics of the sharing economy
passage:
[A] The phrase "sharing economy" describes a range of economic activity in which individuals rent out, lend or exchange goods and services directly with one another, usually through digital platforms. Rather than purchasing a product outright, consumers pay for temporary access to it. A power drill, for instance, is used for only a few minutes across its entire lifetime, yet most households that own one have paid the full purchase price. Economists describe such items as having "idle capacity", and the central promise of the sharing economy is to unlock the value trapped in these under-used assets.

[B] Digital platforms are essential to this model because they dramatically reduce what economists call transaction costs. In earlier decades, a person wishing to lend a spare room to a stranger faced enormous difficulties in advertising the room, agreeing a price and establishing trust. Online marketplaces solve these problems by matching supply with demand instantly and by using rating systems that allow strangers to assess one another's reliability. The largest accommodation platform now lists more rooms than any traditional hotel chain, despite owning no property itself.

[C] Supporters argue that the model brings clear economic benefits. It allows ordinary people to earn additional income from possessions they already own, and it can lower prices for consumers by increasing the supply of available goods. There are environmental advantages too: if several households share a single vehicle, fewer cars need to be manufactured, reducing the consumption of raw materials. For these reasons, some commentators have hailed the sharing economy as a more efficient and sustainable form of capitalism.

[D] Critics, however, point to significant drawbacks. Much of the work generated by these platforms is insecure, offering neither guaranteed hours nor the protections that traditional employees enjoy. Workers are typically classified as independent contractors, which means companies avoid paying for sick leave or pensions. There are wider social concerns as well: in popular tourist cities, the conversion of long-term housing into short-term rentals has been blamed for pushing up rents for local residents. Regulators in several countries have consequently introduced limits on how many nights a property may be rented each year.

[E] The long-term impact of the sharing economy remains uncertain. Some analysts believe it represents a genuine shift away from ownership towards access, particularly among younger consumers who place less value on possessing physical goods. Others suspect that the largest platforms, having grown rapidly by avoiding the costs borne by conventional businesses, will eventually be regulated so heavily that they come to resemble the very industries they once disrupted.

title: Windows on the Infant Cosmos: The James Webb Space Telescope
topic: How the James Webb telescope sees the early universe
passage:
[A] The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), launched in December 2021, was designed to observe some of the earliest galaxies that formed after the Big Bang. Because light travels at a finite speed, telescopes function as instruments of time: the more distant an object, the further back in cosmic history astronomers are effectively looking. Light from the most remote galaxies has taken well over thirteen billion years to reach us, meaning that JWST captures these systems as they appeared when the universe was young. In this sense, the observatory does not merely photograph space; it records the past.

[B] A central challenge in observing such ancient light is a phenomenon known as redshift. As the universe expands, the wavelengths of light travelling across it are stretched, shifting from the visible part of the spectrum towards longer, redder wavelengths. For the earliest galaxies, this stretching is so pronounced that their light arrives at Earth almost entirely as infrared radiation. Earlier observatories such as the Hubble Space Telescope, which was optimised primarily for visible and ultraviolet light, could not detect these faint infrared signals effectively. JWST was therefore built as an infrared instrument, allowing it to perceive wavelengths that were previously beyond reach.

[C] To register such faint heat radiation, the telescope itself must remain extraordinarily cold. Any warmth from the instrument would emit infrared light of its own, overwhelming the delicate signals from distant galaxies. For this reason, JWST is equipped with a five-layer sunshield, roughly the size of a tennis court, which blocks radiation from the Sun, Earth and Moon. This shield keeps the telescope's mirror and detectors at temperatures below minus 220 degrees Celsius. The observatory also operates far from Earth, at a gravitationally stable location approximately 1.5 million kilometres away, where thermal conditions remain steady.

[D] The scientific rewards of this design have been considerable. Within months of beginning operations, JWST identified candidate galaxies that appear to have formed only a few hundred million years after the Big Bang, earlier than many theoretical models had predicted. Some of these galaxies seem surprisingly bright and massive for their age, prompting astronomers to reconsider how quickly the first structures assembled. While such findings remain subject to confirmation, they demonstrate that the telescope is reshaping understanding of the cosmic dawn, the era in which stars and galaxies first illuminated a previously dark universe.

title: The Sleeping Brain and the Making of Memory
topic: Sleep and the consolidation of memory
passage:
[A] For much of the twentieth century, sleep was regarded as a period of passive rest during which the brain simply shut down to recover from the demands of the day. Modern neuroscience has overturned this view. Far from being dormant, the sleeping brain is intensely active, and one of its most important functions is the consolidation of memory, the process by which fragile, newly formed memories are stabilised and transformed into more durable representations. Experiments have repeatedly shown that people who sleep after learning a task retain the information far better than those who remain awake for an equivalent period, even when total waking time is carefully matched.

[B] Sleep is not a uniform state but cycles through distinct stages, broadly divided into rapid eye movement (REM) sleep and non-REM (NREM) sleep. Researchers have found that different types of memory appear to benefit from different stages. Slow-wave sleep, the deepest phase of NREM sleep, is strongly associated with the consolidation of declarative memories, which include facts and events that can be consciously recalled. REM sleep, by contrast, has been linked more closely to procedural memories, such as motor skills, and to the integration of emotional experiences. This division, however, is not absolute, and many scientists now argue that the two stages cooperate rather than compete.

[C] A leading explanation for how consolidation occurs is the active systems consolidation hypothesis. During slow-wave sleep, the hippocampus, a structure critical for forming new memories, is thought to replay patterns of neural activity that were generated during learning. This replay occurs at high speed and repeatedly reactivates the relevant networks. Through this process, memories are gradually transferred from the hippocampus, where they are initially stored, to the neocortex, where they can be retained over the long term. The reactivation is accompanied by characteristic electrical events, including slow oscillations and brief bursts of activity known as sleep spindles.

[D] The practical implications of this research are considerable. Studies suggest that a short daytime nap containing slow-wave sleep can improve later recall, and that pulling an all-nighter before an examination may be counterproductive. Some researchers have even attempted to strengthen specific memories during sleep by replaying sounds or smells that were present during learning, a technique known as targeted memory reactivation. While the field is still developing, the accumulating evidence has firmly established that a good night's sleep is not a luxury but a biological necessity for a well-functioning memory.

options: The hippocampus replays neural patterns from learning and transfers memories to the neocortex

The brain forms entirely new memories without prior learning
The hippocampus and neocortex both stop communicating
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] อธิบายว่า hippocampus เล่นซ้ำรูปแบบกิจกรรมประสาทจากการเรียนรู้ แล้วค่อย ๆ ถ่ายโอนความจำไปยัง neocortex ตรงกับตัวเลือกที่ถูก
questions.evidence: Through this process, memories are gradually transferred from the hippocampus, where they are initially stored, to the neocortex, where they can be retained over the long term.

questions.q: Which of the following does the passage present as a practical implication of sleep research?
questions.a: Staying awake all night before an exam may be counterproductive
options: Staying awake all night before an exam may be counterproductive

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [D] ระบุว่าการอดนอนทั้งคืนก่อนสอบอาจให้ผลตรงกันข้ามกับที่ต้องการ ซึ่งเป็นนัยเชิงปฏิบัติของงานวิจัย
questions.evidence: Studies suggest that a short daytime nap containing slow-wave sleep can improve later recall, and that pulling an all-nighter before an examination may be counterproductive.

questions.a: The two stages cooperate rather than compete
options: The two stages cooperate rather than compete
One stage is entirely useless for memory

The stages are identical in function
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [B] ระบุว่านักวิทยาศาสตร์จำนวนมากในปัจจุบันมองว่าสองช่วงนี้ 'ทำงานร่วมกันมากกว่าจะแข่งกัน'
questions.evidence: This division, however, is not absolute, and many scientists now argue that the two stages cooperate rather than compete.

questions.q: The process by which newly formed memories are stabilised and made more durable is called the ___ of memory.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ใช้วลี 'consolidation of memory' เพื่ออธิบายกระบวนการทำให้ความจำที่เพิ่งก่อตัวมีความมั่นคงและคงทนขึ้น
questions.evidence: one of its most important functions is the consolidation of memory, the process by which fragile, newly formed memories are stabilised and transformed into more durable representations

questions.q: During slow-wave sleep the hippocampus is thought to ___ patterns of neural activity generated during learning.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] ใช้คำว่า 'replay' โดยตรง เพื่ออธิบายว่า hippocampus เล่นซ้ำรูปแบบกิจกรรมประสาทที่เกิดขึ้นตอนเรียนรู้
questions.evidence: the hippocampus, a structure critical for forming new memories, is thought to replay patterns of neural activity that were generated during learning

questions.q: Brief bursts of electrical activity that accompany reactivation during sleep are known as sleep ___.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [C] เรียกช่วงกิจกรรมไฟฟ้าสั้น ๆ ที่มากับการ reactivation ว่า 'sleep spindles' คำที่เติมในช่องว่างคือ spindles
questions.evidence: characteristic electrical events, including slow oscillations and brief bursts of activity known as sleep spindles

title: The Power of Expectation: Understanding the Placebo Effect
passage:
[A] The placebo effect is one of the most intriguing phenomena in modern medicine. A placebo is an inactive substance or treatment, such as a sugar pill or a saline injection, that contains no active ingredient capable of producing a physiological change. Yet patients who receive placebos frequently report genuine improvements in their symptoms. For decades, researchers dismissed such responses as imaginary or as evidence of weak-willed patients. Today, however, the placebo effect is recognised as a real, measurable biological process, and it has become a serious subject of scientific investigation.

[B] Central to the placebo effect is the role of expectation. When a patient believes that a treatment will work, the brain appears to prepare the body for improvement. Neuroimaging studies have shown that taking a placebo can trigger the release of endorphins, the body's natural painkillers, and dopamine, a chemical associated with reward and motivation. In one influential experiment, patients given a placebo painkiller showed activity in the same brain regions that respond to genuine opioid drugs. This suggests that the anticipation of relief can itself set in motion measurable chemical changes.

[C] The strength of the placebo response is shaped by a range of factors. The manner in which a treatment is delivered matters considerably: a warm, confident doctor tends to produce stronger effects than a distant or hurried one. Surprisingly, the physical characteristics of the placebo also play a part. Larger pills are often perceived as more potent than smaller ones, and injections generally produce a greater response than tablets. Colour can influence outcomes too, with red and orange pills tending to be associated with stimulating effects and blue pills with calming ones.

[D] The placebo effect is not equally powerful across all medical conditions. It is most pronounced in conditions involving subjective symptoms, such as pain, anxiety, fatigue and depression, where the patient's perception plays a large role. It has little or no influence on the underlying course of diseases such as cancer, although it may ease the discomfort that accompanies them. Interestingly, research has revealed that placebos can sometimes work even when patients are openly told that they are receiving an inactive treatment, a finding that continues to puzzle scientists.

[E] Understanding the placebo effect has important practical consequences. In clinical trials, new drugs must be tested against placebos to prove that their benefits exceed those produced by expectation alone. Beyond the laboratory, the phenomenon reminds physicians that the psychological dimension of care, including reassurance and empathy, can meaningfully affect how patients feel. Rather than being a nuisance to be eliminated, the placebo effect is increasingly viewed as a resource that thoughtful medical practice might harness.

questions.q: Researchers have always regarded the placebo effect as a genuine biological process.
questions.why_th: เดิมนักวิจัยเคยมองว่าเป็นเรื่องจินตนาการ (dismissed as imaginary) แล้วปัจจุบันจึงยอมรับว่าเป็นกระบวนการทางชีววิทยาที่แท้จริง ข้อความจึงขัดแย้งกับคำว่า 'always'
questions.evidence: For decades, researchers dismissed such responses as imaginary or as evidence of weak-willed patients.

questions.q: Taking a placebo can cause the brain to release endorphins.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ระบุตรงตัวว่าการรับยาหลอกกระตุ้นการหลั่งสาร endorphins
questions.evidence: Neuroimaging studies have shown that taking a placebo can trigger the release of endorphins, the body's natural painkillers, and dopamine, a chemical associated with reward and motivation.

questions.q: Injections tend to produce a stronger placebo response than tablets.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุชัดเจนว่าการฉีดยาให้ผลตอบสนองมากกว่าเม็ดยา
questions.evidence: injections generally produce a greater response than tablets.

questions.q: Blue pills are generally associated with stimulating effects.
questions.why_th: ข้อความขัดแย้ง เพราะยาเม็ดสีน้ำเงินสัมพันธ์กับผลทำให้สงบ (calming) ส่วนสีแดงและส้มต่างหากที่สัมพันธ์กับผลกระตุ้น
questions.evidence: with red and orange pills tending to be associated with stimulating effects and blue pills with calming ones.

questions.q: The placebo effect can cure cancer if the patient's belief is strong enough.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุว่ายาหลอกแทบไม่มีผลต่อการดำเนินโรคอย่างมะเร็ง จึงขัดแย้งกับคำกล่าวที่ว่ายาหลอกสามารถ 'รักษามะเร็งให้หายได้' ทำให้เป็น False
questions.evidence: It has little or no influence on the underlying course of diseases such as cancer, although it may ease the discomfort that accompanies them.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does the release of dopamine during a placebo response indicate?
questions.a: a chemical associated with reward and motivation
options: a chemical associated with reward and motivation
a substance that directly cures physical disease
a hormone that regulates the immune system
a painkiller stronger than any opioid drug
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B อธิบาย dopamine ว่าเป็นสารที่เกี่ยวข้องกับรางวัลและแรงจูงใจ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในเนื้อหา
questions.evidence: dopamine, a chemical associated with reward and motivation.

questions.q: Which factor is said to strengthen the placebo response?
questions.a: a warm and confident manner from the doctor
options: a warm and confident manner from the doctor
a doctor who is distant and hurried
