questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าแพทย์ที่อบอุ่นและมั่นใจให้ผลแรงกว่าแพทย์ที่ห่างเหินหรือรีบเร่ง
questions.evidence: a warm, confident doctor tends to produce stronger effects than a distant or hurried one.

questions.q: For which type of condition is the placebo effect most pronounced?
questions.a: conditions involving subjective symptoms such as pain and anxiety
options: conditions involving subjective symptoms such as pain and anxiety
conditions such as cancer that follow a fixed course
bacterial infections requiring antibiotics
broken bones and physical injuries
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ระบุว่าได้ผลชัดเจนที่สุดกับอาการเชิงอัตวิสัย เช่น ความเจ็บปวดและความวิตกกังวล
questions.evidence: It is most pronounced in conditions involving subjective symptoms, such as pain, anxiety, fatigue and depression, where the patient's perception plays a large role.

questions.q: Why must new drugs be tested against placebos in clinical trials?
questions.a: to prove that their benefits exceed those produced by expectation alone
options: to prove that their benefits exceed those produced by expectation alone
to reduce the cost of manufacturing the drugs
to make patients feel more comfortable during testing
to eliminate the need for a confident doctor
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า E อธิบายว่าต้องทดสอบเทียบกับยาหลอกเพื่อพิสูจน์ว่าผลดีของยาเกินกว่าที่เกิดจากความคาดหวังเพียงอย่างเดียว
questions.evidence: new drugs must be tested against placebos to prove that their benefits exceed those produced by expectation alone.

questions.evidence: Central to the placebo effect is the role of expectation.

questions.evidence: Larger pills are often perceived as more potent than smaller ones,

questions.q: Rather than being a nuisance to be eliminated, the placebo effect is increasingly seen as a ___ that medical practice might harness.
questions.why_th: ประโยคสุดท้ายของย่อหน้า E ใช้คำว่า 'resource' ตรงตัว
questions.evidence: the placebo effect is increasingly viewed as a resource that thoughtful medical practice might harness.

title: Paying for the Shift to Clean Power
topic: The economics of renewable energy transition
passage:
[A] The global movement away from fossil fuels towards renewable sources such as solar and wind is frequently framed as an environmental imperative, yet at its core it is an economic transformation of remarkable scale. For much of the twentieth century, electricity generated from coal and gas was substantially cheaper than that produced by any renewable technology. This price gap discouraged investment in cleaner alternatives and reinforced the dominance of established energy providers. Over the past fifteen years, however, the cost dynamics have shifted dramatically, altering the calculations of governments, corporations and consumers alike.

[B] The principal driver of this shift has been the falling price of the technology itself. Between 2010 and 2020, the cost of electricity from large-scale solar installations declined by roughly eighty-five per cent, while onshore wind became almost half as expensive. Economists attribute these reductions to economies of scale, improved manufacturing techniques and the accumulated experience of a maturing industry. As production volumes rose, the price per unit fell in a predictable pattern that analysts describe as a learning curve. In many regions, newly built solar and wind farms can now generate power more cheaply than newly built coal plants, a threshold that would have seemed implausible two decades ago.

[C] Nevertheless, the transition presents genuine economic difficulties that low headline prices can obscure. Unlike conventional power stations, renewable sources are intermittent: solar panels produce nothing at night and wind turbines fall idle in calm weather. Balancing supply and demand therefore requires costly investment in energy storage and in the transmission networks that carry electricity across long distances. There are also social costs. Communities whose livelihoods depend on coal mining or oil extraction may face unemployment as these industries contract, and the burden of retraining and regional support often falls upon national governments.

[D] Financing the transition remains a formidable challenge, particularly in developing economies where access to affordable capital is limited. Building renewable infrastructure demands large sums of money upfront, even though the fuel itself is effectively free once the equipment is operating. Wealthier nations have used subsidies, carbon pricing and public investment to accelerate the process, but such instruments are not universally available. Many economists argue that the long-term savings and the avoided costs of climate damage justify the initial expenditure, though the distribution of these benefits across generations and regions continues to provoke debate.

questions.q: During most of the twentieth century, renewable electricity was more expensive than electricity from coal and gas.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุชัดเจนว่าตลอดเกือบทั้งศตวรรษที่ 20 ไฟฟ้าจากถ่านหินและก๊าซถูกกว่าพลังงานหมุนเวียนอย่างมาก ซึ่งเท่ากับว่าพลังงานหมุนเวียนแพงกว่า ข้อความนี้จึงเป็นจริง
questions.evidence: For much of the twentieth century, electricity generated from coal and gas was substantially cheaper than that produced by any renewable technology.

questions.q: The cost of electricity from large-scale solar installations fell by about eighty-five per cent between 2010 and 2020.

False
Not Given

title: Reading the Warning Signs: Forecasting Volcanic Eruptions
topic: The science of volcanic eruption forecasting
passage:
[A] Volcanic eruptions are among the most destructive natural events on Earth, yet unlike earthquakes, which strike with almost no warning, volcanoes frequently announce their intentions in advance. In the days, weeks or even months before an eruption, molten rock known as magma begins to rise towards the surface, disturbing the ground above it. Scientists who study these disturbances, called volcanologists, have developed a range of techniques to interpret them. Their goal is not to predict the exact hour of an eruption, which remains impossible, but to estimate the probability that one will occur within a given period, allowing authorities to evacuate populations before disaster strikes.

[B] The most reliable indicator is seismic activity. As magma forces its way upward, it fractures surrounding rock and generates small earthquakes. Networks of instruments called seismometers record these tremors, and a sudden increase in their frequency often signals that an eruption is approaching. A particular pattern known as volcanic tremor, a continuous vibration rather than a series of sharp shocks, is regarded as an especially serious sign. However, seismic signals alone can be misleading, because magma sometimes rises and then cools without ever reaching the surface, producing what scientists call a failed eruption.

[C] For this reason, volcanologists combine seismic data with other measurements. One valuable method is the monitoring of ground deformation. When magma accumulates beneath a volcano, the surface may swell outward by a few centimetres, and modern satellite radar can detect such movements with remarkable precision. Another approach involves analysing the gases released from a volcano. Rising magma emits sulphur dioxide and carbon dioxide, and changes in the quantity or composition of these emissions can reveal how close the magma has come to the surface. Increases in ground temperature, measured by thermal cameras, provide further clues.

[D] Despite these advances, forecasting remains an uncertain science. Each volcano behaves differently, and a signal that reliably precedes an eruption at one site may mean nothing at another. Reliable forecasting depends on a long historical record of a volcano's behaviour, yet many of the world's most dangerous volcanoes have never been closely monitored. In poorer regions, the necessary equipment is often simply unaffordable. Perhaps the greatest challenge is not scientific but social: even an accurate warning is useless if local communities do not trust it or refuse to leave their homes. For this reason, effective forecasting increasingly depends on cooperation between scientists and the public.

questions.q: Volcanic eruptions generally give less warning than earthquakes do.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุตรงกันข้าม คือ แผ่นดินไหวเกิดขึ้นโดยแทบไม่มีสัญญาณเตือน ขณะที่ภูเขาไฟมักส่งสัญญาณล่วงหน้า ดังนั้นภูเขาไฟจึงเตือนล่วงหน้ามากกว่า ไม่ใช่น้อยกว่า จึงเป็น False
questions.evidence: unlike earthquakes, which strike with almost no warning, volcanoes frequently announce their intentions in advance

questions.q: Scientists are now able to predict the exact time at which a volcano will erupt.
options: True

Not Given

questions.evidence: Their goal is not to predict the exact hour of an eruption, which remains impossible

questions.q: Volcanic tremor is considered a more worrying signal than a series of sharp shocks.
options: True

title: Teaching Machines to See
topic: How artificial intelligence learns to recognise images
passage:
[A] When a person glances at a photograph, recognising a cat or a bicycle feels effortless and immediate. For a computer, however, an image is nothing more than a grid of numbers, each representing the brightness or colour of a single point known as a pixel. Teaching a machine to interpret these numbers as meaningful objects has been one of the central challenges of artificial intelligence. The dominant modern solution is a type of program called a neural network, loosely inspired by the way neurons are connected in the human brain. Although the biological comparison is often overstated, the analogy has proved a useful starting point for engineers.

[B] A neural network learns not by being given fixed rules, but through exposure to large quantities of labelled examples. Researchers assemble enormous collections of pictures, each tagged by humans with a description of its contents. The network is shown an image and produces a guess; if the guess is wrong, an automatic procedure adjusts the internal settings, called weights, so that the same mistake is less likely to recur. Repeated across millions of images, this process gradually tunes the network until its predictions become reliable. The quality of the final system therefore depends heavily on the size and accuracy of the training data.

[C] Image recognition relies particularly on a design known as a convolutional neural network. Rather than examining every pixel independently, such a network scans small regions of the picture, searching for simple patterns like edges and corners. Deeper layers combine these basic features into more complex shapes, and eventually into recognisable parts such as an eye or a wheel. This layered structure allows the system to build understanding step by step, moving from the local detail of a few pixels towards the overall meaning of the scene.

[D] Despite impressive results, these systems have notable limitations. A network trained mainly on photographs taken in bright daylight may perform poorly in dim conditions, and small alterations to an image that a human would never notice can occasionally cause confident but absurd errors. Critics also point out that a network does not understand objects in the way people do; it merely detects statistical patterns. Nevertheless, image recognition already supports medical diagnosis, self-driving vehicles and everyday photo applications, and its influence continues to widen as the underlying methods improve.

questions.q: Recognising everyday objects in a photograph is a difficult task for most humans.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า [A] ระบุว่าเมื่อคนมองรูป การจำแมวหรือจักรยานได้นั้นรู้สึก 'effortless and immediate' (ง่ายดายและทันที) ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับประโยคคำถามที่บอกว่าเป็นเรื่องยากโดยตรง จึงตอบ False
questions.evidence: When a person glances at a photograph, recognising a cat or a bicycle feels effortless and immediate.

questions.q: To a computer, an image is essentially a grid of numbers.
options: True
False
Not Given

questions.evidence: For a computer, however, an image is nothing more than a grid of numbers, each representing the brightness or colour of a single point known as a pixel.

False

questions.evidence: The quality of the final system therefore depends heavily on the size and accuracy of the training data.

title: The Hidden Ecosystem Within: Gut Bacteria and Human Health
topic: The role of gut bacteria in human health
passage:
[A] The human digestive tract is home to trillions of microorganisms, collectively known as the gut microbiota. Although bacteria are often associated with disease, the vast majority of those residing in the intestine are harmless or actively beneficial. Scientists now estimate that the number of bacterial cells in the body is roughly equal to the number of human cells, a finding that has overturned earlier assumptions about the ratio. These communities are established shortly after birth and continue to develop throughout early childhood, shaped by factors such as diet, environment and the method of delivery at birth.

[B] One of the most important functions of gut bacteria is assisting in digestion. Certain complex carbohydrates, particularly dietary fibre, cannot be broken down by human enzymes alone. Instead, they pass into the large intestine, where resident bacteria ferment them. This process produces short-chain fatty acids, compounds that supply energy to the cells lining the colon and help regulate inflammation. In addition, some gut bacteria synthesise essential vitamins, including vitamin K and several B vitamins, which the body absorbs and uses for a range of biological processes.

[C] The influence of gut bacteria extends well beyond the digestive system. A growing body of research suggests that the microbiota plays a significant role in training the immune system, teaching it to distinguish between harmful invaders and harmless substances. When this balance is disturbed, a condition sometimes referred to as dysbiosis, the consequences may include an increased susceptibility to certain illnesses. Researchers have linked imbalances in gut bacteria to conditions as varied as obesity, allergies and inflammatory bowel disease, although the precise mechanisms remain under investigation.

[D] Perhaps the most surprising area of study concerns the connection between the gut and the brain. This communication network, often called the gut–brain axis, allows signals to travel in both directions. Some bacteria produce neurotransmitters, the chemical messengers used by nerve cells, and preliminary studies indicate that the composition of the microbiota may influence mood and behaviour. While such findings are still tentative, they have generated considerable interest among scientists seeking new approaches to mental health.

[E] Given these discoveries, there is increasing interest in ways to support a healthy microbiota. Diets rich in fibre and fermented foods are widely recommended, and probiotic supplements containing live bacteria are marketed as a means of restoring balance. However, experts caution that the effects of such products vary considerably between individuals, and that much remains unknown. What is clear is that these microscopic organisms, once largely ignored, are now recognised as vital partners in maintaining human health.

options: True
False
Not Given

questions.evidence: Scientists now estimate that the number of bacterial cells in the body is roughly equal to the number of human cells, a finding that has overturned earlier assumptions about the ratio.

options: True

questions.evidence: Researchers have linked imbalances in gut bacteria to conditions as varied as obesity, allergies and inflammatory bowel disease, although the precise mechanisms remain under investigation.

questions.evidence: some gut bacteria synthesise essential vitamins, including vitamin K and several B vitamins

questions.evidence: When this balance is disturbed, a condition sometimes referred to as dysbiosis, the consequences may include an increased susceptibility to certain illnesses.

title: The Fall of the Maya
topic: The disappearance of the Maya civilisation
passage:
[A] Between roughly AD 250 and 900, the Maya of Mesoamerica built one of the most sophisticated civilisations of the ancient world. Across what is now southern Mexico, Guatemala and Belize, they raised towering stone pyramids, developed a complex writing system, and made remarkable advances in astronomy and mathematics. Yet within the space of little more than a century, many of the great southern cities were abandoned. Populations that had numbered in the hundreds of thousands dwindled, monumental construction ceased, and the forest gradually reclaimed the plazas. This so-called "collapse" has puzzled archaeologists for generations.

[B] Early explanations tended to favour a single dramatic cause. Some scholars proposed a devastating epidemic, others a violent peasant uprising against the ruling elite. Such theories, however, struggle to account for the fact that the decline was neither uniform nor instantaneous. While the cities of the southern lowlands were emptying, settlements to the north, such as Chichen Itza, continued to flourish for centuries. The Maya themselves never truly vanished; millions of their descendants still live in the region today and speak Mayan languages. For this reason, most modern researchers prefer to speak of a transformation rather than an extinction.

[C] The most influential recent theories emphasise environmental pressures. Analysis of sediment cores drawn from lake beds indicates that the region experienced a series of severe droughts between approximately AD 800 and 1000, precisely when the collapse was at its height. The Maya had cleared vast tracts of forest for agriculture, which may have reduced rainfall and accelerated soil erosion. As harvests failed, competition for dwindling resources is thought to have intensified, and warfare between rival city-states became more frequent.

[D] Few archaeologists now believe that any single factor was responsible. Instead, the prevailing view is that a combination of prolonged drought, environmental degradation, overpopulation and political instability created pressures that the Maya system could not withstand. What makes the case particularly compelling is that it offers a cautionary parallel for the present day, illustrating how even an advanced society can be undermined when it exhausts the natural resources on which it depends.

title: The Economics of the Minimum Wage
passage:
[A] Few instruments of economic policy have generated as much sustained controversy as the minimum wage. New Zealand introduced the world's first national minimum wage in 1894, and the United States followed in 1938, when the Fair Labor Standards Act established a federal floor of twenty-five cents per hour. The measure was designed to protect vulnerable workers from exploitation and to guarantee a basic standard of living. Today, more than ninety per cent of countries maintain some form of statutory wage floor, although the level and coverage of these laws vary enormously.

[B] For much of the twentieth century, economists were broadly sceptical of such policies. Standard theory treats the minimum wage as a price floor: if the legal minimum is set above the equilibrium wage that supply and demand would otherwise produce, employers will hire fewer workers, and unemployment among the low-skilled will rise. In an influential 1946 article, the economist George Stigler argued that minimum wages were poorly targeted, since many low-wage earners belonged to relatively affluent households, while the poorest individuals often had no job at all and therefore could not benefit from higher hourly rates.

[C] This consensus was challenged in 1994, when David Card and Alan Krueger published a study comparing fast-food restaurants in New Jersey, which had raised its minimum wage, with similar restaurants in neighbouring Pennsylvania, which had not. Contrary to the standard prediction, employment in New Jersey did not fall; if anything, it rose slightly. One explanation invokes monopsony, a situation in which employers possess enough market power to hold wages below the competitive level. Under such conditions, a moderate wage floor can raise pay without destroying jobs, and may even encourage more people to seek work.

[D] Subsequent research has produced a more nuanced picture. Most contemporary studies find that modest increases have little measurable effect on overall employment, though some detect reduced hours or slower hiring for teenagers and the least experienced workers. Economists increasingly emphasise the ratio of the minimum wage to the median wage in a region: floors below roughly sixty per cent of the median appear relatively harmless, whereas higher ratios carry greater risk of job losses, particularly in poorer areas.

[E] The policy debate has consequently shifted from whether to have a minimum wage to how it should be designed. Several countries now index their wage floors to inflation or average earnings, removing the issue from annual political bargaining, while others permit regional variation to reflect local living costs. What began as a simple question of fairness has become one of the most carefully measured topics in empirical economics.

questions.q: New Zealand was the first country in the world to introduce a national minimum wage.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรงๆ ว่านิวซีแลนด์เป็นประเทศแรกของโลกที่ออกกฎหมายค่าแรงขั้นต่ำระดับชาติในปี 1894
questions.evidence: New Zealand introduced the world's first national minimum wage in 1894, and the United States followed in 1938, when the Fair Labor Standards Act established a federal floor of twenty-five cents per hour.

questions.evidence: Economists increasingly emphasise the ratio of the minimum wage to the median wage in a region: floors below roughly sixty per cent of the median appear relatively harmless, whereas higher ratios carry greater risk of job losses, particularly in poorer areas.

questions.evidence: New Zealand introduced the world's first national minimum wage in 1894, and the United States followed in 1938, when the Fair Labor Standards Act established a federal floor of twenty-five cents per hour.

questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าภายใต้ monopsony ค่าแรงขั้นต่ำระดับปานกลางสามารถเพิ่มค่าจ้างได้โดยไม่ทำลาย 'jobs'

questions.evidence: Several countries now index their wage floors to inflation or average earnings, removing the issue from annual political bargaining, while others permit regional variation to reflect local living costs.

title: From Boneshaker to Safety Bicycle
passage:
[A] The bicycle, today one of the most widely used vehicles on earth, has a surprisingly contested early history. Sketches of a two-wheeled machine were once attributed to the workshop of Leonardo da Vinci, but most historians now dismiss these drawings as later forgeries. The first documented ancestor of the bicycle was the draisine, invented in 1817 by the German civil servant Karl von Drais. His machine consisted of a wooden frame mounted on two wheels; the rider straddled the frame and pushed against the ground with the feet. Although it had no pedals, the draisine demonstrated the essential principle that a two-wheeled vehicle could be balanced while in motion. It enjoyed a brief fashion in several European capitals, but poor road surfaces and collisions with pedestrians led some cities to ban the machine entirely.

[B] The next significant advance came in the 1860s in Paris, where mechanics fitted pedals directly to the front wheel, creating the velocipede. These machines were nicknamed boneshakers because their iron-rimmed wooden wheels transmitted every vibration of the cobbled streets to the rider. Since a larger wheel travelled further with each turn of the pedals, manufacturers steadily enlarged the front wheel, producing the high-wheeled penny-farthing. This design offered impressive speed, but it placed the rider high above the ground, and falls were frequent and occasionally fatal. As a result, cycling in this era remained largely the pursuit of adventurous young men.

[C] The decisive breakthrough arrived in 1885, when the English inventor John Kemp Starley introduced the Rover safety bicycle. Its two wheels were of equal size, and a chain drive to the rear wheel delivered speed without a perilous riding position. Three years later, John Boyd Dunlop's pneumatic tyre replaced solid rubber and transformed the comfort of cycling. Together these innovations triggered the great cycling boom of the 1890s. Prices fell rapidly as mass production expanded, and the bicycle ceased to be a luxury item, spreading through the middle and working classes alike.

[D] Historians argue that the bicycle's social consequences were as important as its mechanical evolution. It gave ordinary people, and women in particular, an unprecedented degree of personal mobility, and it has been credited with encouraging practical clothing reform. Furthermore, the industry pioneered manufacturing techniques, such as precision ball bearings and metal stamping, that were later adopted by automobile makers. Although the motor car eventually displaced the bicycle in much of the industrialised world, recent concerns about congestion and climate change have restored the machine to a central place in urban transport planning.

questions.q: Most historians believe that Leonardo da Vinci's workshop produced genuine designs for a two-wheeled machine.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่านักประวัติศาสตร์ส่วนใหญ่ปัดตกภาพร่างเหล่านั้นว่าเป็นของปลอมที่ทำขึ้นภายหลัง จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความโดยตรง
questions.evidence: Sketches of a two-wheeled machine were once attributed to the workshop of Leonardo da Vinci, but most historians now dismiss these drawings as later forgeries.

questions.evidence: It enjoyed a brief fashion in several European capitals, but poor road surfaces and collisions with pedestrians led some cities to ban the machine entirely.

questions.evidence: As a result, cycling in this era remained largely the pursuit of adventurous young men.

options: Their wheels transmitted road vibrations to the rider

questions.evidence: Its two wheels were of equal size, and a chain drive to the rear wheel delivered speed without a perilous riding position.

options: Automobile manufacturing

questions.evidence: Furthermore, the industry pioneered manufacturing techniques, such as precision ball bearings and metal stamping, that were later adopted by automobile makers.

title: The Ocean's Hidden Engine
passage:
[A] The world's oceans are in constant motion, driven by a combination of wind, the rotation of the Earth, and differences in the density of seawater. Together, these forces create a vast network of currents that transport heat around the planet. Surface currents, propelled largely by prevailing winds, move warm water away from the equator towards the poles, while colder water flows back towards the tropics. This continuous exchange of energy plays a decisive role in shaping regional climates, making some areas far milder than their latitude alone would suggest.

[B] One of the most studied surface currents is the Gulf Stream, which carries warm water from the Gulf of Mexico across the Atlantic towards northwestern Europe. The heat released by this current keeps countries such as Britain and Norway noticeably warmer in winter than other places at similar latitudes, for instance parts of Canada. Without the Gulf Stream, the climate of these European nations would be considerably harsher. Scientists estimate that the current transports roughly the energy equivalent of a million power stations, an amount that illustrates the enormous scale of the ocean's influence.

[C] Beneath the surface lies a slower but equally important system known as the thermohaline circulation, sometimes called the global conveyor belt. This movement is governed by temperature and salinity rather than wind. In the cold polar regions, surface water becomes denser as it cools and as ice formation increases its salt content. This dense water sinks to the ocean floor and travels slowly towards the equator, drawing warmer water in behind it. A single circuit of this global loop is thought to take approximately one thousand years to complete.

[D] Researchers are increasingly concerned that climate change could disrupt these circulation patterns. As polar ice melts, large quantities of fresh water enter the ocean, reducing the salinity and therefore the density of surface water. If this water no longer sinks efficiently, the conveyor belt could slow down. Some scientists warn that a significant weakening of the thermohaline circulation might, paradoxically, cool parts of Europe even as the planet as a whole grows warmer. Although the precise consequences remain uncertain, most experts agree that the oceans will continue to be a central factor in determining the climate of the coming century.

questions.q: Surface ocean currents are driven mainly by the prevailing winds.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่ากระแสน้ำผิวหน้าถูกขับเคลื่อนโดยลมประจำถิ่นเป็นส่วนใหญ่
questions.evidence: Surface currents, propelled largely by prevailing winds, move warm water away from the equator towards the poles, while colder water flows back towards the tropics.

questions.evidence: As polar ice melts, large quantities of fresh water enter the ocean, reducing the salinity and therefore the density of surface water.

questions.q: Why does Britain have a milder winter climate than its latitude would suggest?
questions.a: Warm water carried by the Gulf Stream releases heat over the region.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าความร้อนที่ปล่อยจากกระแสน้ำกัลฟ์สตรีมทำให้ประเทศอุ่นขึ้น
questions.evidence: The heat released by this current keeps countries such as Britain and Norway noticeably warmer in winter than other places at similar latitudes, for instance parts of Canada.

title: The history of coffee houses
passage:
[A] The public coffee house emerged in the Ottoman city of Constantinople in the sixteenth century, where establishments serving the newly popular beverage rapidly became fixtures of urban life. These venues offered more than a drink; they provided a space in which men could gather to converse, listen to music, play games such as chess, and exchange news. Because they encouraged discussion that authorities could not easily monitor, coffee houses were periodically viewed with suspicion, and rulers occasionally attempted to close them. Such prohibitions consistently proved short-lived, for the institution had already become too deeply woven into daily routine to be suppressed.

[B] Coffee reached Western Europe through trade during the seventeenth century, and the first English coffee house opened in Oxford in 1650. London followed two years later, and within a few decades the capital contained several thousand such establishments. Entry typically cost one penny, and for that modest sum a visitor could read newspapers, debate politics, and hear the latest commercial reports. This affordability earned the coffee houses the nickname "penny universities", since a customer could acquire knowledge for the price of a single cup rather than the cost of a formal education.

[C] The coffee house came to serve distinct commercial and intellectual functions. Certain premises attracted particular clienteles: merchants, writers, or men of science each favoured specific houses where they knew their associates would be found. The most celebrated example was that of Edward Lloyd, whose establishment near the River Thames became a meeting point for those involved in shipping and marine insurance. The information exchanged there was so valuable that Lloyd's eventually developed into one of the world's leading insurance markets, though the coffee itself had long since ceased to be its main attraction.

[D] Not everyone welcomed the new institution. In 1674 a pamphlet supposedly written by women complained that their husbands spent excessive time in coffee houses, neglecting domestic duties. King Charles II of England, uneasy about the political conversation the houses fostered, issued a proclamation to suppress them in 1675, but withdrew it within days amid widespread objection. By the eighteenth century, however, the coffee house was gradually eclipsed as private clubs and tea gained popularity. Although its dominance faded, the coffee house had established an enduring model of sociable public space that later cafés would inherit.

questions.q: The earliest public coffee houses appeared in Europe before they existed in the Ottoman Empire.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าคอฟฟี่เฮาส์เกิดที่คอนสแตนติโนเปิลในศตวรรษที่ 16 ก่อนจะมาถึงยุโรปในศตวรรษที่ 17 จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: The public coffee house emerged in the Ottoman city of Constantinople in the sixteenth century, where establishments serving the newly popular beverage rapidly became fixtures of urban life.

title: Lifting the City: The Invention of the Elevator
passage:
[A] Devices for raising loads have existed for thousands of years. The Roman architect Vitruvius recorded that the mathematician Archimedes built a hoisting platform as early as the third century BC, and similar mechanisms, powered by human labour, animals or water wheels, were used throughout the ancient and medieval worlds. These early machines relied on ropes wound around a drum. Because a single frayed or broken rope meant the platform would plunge to the ground, such lifts were reserved for goods rather than people, and their use remained limited for many centuries.

[B] The decisive breakthrough came not from making platforms rise, but from making them safe to fall. In 1852, an American mechanic named Elisha Otis designed a safety brake that would stop a lift instantly if its rope failed. A toothed bar ran the length of the shaft, and a spring, held under tension by the taut rope, would spring outward and lock into the teeth the moment the tension was lost. Otis demonstrated his invention dramatically at the New York World's Fair in 1854, standing on a raised platform and ordering an assistant to cut the supporting rope. Instead of dropping, the platform held fast. This public display reassured a doubtful public that passengers could travel safely.

[C] Otis's safety device transformed the elevator from a curiosity into a practical necessity. He installed his first passenger elevator in a New York department store in 1857. However, early elevators were still driven by steam or hydraulic power, which was slow and required bulky machinery. The introduction of the electric motor changed this. In 1880, the German engineer Werner von Siemens built the first electric elevator, and within two decades electric models had largely replaced their steam-driven predecessors in the world's growing cities.

[D] The consequences of this technology extended far beyond convenience. Before the elevator, the upper floors of a building were considered undesirable, because reaching them meant a tiring climb; consequently, the wealthiest tenants occupied the lower storeys. The elevator reversed this hierarchy, turning the once-neglected top floors into the most sought-after and expensive space. More importantly, it made tall buildings genuinely usable. Together with the development of steel-frame construction, the elevator enabled the skyscraper, and with it the vertical growth of the modern city. What had begun as a simple hoist for raising cargo had become the engine of urban life.

questions.q: Vitruvius personally designed the hoisting platform described in the passage.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่า Archimedes เป็นผู้สร้าง ส่วน Vitruvius เพียงบันทึกไว้ จึงขัดแย้ง = False

title: Harnessing the Wind
passage:
[A] Wind power has grown from a marginal energy source into one of the fastest-expanding technologies in the global electricity system. The principle behind it is deceptively simple: moving air turns the blades of a turbine, which drives a generator that converts mechanical rotation into electrical current. Yet turning this basic idea into a reliable industrial supply has required decades of refinement in materials, aerodynamics and control software. Modern machines bear little resemblance to the small farm windmills of the nineteenth century, which were used chiefly for pumping water and grinding grain rather than producing electricity.

[B] A contemporary turbine is dominated by three long blades mounted on a horizontal axis. These blades are shaped like aircraft wings, so that passing air generates lift rather than simply pushing against a flat surface. The amount of power a turbine can capture rises sharply with the length of its blades, because the area swept by the rotor increases with the square of the radius. This relationship explains why manufacturers have steadily enlarged their machines; the biggest offshore models now carry blades longer than a football pitch. Power also rises with the cube of wind speed, which means that a modest increase in wind can produce a substantial gain in output.

[C] Location is critical to a project's success. Engineers rely on months of measurements from tall masts fitted with sensors before committing to a site, since even neighbouring valleys can differ greatly in their average wind. Offshore installations have become increasingly attractive because winds at sea are stronger and steadier than those over land, and because large structures provoke fewer objections from residents. Building in water is nonetheless expensive, as foundations must withstand waves, tides and corrosion from salt.

[D] The chief limitation of wind remains its variability. Because the wind does not blow constantly, output fluctuates in ways that operators cannot fully predict, and electricity is difficult to store in large quantities. Grid managers therefore combine wind with other sources and, increasingly, with batteries that release power when the air is still. Despite these challenges, the cost of wind-generated electricity has fallen dramatically, and in many regions it is now cheaper than power from newly built fossil-fuel plants.

title: The Distributed Mind of the Octopus
passage:
[A] The octopus presents biologists with a striking puzzle. As an invertebrate belonging to the group known as cephalopods, it is only distantly related to the animals normally regarded as intelligent, such as mammals and birds. The common ancestor shared by octopuses and humans lived more than 500 million years ago and possessed little more than a rudimentary nervous system. Yet the modern octopus displays behaviour that many researchers are willing to describe as a form of thinking. This makes it a valuable subject for anyone interested in how complex cognition can arise through entirely separate evolutionary paths.

[B] What is most remarkable about the octopus is the organisation of its nervous system. An octopus possesses around 500 million neurons, a figure comparable to that of a dog. However, only about one third of these are located in the central brain. The remainder are distributed throughout the animal's eight arms, each of which contains a dense cluster of nerve cells. Because of this arrangement, an individual arm can carry out certain tasks, such as reaching towards food or recognising texture, without direct instruction from the central brain. Some scientists therefore argue that the octopus does not possess a single mind but rather a collection of semi-independent processing centres.

[C] This unusual anatomy raises difficult questions about how the animal experiences the world. When an octopus solves a problem, it is unclear whether the decision originates in the brain or emerges from the combined activity of the arms. Laboratory studies have shown that octopuses can open jars to obtain prey, navigate simple mazes, and remember the solution for a period of weeks. Certain individuals have even been observed carrying coconut shells across the seabed in order to assemble a shelter later, a behaviour that suggests planning for the future rather than mere reaction to immediate circumstances.

[D] The octopus is also capable of learning by observation, an ability once thought to be restricted to vertebrates. In one experiment, untrained octopuses watched trained ones select a particular object and afterwards copied the choice. Such findings have encouraged some philosophers to treat the octopus as an important test case for theories of consciousness. If a creature so different from ourselves can think, then intelligence may be far less rare in nature than was previously assumed. For this reason, the octopus continues to attract attention well beyond the field of marine biology.

questions.evidence: As an invertebrate belonging to the group known as cephalopods, it is only distantly related to the animals normally regarded as intelligent, such as mammals and birds.

title: The Chemistry of Cooking
passage:
[A] Cooking is often regarded as an art, yet at its foundation it is a series of controlled chemical reactions. When heat is applied to food, the molecules within it are rearranged, producing new compounds that alter flavour, colour, texture and aroma. Understanding these transformations allows a cook to work with predictable outcomes rather than relying on chance. For this reason, some scientists describe the kitchen as the most common chemistry laboratory in the world, where reactions are performed daily without any awareness of the processes involved.

[B] Perhaps the most celebrated reaction in cooking is the Maillard reaction, first described by the French chemist Louis-Camille Maillard in 1912. It occurs when amino acids, the building blocks of proteins, react with certain sugars in the presence of heat. This reaction is responsible for the brown crust on roasted meat, the golden surface of freshly baked bread and the rich colour of coffee beans. The Maillard reaction generally requires temperatures above 140 degrees Celsius, which is why boiled or steamed foods, whose surfaces cannot exceed the boiling point of water, rarely develop such browning. The reaction produces hundreds of distinct aroma compounds, giving cooked food its characteristic savoury depth.

[C] Caramelisation is a related but separate process that involves the breakdown of sugars alone, without the participation of proteins. As sugar is heated beyond roughly 160 degrees Celsius, its molecules fragment and recombine, creating the sweet, slightly bitter notes found in caramel sauce and roasted vegetables. Although both reactions produce brown colours, they should not be confused, as they follow different chemical pathways and occur at different temperatures.

[D] Proteins themselves undergo dramatic change during cooking through a process known as denaturation. In their natural state, protein molecules are folded into complex shapes, but heat causes these structures to unravel and then bond together in new arrangements. This is why a transparent, liquid egg white turns firm and opaque when fried. Acids, such as the lemon juice used in preparing certain fish dishes, can produce a similar effect without any heat at all, effectively cooking the food through chemistry rather than temperature.

[E] Water also plays a decisive role in the chemistry of the kitchen. It acts as a solvent, dissolving salts and sugars so that they can be distributed evenly, and it conducts heat efficiently throughout a dish. However, because water cannot rise above 100 degrees Celsius at normal atmospheric pressure, methods that rely on it are limited in the reactions they can achieve. This limitation explains why frying and roasting, which reach far higher temperatures, generate flavours that boiling simply cannot.

questions.q: The Maillard reaction was named after the scientist who first described it.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่านักเคมี Louis-Camille Maillard เป็นผู้อธิบายปฏิกิริยานี้เป็นครั้งแรกในปี 1912 และปฏิกิริยาถูกตั้งชื่อตามเขา
questions.evidence: Perhaps the most celebrated reaction in cooking is the Maillard reaction, first described by the French chemist Louis-Camille Maillard in 1912.

questions.evidence: Acids, such as the lemon juice used in preparing certain fish dishes, can produce a similar effect without any heat at all, effectively cooking the food through chemistry rather than temperature.

questions.evidence: It acts as a solvent, dissolving salts and sugars so that they can be distributed evenly, and it conducts heat efficiently throughout a dish.

title: Charting the World: A History of Maps
passage:
[A] The impulse to represent the surrounding world in graphic form is among the oldest of human intellectual activities. The earliest surviving world map, a Babylonian clay tablet produced around 600 BC, depicts the city of Babylon at the centre of a circular world encircled by a body of water labelled the "bitter river". Scholars generally agree that such early maps served symbolic and religious purposes rather than practical navigation, expressing a community's understanding of its place in the cosmos rather than guiding travellers from one location to another.

[B] It was Greek scholarship that transformed mapmaking into a scientific discipline. In the second century AD, the astronomer Claudius Ptolemy completed his Geographia, a work that recorded the coordinates of some 8,000 places and explained how the curved surface of the Earth could be projected onto a flat sheet. Although Ptolemy underestimated the circumference of the Earth, his systematic use of latitude and longitude established principles that cartographers followed for well over a thousand years.

[C] During the European Middle Ages, scientific cartography largely gave way to the mappa mundi, a richly illustrated map that placed Jerusalem at the centre of the world and was intended to convey religious meaning rather than geographical accuracy. Elsewhere, however, Islamic scholars such as al-Idrisi preserved and refined Ptolemaic methods. The decisive turning point came with the age of maritime exploration in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. Portolan charts, practical guides for sailors that were crisscrossed with compass lines, achieved remarkable accuracy along well-travelled coastlines. Then, in 1569, the Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator published a projection that enabled navigators to plot a straight-line course of constant bearing. His projection remains in use in marine navigation today, even though it greatly exaggerates the size of landmasses near the poles.

[D] The modern era of mapping began in eighteenth-century France, where the Cassini family undertook the first survey of an entire country based on triangulation, an enterprise so ambitious that it took four generations to complete. In the twentieth century, aerial photography allowed vast territories to be mapped with unprecedented speed, and later in the century mapmakers gained access to satellite imagery and the Global Positioning System. Today, digital maps are updated almost continuously and are consulted by billions of people, yet they continue the same essential project begun on that Babylonian tablet: the attempt to make the world comprehensible by reducing it to a readable image.

questions.q: The earliest surviving world map shows Babylon at the centre of the world.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุชัดเจนว่าแผ่นดินเหนียวบาบิโลนแสดงเมืองบาบิโลนอยู่ตรงกลางของโลกทรงกลม ข้อความนี้จึงตรงกับเนื้อหา (True)
questions.evidence: The earliest surviving world map, a Babylonian clay tablet produced around 600 BC, depicts the city of Babylon at the centre of a circular world encircled by a body of water labelled the "bitter river".

questions.q: Ptolemy's Geographia recorded the coordinates of more than 10,000 places.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่า Geographia บันทึกพิกัดประมาณ 8,000 แห่ง ไม่ใช่มากกว่า 10,000 แห่ง จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความโดยตรง (False)
questions.evidence: In the second century AD, the astronomer Claudius Ptolemy completed his Geographia, a work that recorded the coordinates of some 8,000 places and explained how the curved surface of the Earth could be projected onto a flat sheet.

questions.q: Ptolemy personally travelled to the places whose coordinates he recorded.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวเพียงว่าปโตเลมีบันทึกพิกัดของสถานที่ต่าง ๆ แต่ไม่ได้ระบุว่าเขาเดินทางไปยังสถานที่เหล่านั้นด้วยตนเองหรือไม่ (Not Given)

questions.q: Islamic scholars abandoned the methods developed by Ptolemy.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่านักวิชาการอิสลามอย่าง al-Idrisi ได้อนุรักษ์และพัฒนาวิธีการของปโตเลมีต่อ ไม่ใช่ละทิ้ง จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ (False)
questions.evidence: Elsewhere, however, Islamic scholars such as al-Idrisi preserved and refined Ptolemaic methods.

questions.q: The Mercator projection is still used in marine navigation.
questions.why_th: บทความยืนยันว่าแผนที่ฉายแบบ Mercator ยังคงใช้ในการเดินเรือจนถึงปัจจุบัน แม้จะขยายขนาดแผ่นดินใกล้ขั้วโลกเกินจริง (True)
questions.evidence: His projection remains in use in marine navigation today, even though it greatly exaggerates the size of landmasses near the poles.

questions.q: According to the passage, the earliest maps were mainly intended to
options: express symbolic and religious ideas
help travellers find their way
record the boundaries of empires
assist sailors in coastal navigation
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่านักวิชาการเห็นพ้องกันว่าแผนที่ยุคแรกมีจุดประสงค์เชิงสัญลักษณ์และศาสนา ไม่ใช่เพื่อการนำทางจริง
questions.evidence: Scholars generally agree that such early maps served symbolic and religious purposes rather than practical navigation, expressing a community's understanding of its place in the cosmos rather than guiding travellers from one location to another.

questions.q: What limitation of Ptolemy's work does the passage mention?
options: He underestimated the circumference of the Earth
He ignored latitude and longitude
He mapped only the Mediterranean region
His work was lost for a thousand years
questions.a: He underestimated the circumference of the Earth
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวว่าปโตเลมีประเมินเส้นรอบวงของโลกต่ำเกินไป (underestimated) ซึ่งเป็นข้อจำกัดเพียงข้อเดียวที่ถูกกล่าวถึง
questions.evidence: Although Ptolemy underestimated the circumference of the Earth, his systematic use of latitude and longitude established principles that cartographers followed for well over a thousand years.

questions.q: The main purpose of the medieval mappa mundi was to
options: convey religious meaning
achieve geographical accuracy
guide maritime exploration
record newly discovered lands
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า mappa mundi มีจุดมุ่งหมายเพื่อสื่อความหมายทางศาสนา มากกว่าความถูกต้องทางภูมิศาสตร์
questions.evidence: During the European Middle Ages, scientific cartography largely gave way to the mappa mundi, a richly illustrated map that placed Jerusalem at the centre of the world and was intended to convey religious meaning rather than geographical accuracy.

questions.q: Why was Mercator's projection especially useful to navigators?
options: It enabled them to plot a straight-line course of constant bearing
It showed landmasses near the poles at their true size
It was the first map to use compass lines
It reduced the time needed to survey coastlines
questions.a: It enabled them to plot a straight-line course of constant bearing
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าแผนที่ฉายของ Mercator ช่วยให้นักเดินเรือวางเส้นทางตรงที่มีทิศทางคงที่ (constant bearing) ได้
questions.evidence: Then, in 1569, the Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator published a projection that enabled navigators to plot a straight-line course of constant bearing.

questions.q: Portolan charts were practical guides for sailors that were crisscrossed with ___ lines.
questions.why_th: คำตอบมาจากประโยคที่บรรยาย portolan charts ว่ามีเส้น compass lines พาดขวางไปมา ใช้คำเดียวตรงจากบทความ
questions.evidence: Portolan charts, practical guides for sailors that were crisscrossed with compass lines, achieved remarkable accuracy along well-travelled coastlines.

questions.q: The Cassini family's survey of the whole of France was based on ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการสำรวจประเทศฝรั่งเศสทั้งประเทศครั้งแรกใช้หลักการ triangulation คำตอบยกมาจากบทความโดยตรง
questions.evidence: The modern era of mapping began in eighteenth-century France, where the Cassini family undertook the first survey of an entire country based on triangulation, an enterprise so ambitious that it took four generations to complete.

questions.q: Later in the twentieth century, mapmakers gained access to ___ and the Global Positioning System.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวว่าในช่วงหลังของศตวรรษที่ 20 นักทำแผนที่เข้าถึง satellite imagery และ GPS คำตอบเป็นวลีสองคำตรงจากบทความ
questions.evidence: In the twentieth century, aerial photography allowed vast territories to be mapped with unprecedented speed, and later in the century mapmakers gained access to satellite imagery and the Global Positioning System.

title: The Puzzle of Left-Handedness
passage:
[A] Across every human society ever studied, a consistent minority of people prefer to use their left hand for skilled tasks such as writing or throwing. Estimates suggest that roughly one in ten individuals is left-handed, a proportion that has remained remarkably stable over thousands of years and appears in populations on every continent. This uniformity has long fascinated scientists, because it implies that whatever causes left-handedness is a deep-rooted feature of human biology rather than a passing cultural fashion. Yet despite decades of research, no single explanation has been universally accepted.

[B] The most obvious candidate is genetics. Handedness clearly runs in families: two left-handed parents are considerably more likely to have a left-handed child than two right-handed parents are. However, the pattern does not follow the simple rules that govern traits such as eye colour. Identical twins, who share all of their genes, quite often differ in the hand they favour, which demonstrates that genes cannot be the whole story. Modern studies have identified several genes associated with handedness, but each exerts only a tiny influence, and together they account for just a fraction of the variation observed.

[C] Because genetics alone is insufficient, researchers have turned to the developing brain. The two halves of the brain are not identical; in most people the left hemisphere, which controls the right side of the body, is dominant for both language and fine motor control. In left-handers this arrangement is more variable. Some scientists argue that handedness is fixed very early, even before birth, since ultrasound scans reveal that many foetuses already show a preference for sucking one thumb rather than the other. Conditions in the womb, including the levels of certain hormones the developing baby is exposed to, may therefore help to tip the balance one way or the other.

[D] The persistence of left-handedness raises a further question: if right-handers form such an overwhelming majority, why has the minority not disappeared? One influential theory proposes that being different confers a hidden advantage in competitive situations. In sports such as fencing, tennis and boxing, left-handers are unusually successful, apparently because their opponents, accustomed to facing right-handers, are caught off guard. Historically, a similar surprise effect may have proved valuable in physical combat. This idea suggests that left-handedness survives precisely because it is rare, a balance that would collapse if left-handers ever became the majority.

[E] Whatever its ultimate cause, left-handedness is no longer regarded as a defect to be corrected. For much of the twentieth century, children who wrote with their left hand were routinely forced to switch, sometimes with damaging psychological effects. Today most educators accept that handedness is a natural variation, and the once-common practice of retraining has largely disappeared in many countries.

questions.q: The proportion of left-handed people has changed significantly over the past few thousand years.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าสัดส่วนคงที่มาก (remarkably stable) ตลอดหลายพันปี ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Estimates suggest that roughly one in ten individuals is left-handed, a proportion that has remained remarkably stable over thousands of years and appears in populations on every continent.

questions.q: Scientists have reached general agreement on a single cause of left-handedness.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่ายังไม่มีคำอธิบายใดได้รับการยอมรับอย่างเป็นสากล
questions.evidence: Yet despite decades of research, no single explanation has been universally accepted.

questions.q: Identical twins always share the same hand preference.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าฝาแฝดแท้มักจะถนัดมือต่างกัน
questions.evidence: Identical twins, who share all of their genes, quite often differ in the hand they favour, which demonstrates that genes cannot be the whole story.

questions.q: Left-handed people tend to earn higher incomes than right-handed people.
questions.why_th: บทความไม่ได้กล่าวถึงเรื่องรายได้ของคนถนัดซ้ายเลย

questions.q: Forcing children to write with their right hand could cause psychological harm.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการบังคับเปลี่ยนมือบางครั้งก่อผลเสียทางจิตใจ
questions.evidence: For much of the twentieth century, children who wrote with their left hand were routinely forced to switch, sometimes with damaging psychological effects.

questions.q: What does the passage say about the genes linked to handedness?
questions.a: Each gene has only a very small effect on handedness.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าแต่ละยีนมีอิทธิพลเพียงเล็กน้อยและรวมกันอธิบายได้แค่ส่วนหนึ่ง
questions.evidence: Modern studies have identified several genes associated with handedness, but each exerts only a tiny influence, and together they account for just a fraction of the variation observed.
options: Each gene has only a very small effect on handedness.
A single gene determines handedness completely.
No genes have yet been connected to handedness.
The relevant genes also control eye colour.

questions.q: According to the passage, in most people the left hemisphere of the brain is dominant for
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าซีกซ้ายควบคุมทั้งภาษาและการเคลื่อนไหวละเอียด
questions.evidence: in most people the left hemisphere, which controls the right side of the body, is dominant for both language and fine motor control.
options: language and fine motor control.
memory and emotion.
vision and hearing.
balance and coordination.

questions.q: What evidence suggests that handedness may be established before birth?
questions.a: Ultrasound scans show foetuses preferring to suck one particular thumb.
questions.why_th: บทความยกตัวอย่างภาพอัลตราซาวด์ที่ทารกในครรภ์ชอบดูดนิ้วข้างหนึ่ง
questions.evidence: since ultrasound scans reveal that many foetuses already show a preference for sucking one thumb rather than the other.
options: Ultrasound scans show foetuses preferring to suck one particular thumb.
Newborn babies can immediately write with one hand.
Genes for handedness are switched off at birth.
Mothers can feel which hand the baby prefers.

questions.q: The theory in paragraph D explains the survival of left-handedness by suggesting that being left-handed
questions.a: gives an advantage in competitive situations because it is unexpected.
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าความแตกต่างสร้างข้อได้เปรียบเพราะคู่ต่อสู้ตั้งตัวไม่ทัน
questions.evidence: One influential theory proposes that being different confers a hidden advantage in competitive situations.
options: gives an advantage in competitive situations because it is unexpected.
makes people physically stronger than right-handers.
improves performance in every kind of sport equally.
allows people to learn languages more quickly.

questions.q: Left-handedness appears in human populations on every ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าพบในประชากรทุกทวีป
questions.evidence: a proportion that has remained remarkably stable over thousands of years and appears in populations on every continent.

questions.q: The levels of certain ___ that the developing baby is exposed to in the womb may influence handedness.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึงระดับฮอร์โมนบางชนิดในครรภ์ที่อาจมีผล
questions.evidence: including the levels of certain hormones the developing baby is exposed to, may therefore help to tip the balance one way or the other.

questions.q: Left-handers are unusually successful in sports such as fencing, tennis and ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุกีฬาที่คนถนัดซ้ายประสบความสำเร็จ ได้แก่ ฟันดาบ เทนนิส และมวย
questions.evidence: In sports such as fencing, tennis and boxing, left-handers are unusually successful, apparently because their opponents, accustomed to facing right-handers, are caught off guard.

title: Guardians of the Past: How Museums Preserve Their Collections
passage:
[A] Museums are entrusted with the care of objects that may be hundreds or even thousands of years old, and preserving these artefacts for future generations is among their most demanding responsibilities. Every material, whether stone, metal, textile or paper, begins to deteriorate the moment it is created, and the task of the museum is not to halt this decay entirely, which is impossible, but to slow it dramatically. This discipline, known as conservation, combines scientific analysis with careful handling and continuous monitoring of the surrounding environment.

[B] The most significant threats to an artefact are often invisible. Fluctuations in temperature and relative humidity can cause materials to expand and contract repeatedly, leading to cracking, warping and eventually structural failure. For this reason, most galleries maintain a stable climate, typically around 20 degrees Celsius with a relative humidity of roughly 50 per cent. Light presents a further danger, particularly ultraviolet radiation, which fades pigments and weakens organic fibres. Sensitive items such as watercolours and manuscripts are therefore displayed under dimmed lighting and are frequently rotated into storage to limit their total exposure.

[C] Biological agents pose an equally serious problem. Insects, mould and bacteria can consume organic materials such as wood, leather and silk, sometimes destroying an object within a remarkably short period. To counter this, conservators inspect collections regularly and control the conditions in which items are kept, since pests thrive in warm, damp and dark surroundings. When an infestation is discovered, affected objects may be isolated and frozen at very low temperatures, a method that kills insects without introducing harmful chemicals.

[D] Physical intervention is undertaken only when necessary, and the guiding principle is minimal interference. Conservators generally prefer to stabilise an object rather than restore it to a pristine appearance, because aggressive treatment can erase valuable historical evidence. A further principle is reversibility: any material added during treatment, such as adhesive or supporting fabric, should be removable in the future without damaging the original. Detailed records are kept of every procedure, ensuring that later specialists understand precisely what has been done.

[E] Preservation, however, extends well beyond the objects on display. The majority of a museum's holdings are never exhibited at all, resting instead in specialised stores where conditions can be tightly regulated. Increasingly, institutions also create high-resolution digital copies of their collections. While such records cannot replace an original artefact, they allow scholars worldwide to study fragile items without handling them, thereby reducing the risks that inevitably accompany access.

questions.q: Conservation aims to stop the deterioration of artefacts completely.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าการหยุดการเสื่อมสภาพทั้งหมดเป็นไปไม่ได้ เป้าหมายคือชะลอให้ช้าลงอย่างมาก จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Every material, whether stone, metal, textile or paper, begins to deteriorate the moment it is created, and the task of the museum is not to halt this decay entirely, which is impossible, but to slow it dramatically.

questions.q: Ultraviolet radiation can cause the colours in some artefacts to fade.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวชัดเจนว่ารังสีอัลตราไวโอเลตทำให้เม็ดสีจางลง ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Light presents a further danger, particularly ultraviolet radiation, which fades pigments and weakens organic fibres.

questions.q: Freezing infested objects is more effective than using chemicals.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกเพียงว่าการแช่แข็งฆ่าแมลงได้โดยไม่ต้องใช้สารเคมีอันตราย แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบว่าวิธีใดได้ผลมากกว่ากัน

questions.q: Conservators usually try to make damaged objects look brand new.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าผู้อนุรักษ์นิยมทำให้วัตถุคงสภาพ (stabilise) มากกว่าฟื้นฟูให้ดูใหม่เอี่ยม จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Conservators generally prefer to stabilise an object rather than restore it to a pristine appearance, because aggressive treatment can erase valuable historical evidence.

questions.q: Most of the items owned by a museum are on public display.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าของสะสมส่วนใหญ่ไม่เคยถูกนำมาจัดแสดงเลย แต่เก็บไว้ในคลังเฉพาะทาง จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: The majority of a museum's holdings are never exhibited at all, resting instead in specialised stores where conditions can be tightly regulated.

questions.q: Why do many galleries keep temperature and humidity stable?
questions.a: Repeated expansion and contraction of materials can lead to cracking and warping.
options: Repeated expansion and contraction of materials can lead to cracking and warping.
Visitors are more comfortable in a controlled climate.
Stable conditions make artefacts appear brighter to the public.
It reduces the electricity used by lighting systems.
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าความผันผวนของอุณหภูมิและความชื้นทำให้วัสดุขยายและหดตัวซ้ำ ๆ จนแตกร้าวและบิดงอ ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึงในบทความ
questions.evidence: Fluctuations in temperature and relative humidity can cause materials to expand and contract repeatedly, leading to cracking, warping and eventually structural failure.

questions.q: According to the passage, biological agents are dangerous because they
questions.a: can consume organic materials very quickly.
options: can consume organic materials very quickly.
are impossible to detect during inspections.
spread more rapidly than temperature damage.
cannot be removed once they appear.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าแมลง เชื้อรา และแบคทีเรียกินวัสดุอินทรีย์และอาจทำลายวัตถุได้ในเวลาอันสั้น ส่วนตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับบทความหรือไม่มีการกล่าวถึง
questions.evidence: Insects, mould and bacteria can consume organic materials such as wood, leather and silk, sometimes destroying an object within a remarkably short period.

questions.q: The principle of reversibility means that materials added during treatment should
questions.a: be able to be removed later without harming the original.
options: be able to be removed later without harming the original.
be recorded in detail for future specialists.
match the appearance of the original object.
be applied only by experienced conservators.
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายว่าหลักการย้อนกลับได้ (reversibility) คือวัสดุที่เพิ่มเข้าไปต้องถอดออกได้ในอนาคตโดยไม่ทำลายของเดิม ส่วนการบันทึกรายละเอียดเป็นอีกหลักการหนึ่ง ไม่ใช่ความหมายของ reversibility
questions.evidence: A further principle is reversibility: any material added during treatment, such as adhesive or supporting fabric, should be removable in the future without damaging the original.

questions.q: What does the passage say about digital copies of collections?
questions.a: They let scholars study fragile items without handling them.
options: They let scholars study fragile items without handling them.
They will eventually replace original artefacts entirely.
They are more valuable than the objects on display.
They are created only for items kept in storage.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าสำเนาดิจิทัลช่วยให้นักวิชาการทั่วโลกศึกษาวัตถุเปราะบางได้โดยไม่ต้องสัมผัส และย้ำว่าสำเนาไม่สามารถแทนที่ของจริงได้ จึงตัดตัวเลือกอื่นออก
questions.evidence: While such records cannot replace an original artefact, they allow scholars worldwide to study fragile items without handling them, thereby reducing the risks that inevitably accompany access.

questions.q: Sensitive items such as watercolours and manuscripts are displayed under ___ lighting.
questions.why_th: บทความใช้คำว่า dimmed lighting (แสงที่หรี่ลง) สำหรับวัตถุที่ไวต่อแสง ตอบตามคำในบทความ
questions.evidence: Sensitive items such as watercolours and manuscripts are therefore displayed under dimmed lighting and are frequently rotated into storage to limit their total exposure.

questions.q: Pests thrive in warm, damp and ___ surroundings.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าสัตว์และแมลงรบกวนเจริญเติบโตได้ดีในสภาพแวดล้อมที่อบอุ่น ชื้น และมืด ตอบตามคำในบทความ
questions.evidence: To counter this, conservators inspect collections regularly and control the conditions in which items are kept, since pests thrive in warm, damp and dark surroundings.

questions.q: The guiding principle of physical intervention is described as minimal ___.
questions.why_th: บทความใช้วลี minimal interference (การแทรกแซงให้น้อยที่สุด) เป็นหลักการชี้นำการแทรกแซงทางกายภาพ
questions.evidence: Physical intervention is undertaken only when necessary, and the guiding principle is minimal interference.

title: The Rise of Electric Vehicles
passage:
[A] Although electric vehicles, commonly abbreviated to EVs, are frequently regarded as a recent innovation, their origins reach back to the nineteenth century. Early prototypes appeared in the 1830s, and by 1900 electric cars accounted for roughly a third of the vehicles on the roads of the United States. They were valued for being quiet and easy to operate, unlike the noisy petrol engines of the period. However, the discovery of abundant, inexpensive oil, together with the mass production of the internal combustion engine, soon rendered the electric car uncompetitive, and it virtually disappeared for much of the twentieth century.

[B] Interest in electric transport was rekindled towards the end of the twentieth century, driven largely by mounting concern about air pollution and the finite nature of fossil fuels. The decisive breakthrough, however, was technological. The development of the lithium-ion battery, which stores far more energy for its weight than earlier designs, allowed manufacturers to build cars capable of travelling meaningful distances on a single charge. As production expanded, the cost of these batteries fell dramatically, dropping by almost ninety per cent between 2010 and 2020. This steady decline has been the single most important factor in making EVs affordable to ordinary consumers.

[C] Governments have also played a significant part in accelerating adoption. Many countries now offer financial incentives, such as tax reductions or direct subsidies, to buyers of electric cars. Others have announced target dates after which the sale of new petrol and diesel vehicles will be prohibited. Alongside these measures, substantial public investment has been directed towards charging infrastructure, since the availability of convenient charging points strongly influences whether motorists are willing to make the switch.

[D] Nevertheless, the transition is not without difficulties. Critics point out that manufacturing a battery requires large quantities of minerals such as lithium and cobalt, the extraction of which can cause considerable environmental harm. Moreover, an electric vehicle is only genuinely clean if the electricity charging it is generated from renewable sources rather than coal or gas. Questions also remain about how the growing number of used batteries will eventually be recycled. Despite these concerns, most analysts agree that electric vehicles will form the backbone of road transport within a few decades, and sales continue to climb year on year across virtually every major market.

questions.q: Electric vehicles were first invented in the twentieth century.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าจุดกำเนิดของรถไฟฟ้าย้อนไปถึงศตวรรษที่ 19 และต้นแบบปรากฏในทศวรรษ 1830 ไม่ใช่ศตวรรษที่ 20 จึงขัดแย้งโดยตรง
questions.evidence: Early prototypes appeared in the 1830s, and by 1900 electric cars accounted for roughly a third of the vehicles on the roads of the United States.

title: How Rivers Change Course
passage:
[A] Rivers are among the most dynamic features of the natural landscape, and few of them follow the same path for long. Over decades or centuries, a river may abandon its old channel and carve an entirely new one, a process that geologists call avulsion. Although such changes can appear sudden, they are usually the outcome of gradual pressures that build up over many years before a single event finally shifts the water elsewhere.

[B] The most common driver of change is the deposition of sediment. As a river flows, it carries sand, silt and gravel eroded from upstream. When the current slows, particularly across flat lowland plains, this material settles on the riverbed. Layer upon layer, the sediment raises the channel until, in some cases, the riverbed sits higher than the surrounding land. A river in this precarious position is described as perched. Only the natural banks, or levees, keep the water in place, and when these are breached the river spills sideways and seeks a lower route.

[C] Meandering also reshapes a river's course. On gently sloping ground, water tends to swing from side to side in broad loops. The current runs fastest on the outer edge of each bend, wearing the bank away, while sediment accumulates on the slower inner edge. Over time the loops grow more pronounced, and two bends may eventually meet. When this happens, the river cuts straight across the neck of land between them, leaving the abandoned loop isolated as a crescent-shaped pool known as an oxbow lake.

[D] External forces can accelerate these processes. Major floods provide the energy needed to burst through levees or slice across a meander neck in a matter of hours. Earthquakes may tilt the land or block a valley, forcing water to find a new outlet. Human activity has become increasingly influential too: the construction of dams alters the supply of sediment downstream, while the straightening of channels for navigation or flood control can change the speed and behaviour of the flow in unexpected ways.

[E] Understanding why rivers move is more than an academic exercise. Communities have long settled on fertile floodplains, and a shifting channel can threaten homes, farmland and transport links. Engineers therefore study historical patterns and build defences to hold rivers in place. Yet many researchers now argue that resisting change entirely is neither possible nor wise, and that allowing rivers some freedom to move may ultimately reduce the damage they cause.

questions.q: The sudden appearance of a new river channel is usually the result of pressures that develop slowly over time.

questions.evidence: Earthquakes may tilt the land or block a valley, forcing water to find a new outlet.

questions.evidence: Only the natural banks, or levees, keep the water in place, and when these are breached the river spills sideways and seeks a lower route.

title: Working on Demand: The Rise of the Gig Economy
passage:
[A] The term "gig economy" describes a labour market characterised by short-term contracts and freelance work rather than permanent employment. The word "gig", borrowed from the world of music, once referred to a single paid performance, and it now captures the fragmented nature of much modern labour. Enabled largely by digital platforms and smartphone applications, this model connects workers directly with customers who require a specific task, whether that is delivering a meal, driving a passenger, or completing a piece of graphic design. Although casual and temporary work has existed for centuries, technology has dramatically expanded its scale and reach.

[B] Supporters of the gig economy emphasise the flexibility it offers. Workers can, in principle, decide when and how much they work, fitting employment around family responsibilities, study, or other commitments. For businesses, the arrangement reduces the fixed costs associated with a permanent workforce, since labour can be summoned only when demand arises. Economists note that this responsiveness allows companies to adjust quickly to fluctuations in the market. Consumers, meanwhile, benefit from services that are cheaper and more readily available than they once were.

[C] Critics, however, argue that the flexibility is frequently overstated. Because most gig workers are classified as independent contractors rather than employees, they are usually excluded from benefits such as paid holidays, sick leave, and pension contributions. Their income can also be highly unpredictable, rising and falling with consumer demand and with the pricing algorithms that platforms control. Some studies suggest that, once unpaid waiting time is taken into account, hourly earnings fall below the legal minimum wage in certain occupations. The absence of a guaranteed income leaves many workers financially vulnerable.

[D] Governments across the world have begun to respond to these concerns. In several countries, courts have ruled that particular categories of gig workers should be reclassified as employees, thereby entitling them to greater protection. Platform companies have generally resisted such changes, warning that additional obligations could raise prices and reduce the very flexibility that attracts workers in the first place. The debate reflects a deeper question about how employment law, much of it designed for an earlier industrial age, should adapt to a rapidly changing world of work.

[E] Predicting the future of the gig economy is difficult. Some analysts believe it will continue to grow as automation and artificial intelligence reshape traditional careers, while others expect tighter regulation to slow its expansion. What seems certain is that the boundary between employment and self-employment, once relatively clear, has become increasingly blurred, and societies will need to decide how the benefits and risks of this new model should be shared.

questions.evidence: The word "gig", borrowed from the world of music, once referred to a single paid performance, and it now captures the fragmented nature of much modern labour.
