title: Life in the Concrete Jungle: How Wildlife Adapts to Cities
passage:
[A] As urban areas continue to expand across the globe, a growing number of animal species are learning to thrive within them. Once regarded as hostile environments unsuitable for most wildlife, cities are now recognised by ecologists as complex habitats that support surprising biological diversity. The animals that succeed in these settings are not merely surviving; many display measurable behavioural and physiological changes that distinguish them from their rural counterparts. This process of urban adaptation has become a rich field of scientific study, offering insights into how quickly organisms can respond to human-altered landscapes.

[B] One of the most extensively documented examples concerns birdsong. Great tits living in noisy city centres have been found to sing at a higher pitch than those in quieter woodlands. Because low-frequency traffic noise tends to mask the lower notes of their songs, urban birds that shift towards higher frequencies are more likely to be heard by potential mates. Researchers regard this vocal adjustment as a clear response to the acoustic conditions of the city rather than a random variation. Similar patterns have since been recorded in several other songbird species, suggesting that noise pollution is a powerful selective pressure shaping communication.

[C] Diet is another area in which urban animals frequently diverge from their wild relatives. Foxes, raccoons and certain gulls have all become adept at exploiting human food waste, which is abundant and predictable in built-up areas. This dietary flexibility often correlates with boldness: individuals that are less fearful of people and traffic gain easier access to these resources. However, reliance on discarded food is not without cost, as it can lead to nutritional imbalances and increased exposure to disease. The long-term consequences of such feeding habits remain uncertain and are still being investigated.

[D] Physical and physiological traits may also shift in city-dwelling populations. Some urban lizards, for instance, have developed longer limbs and larger toe pads that help them grip smooth artificial surfaces such as walls and painted fences. Certain insects have altered their activity cycles to take advantage of artificial lighting, while a number of mammals have become increasingly nocturnal to avoid contact with humans during daylight hours. These changes illustrate a broader principle: the pressures of city life can drive rapid evolutionary and behavioural responses, sometimes over remarkably short timescales.

[E] Understanding these adaptations carries practical value for city planners and conservationists alike. By recognising which features of the urban landscape either help or hinder wildlife, designers can create greener, more permeable cities that accommodate a wider range of species. Green corridors, native planting and reduced night-time lighting are among the measures increasingly proposed to support urban biodiversity. In this way, the study of urban wildlife adaptation is not only a scientific curiosity but also a guide to building cities where humans and animals can coexist more successfully.

title: The Trained Memory: How Champions Build Recall
passage:
[A] Every year, competitors gather at the World Memory Championships to perform feats that appear almost superhuman: recalling the order of a shuffled deck of cards in under thirty seconds, or memorising hundreds of random digits in a single sitting. Yet neuroscientists who have studied these individuals report a surprising finding. Brain scans reveal no unusual anatomical features, and standardised tests show that memory champions possess ordinary intelligence and average natural memory. What distinguishes them is not innate talent but a set of deliberate techniques, refined through thousands of hours of disciplined practice.

[B] The foundation of nearly every champion's method is an ancient device known as the method of loci, or the "memory palace". The technique requires a person to imagine a familiar location, such as their childhood home, and to place vivid mental images representing the items to be remembered at specific points along a route through that space. To recall the information, the competitor simply takes an imaginary walk, retrieving each image in sequence. Because the human brain evolved to remember places and striking pictures far more readily than abstract symbols, converting dry data into memorable scenes dramatically improves retention. A string of numbers, for instance, might be transformed into a bizarre story unfolding room by room.

[C] Building such images at speed demands additional systems. Many champions employ a technique that assigns a person, an action and an object to every possible pair of digits, allowing long numbers to be encoded as compact, cinematic scenes. Mastering this requires enormous repetition, and champions typically train for several hours each day. Crucially, they practise under strict time pressure, using software that mimics competition conditions and records every error. By reviewing these mistakes methodically, they identify weak associations and reinforce them, a process that gradually raises both their speed and their accuracy.

[D] Perhaps the most reassuring lesson from this research is that the underlying skills can be taught. In one influential study, ordinary volunteers who were trained in these strategies for six weeks showed marked improvements in their ability to memorise lists, and brain imaging revealed that their patterns of neural activity had begun to resemble those of elite competitors. The champions themselves are quick to emphasise this point. Their extraordinary performances, they insist, are less a matter of gift than of method, patience and relentless practice available, in principle, to anyone willing to invest the effort.

title: From Reed to Rollerball: The Evolution of Writing Instruments
passage:
[A] The desire to record language in a permanent form has driven human ingenuity for millennia. The earliest sophisticated writing tool was arguably the reed pen, cut from hollow marsh grasses and used by Egyptian scribes to apply ink to papyrus more than four thousand years ago. Because the reed's fibrous tip wore down quickly, scribes were obliged to trim it repeatedly, and a single day's work might consume several pens. Despite this inconvenience, the reed pen remained the dominant instrument across much of the ancient Mediterranean world for centuries.

[B] By the early medieval period, the reed had largely been supplanted in Europe by the quill, a pen fashioned from the flight feathers of large birds, most commonly the goose. The quill's principal advantage lay in its durability and the fine, flexible line it produced, qualities that made it ideal for the elaborate manuscripts copied in monasteries. Preparing a quill was a skilled task: the feather had to be hardened, and its tip cut into a nib with a small knife. It is from this practice that the modern word "penknife" derives. Quills dominated Western writing for roughly a thousand years, though they too required constant re-cutting.

[C] The nineteenth century brought a decisive shift towards metal. Although steel nibs had been produced by hand earlier, it was the development of mass-production techniques in Birmingham during the 1820s that made cheap, durable metal pens widely available. These dip pens, however, still had to be repeatedly plunged into an inkwell, an awkward process that frequently produced blots. The obvious solution was to store ink within the pen itself, but early attempts leaked badly. It was Lewis Waterman who, in 1884, patented a reliable fountain pen incorporating a channel that regulated the flow of ink, allowing air to enter as ink left the reservoir.

[D] The twentieth century's most significant innovation was the ballpoint pen. The Hungarian journalist Laszlo Biro, frustrated by the smudging of fountain-pen ink, observed that the quick-drying ink used in newspaper printing might be adapted for personal writing. Because such ink was too thick to flow through a conventional nib, Biro devised a tip fitted with a tiny rotating ball that delivered ink to the paper as it moved. Patented in 1938, the ballpoint proved robust and economical, and by the second half of the century it had become the most widely used writing instrument in the world.

title: The Great Library of Alexandria
passage:
[A] The Great Library of Alexandria, established in the Egyptian city of Alexandria during the early third century BCE, is widely regarded as the most celebrated repository of knowledge in the ancient world. It was founded under the patronage of the Ptolemaic dynasty, most probably by Ptolemy I Soter and expanded by his successor, Ptolemy II Philadelphus. The Library formed part of a larger research institution known as the Mouseion, or "shrine of the Muses", which offered scholars accommodation, meals and a stipend, allowing them to pursue their studies free from financial concern.

[B] The ambition behind the Library was remarkable. Its custodians sought to gather copies of every text in existence, and to this end the Ptolemies pursued an aggressive acquisition policy. Ships arriving at the harbour were reportedly searched, and any scrolls found aboard were confiscated and copied; the copies were then returned to their owners while the originals were retained by the Library. Scholars have estimated that at its height the collection may have contained several hundred thousand papyrus scrolls, although no reliable inventory has survived, and modern figures remain speculative.

[C] The Library attracted many of the finest minds of the Hellenistic age. Among them were Eratosthenes, who calculated the circumference of the Earth with striking accuracy, and Euclid, whose geometrical treatises shaped mathematics for two millennia. The institution also became a centre for the editing and preservation of texts, particularly the epics of Homer, whose works scholars painstakingly compared across different manuscripts to establish authoritative versions.

[D] The decline of the Library has long been the subject of debate. Popular tradition attributes its destruction to a single catastrophic fire, often linked to Julius Caesar's campaign in the city in 48 BCE, when flames from the harbour spread to nearby buildings. However, most historians now argue that the Library's demise was gradual rather than sudden. A combination of fires, dwindling royal funding, political instability and the natural deterioration of fragile papyrus is thought to have eroded the collection over several centuries. By the time of the Roman Empire's later years, the institution had effectively ceased to function, though its reputation endured.

questions.q: The Great Library of Alexandria was founded during the third century BCE.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุชัดว่าก่อตั้งในช่วงต้นศตวรรษที่สามก่อนคริสตกาล
questions.evidence: The Great Library of Alexandria, established in the Egyptian city of Alexandria during the early third century BCE, is widely regarded as the most celebrated repository of knowledge in the ancient world.

questions.q: Scholars living at the Mouseion were required to pay for their own accommodation and meals.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่าสถาบันให้ที่พัก อาหาร และเงินเดือน จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: The Library formed part of a larger research institution known as the Mouseion, or "shrine of the Muses", which offered scholars accommodation, meals and a stipend, allowing them to pursue their studies free from financial concern.

questions.q: A complete and reliable list of the scrolls held by the Library still exists today.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าไม่มีบัญชีรายการที่เชื่อถือได้หลงเหลืออยู่
questions.evidence: Scholars have estimated that at its height the collection may have contained several hundred thousand papyrus scrolls, although no reliable inventory has survived, and modern figures remain speculative.

questions.q: Ptolemy II Philadelphus personally travelled abroad to search for new scrolls.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องกล่าวถึงการขยายห้องสมุดโดย Ptolemy II แต่ไม่ได้บอกว่าเขาเดินทางไปหาม้วนหนังสือด้วยตนเอง

questions.q: Most historians today believe the Library was destroyed suddenly in a single fire.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่านักประวัติศาสตร์ส่วนใหญ่เชื่อว่าการเสื่อมสลายเป็นไปอย่างค่อยเป็นค่อยไป ไม่ใช่ฉับพลัน
questions.evidence: However, most historians now argue that the Library's demise was gradual rather than sudden.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did the Ptolemies typically treat scrolls found on ships in the harbour?
questions.a: They kept the originals and returned copies to the owners.
options: They kept the originals and returned copies to the owners.
They returned the originals and destroyed the copies.
They purchased the scrolls at a fair market price.
They gave the scrolls to visiting scholars as gifts.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่าเก็บต้นฉบับไว้และคืนสำเนาให้เจ้าของ
questions.evidence: Ships arriving at the harbour were reportedly searched, and any scrolls found aboard were confiscated and copied; the copies were then returned to their owners while the originals were retained by the Library.

questions.q: What is Eratosthenes credited with in the passage?
questions.a: Calculating the circumference of the Earth with striking accuracy.
options: Calculating the circumference of the Earth with striking accuracy.
Writing geometrical treatises that shaped mathematics.
Editing the epics of Homer across manuscripts.
Founding the Mouseion research institution.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่า Eratosthenes คำนวณเส้นรอบวงของโลกได้อย่างแม่นยำ
questions.evidence: Among them were Eratosthenes, who calculated the circumference of the Earth with striking accuracy, and Euclid, whose geometrical treatises shaped mathematics for two millennia.

questions.q: Which text does the passage say scholars at the Library worked to preserve and edit in particular?
options: The epics of Homer.
The treatises of Euclid.
The writings of Ptolemy I Soter.
The inventories of the harbour.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องกล่าวถึงการแก้ไขและรักษามหากาพย์ของ Homer เป็นพิเศษ
questions.evidence: The institution also became a centre for the editing and preservation of texts, particularly the epics of Homer, whose works scholars painstakingly compared across different manuscripts to establish authoritative versions.

questions.q: According to the passage, what event is popularly linked to the Library's destruction?
questions.a: Julius Caesar's campaign in the city in 48 BCE.
options: Julius Caesar's campaign in the city in 48 BCE.
The founding of the Roman Empire.
A flood from the harbour.
The death of Ptolemy I Soter.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องระบุว่าตำนานเชื่อมโยงการทำลายกับการทัพของ Julius Caesar ในปี 48 ก่อนคริสตกาล
questions.evidence: Popular tradition attributes its destruction to a single catastrophic fire, often linked to Julius Caesar's campaign in the city in 48 BCE, when flames from the harbour spread to nearby buildings.

questions.q: The Library was part of a research institution called the Mouseion, meaning "shrine of the ___".
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องบอกว่า Mouseion แปลว่า shrine of the Muses
questions.evidence: The Library formed part of a larger research institution known as the Mouseion, or "shrine of the Muses", which offered scholars accommodation, meals and a stipend, allowing them to pursue their studies free from financial concern.

questions.q: The Library sought to gather copies of every text in existence through an aggressive ___ policy.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องใช้คำว่า aggressive acquisition policy
questions.evidence: Its custodians sought to gather copies of every text in existence, and to this end the Ptolemies pursued an aggressive acquisition policy.

questions.q: The gradual deterioration of fragile ___ contributed to the loss of the collection.
questions.why_th: เนื้อเรื่องกล่าวถึงการเสื่อมสภาพตามธรรมชาติของ papyrus ที่เปราะบาง
questions.evidence: A combination of fires, dwindling royal funding, political instability and the natural deterioration of fragile papyrus is thought to have eroded the collection over several centuries.

title: Microfinance and the Small Business Dream
passage:
[A] Microfinance, the provision of small loans, savings facilities and insurance to people excluded from conventional banking, rose to prominence in the 1970s. Its best-known pioneer, the Bangladeshi economist Muhammad Yunus, began by lending the equivalent of twenty-seven dollars to a group of villagers who made bamboo furniture, and in 1983 he formalised his experiment as the Grameen Bank. The underlying premise was straightforward: the poor are not inherently uncreditworthy; they simply lack the collateral that commercial lenders demand. By 2010, microfinance institutions were serving more than 200 million borrowers worldwide, the majority of them women.

[B] A distinctive feature of early microfinance was group lending. Instead of requiring physical collateral, institutions issued loans to small groups of borrowers who were jointly responsible for repayment. Because a default by one member could jeopardise future credit for the entire group, peer pressure functioned as a substitute for collateral. This arrangement produced repayment rates that frequently exceeded 95 per cent, a figure that surpassed the performance of many commercial banks. Group lending also lowered administrative costs, since loan officers could deal with several clients in a single visit.

[C] The assumption that microcredit would transform small enterprises has, however, proved difficult to verify. A series of randomised controlled trials conducted in six countries, published in 2015, found that access to microloans modestly increased business investment and the likelihood of starting an enterprise, yet produced no significant rise in average household income within the study periods. Researchers concluded that while microfinance expands choices for the poor, it is rarely, on its own, a route out of poverty. Many borrowers, moreover, use loans to smooth consumption — paying school fees or medical bills — rather than to expand a business.

[D] Critics have raised sharper objections. Annual interest rates at some institutions exceed 30 per cent, reflecting the high cost of administering many tiny loans. In the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh, aggressive lending and multiple borrowing culminated in a repayment crisis in 2010, prompting the government to impose strict regulation. Some scholars argue that the commercialisation of the sector has shifted its priorities from social mission to profit.

[E] The industry is now being reshaped by digital technology. Mobile money platforms such as Kenya's M-Pesa allow lenders to disburse funds and collect repayments without physical branches, cutting transaction costs dramatically. Advocates believe such innovations could restore microfinance's original promise; sceptics reply that easier credit may simply accelerate over-indebtedness. What is clear is that small loans alone cannot substitute for the roads, schooling and stable institutions on which small businesses ultimately depend.

questions.q: Muhammad Yunus established the Grameen Bank during the 1970s.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่า Yunus เริ่มทดลองปล่อยกู้ในทศวรรษ 1970 แต่ก่อตั้ง Grameen Bank อย่างเป็นทางการในปี 1983 จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้โดยตรง
questions.evidence: Its best-known pioneer, the Bangladeshi economist Muhammad Yunus, began by lending the equivalent of twenty-seven dollars to a group of villagers who made bamboo furniture, and in 1983 he formalised his experiment as the Grameen Bank.

questions.q: Repayment rates achieved through group lending were often higher than those of many commercial banks.
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าอัตราการชำระคืนมักเกิน 95% ซึ่งเป็นตัวเลขที่สูงกว่าผลงานของธนาคารพาณิชย์หลายแห่ง ตรงกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: This arrangement produced repayment rates that frequently exceeded 95 per cent, a figure that surpassed the performance of many commercial banks.

questions.q: Muhammad Yunus received international awards for his work in microfinance.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวถึง Yunus ในฐานะผู้บุกเบิกเท่านั้น ไม่ได้พูดถึงรางวัลใด ๆ ที่เขาได้รับ จึงไม่มีข้อมูลยืนยันหรือขัดแย้ง

questions.q: The randomised controlled trials showed that microloans significantly raised average household income.
questions.why_th: งานวิจัยพบว่าไมโครเครดิตเพิ่มการลงทุนในธุรกิจเล็กน้อย แต่ไม่ได้ทำให้รายได้ครัวเรือนเฉลี่ยเพิ่มขึ้นอย่างมีนัยสำคัญ ซึ่งขัดแย้งกับข้อความนี้
questions.evidence: A series of randomised controlled trials conducted in six countries, published in 2015, found that access to microloans modestly increased business investment and the likelihood of starting an enterprise, yet produced no significant rise in average household income within the study periods.

questions.q: The repayment crisis in Andhra Pradesh led the government to introduce strict regulation.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าวิกฤตการชำระหนี้ปี 2010 ในรัฐอานธรประเทศทำให้รัฐบาลออกกฎระเบียบที่เข้มงวด ตรงกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: In the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh, aggressive lending and multiple borrowing culminated in a repayment crisis in 2010, prompting the government to impose strict regulation.

questions.q: According to paragraph A, the underlying premise of microfinance was that poor people
options: are unwilling to repay the loans they receive
simply lack the collateral that commercial lenders demand
prefer to borrow from informal moneylenders
need larger loans than commercial banks can offer
questions.a: simply lack the collateral that commercial lenders demand
questions.why_th: บทความอธิบายหลักคิดพื้นฐานว่าคนจนไม่ได้ไร้เครดิตโดยธรรมชาติ เพียงแต่ขาดหลักทรัพย์ค้ำประกันที่ธนาคารพาณิชย์เรียกร้อง
questions.evidence: The underlying premise was straightforward: the poor are not inherently uncreditworthy; they simply lack the collateral that commercial lenders demand.

questions.q: Why did group lending achieve high repayment rates?
options: Borrowers were required to provide physical collateral
Loan officers visited each client individually
Peer pressure functioned as a substitute for collateral
Governments guaranteed every loan in the group
questions.a: Peer pressure functioned as a substitute for collateral
questions.why_th: เพราะการผิดนัดของสมาชิกคนหนึ่งอาจทำให้ทั้งกลุ่มเสียสิทธิ์กู้ในอนาคต แรงกดดันจากเพื่อนในกลุ่มจึงทำหน้าที่แทนหลักทรัพย์ค้ำประกัน
questions.evidence: Because a default by one member could jeopardise future credit for the entire group, peer pressure functioned as a substitute for collateral.

questions.q: Paragraph C states that many borrowers use their microloans to
options: purchase agricultural land
repay debts owed to other lenders
hire additional employees for their businesses
pay school fees or medical bills
questions.a: pay school fees or medical bills
questions.why_th: บทความบอกว่าผู้กู้จำนวนมากใช้เงินกู้เพื่อประคองการใช้จ่ายในชีวิตประจำวัน เช่น จ่ายค่าเทอมหรือค่ารักษาพยาบาล แทนที่จะขยายธุรกิจ
questions.evidence: Many borrowers, moreover, use loans to smooth consumption — paying school fees or medical bills — rather than to expand a business.

questions.q: What explanation does the passage give for interest rates exceeding 30 per cent?
options: The high risk of borrowers defaulting
The high cost of administering many tiny loans
Government taxation of microfinance institutions
The expense of building physical branches
questions.a: The high cost of administering many tiny loans
questions.why_th: บทความระบุว่าดอกเบี้ยที่สูงกว่า 30% ต่อปีสะท้อนต้นทุนที่สูงในการบริหารจัดการเงินกู้จำนวนมากที่มีขนาดเล็ก
questions.evidence: Annual interest rates at some institutions exceed 30 per cent, reflecting the high cost of administering many tiny loans.

questions.q: In early microfinance, peer pressure within borrowing groups acted as a substitute for ___.
questions.why_th: บทความระบุตรง ๆ ว่าแรงกดดันจากเพื่อนในกลุ่มทำหน้าที่แทน 'collateral' (หลักทรัพย์ค้ำประกัน)
questions.evidence: Because a default by one member could jeopardise future credit for the entire group, peer pressure functioned as a substitute for collateral.

questions.q: Trials published in 2015 found that access to microloans modestly increased ___ and the likelihood of starting an enterprise.
questions.why_th: งานวิจัยแบบสุ่มพบว่าไมโครเครดิตเพิ่ม 'business investment' (การลงทุนในธุรกิจ) เล็กน้อย คำตอบต้องคัดลอกจากบทความตรงตัว
questions.evidence: A series of randomised controlled trials conducted in six countries, published in 2015, found that access to microloans modestly increased business investment and the likelihood of starting an enterprise, yet produced no significant rise in average household income within the study periods.

questions.q: Mobile money platforms let lenders disburse funds and collect repayments without physical ___.
questions.why_th: บทความกล่าวว่าแพลตฟอร์มเงินมือถืออย่าง M-Pesa ช่วยให้ผู้ปล่อยกู้ทำงานได้โดยไม่ต้องมี 'branches' (สาขา) จริง ๆ
questions.evidence: Mobile money platforms such as Kenya's M-Pesa allow lenders to disburse funds and collect repayments without physical branches, cutting transaction costs dramatically.

title: The Screen Time Debate: What the Research Really Shows
passage:
[A] Over the past two decades, the amount of time children spend looking at digital screens has risen sharply, prompting widespread concern among parents, educators and policymakers. Popular commentary frequently presents screen use as a straightforward danger, linking it to poor sleep, reduced attention and rising rates of anxiety. Yet researchers who study the topic caution that the evidence is far more complicated than headlines suggest. Much of the early alarm, they argue, rested on studies that measured only the total number of hours spent on devices, ignoring what children were actually doing during that time.

[B] A central problem is the difficulty of establishing cause and effect. Most studies in this field are correlational, meaning they observe that two things occur together without proving that one produces the other. A child who sleeps poorly may spend more time on a tablet because they are already awake, rather than the device being the source of the sleeplessness. Because families cannot easily be assigned at random to different levels of screen exposure, disentangling the influence of technology from other factors, such as household income or parental habits, remains a persistent obstacle for scientists.

[C] Recent work has shifted attention from quantity to quality and context. A large British study following thousands of adolescents found that moderate use showed almost no measurable link to wellbeing, and that the strongest effects appeared only at the extremes of very heavy use. Researchers increasingly distinguish between passive consumption, such as watching videos alone, and active, social or creative uses, including messaging friends or making digital art. The setting matters too: a screen shared with a parent who talks about the content appears to support learning, whereas the same device used silently late at night may not.

[D] These findings have practical implications for the guidance offered to families. Several health bodies have quietly moved away from fixed hourly limits, which are hard to enforce and poorly supported by data, towards more flexible principles. They now emphasise protecting sleep, ensuring that screens do not displace physical activity and conversation, and keeping devices out of bedrooms at night. Crucially, most experts agree that no single rule can suit every child, and that the influence of a screen depends heavily on the age of the user and the purpose of the activity.

questions.q: Public discussion about screen use tends to portray it as clearly harmful.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A บอกว่าคำวิจารณ์ทั่วไปมองว่าการใช้จอเป็นอันตรายอย่างตรงไปตรงมา
questions.evidence: Popular commentary frequently presents screen use as a straightforward danger, linking it to poor sleep, reduced attention and rising rates of anxiety.

questions.q: Early studies often failed to record the specific activities children performed on their devices.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า A ระบุว่างานวิจัยยุคแรกวัดแค่จำนวนชั่วโมง ไม่สนใจว่าเด็กทำอะไร
questions.evidence: Much of the early alarm, they argue, rested on studies that measured only the total number of hours spent on devices, ignoring what children were actually doing during that time.

questions.q: Correlational studies can demonstrate that screen use directly causes poor sleep.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B บอกว่างานแบบ correlational สังเกตแค่ว่าเกิดร่วมกัน ไม่ได้พิสูจน์ว่าอย่างหนึ่งทำให้เกิดอีกอย่าง จึงขัดแย้งกับข้อความ
questions.evidence: Most studies in this field are correlational, meaning they observe that two things occur together without proving that one produces the other.

questions.q: The British study concluded that girls were more affected by heavy screen use than boys.
questions.why_th: บทความไม่ได้กล่าวถึงความแตกต่างระหว่างเพศเลย

questions.q: Some health organisations have reduced their reliance on strict hourly screen-time limits.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D บอกว่าหน่วยงานสุขภาพหลายแห่งได้ค่อยๆ เลิกใช้การจำกัดเป็นชั่วโมงตายตัว
questions.evidence: Several health bodies have quietly moved away from fixed hourly limits, which are hard to enforce and poorly supported by data, towards more flexible principles.

questions.q: According to paragraph B, why is it hard to prove that technology causes harm?
questions.a: Families cannot easily be assigned at random to different amounts of screen use.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า B ระบุว่าครอบครัวไม่สามารถถูกสุ่มให้มีระดับการใช้จอต่างกันได้ง่าย ทำให้แยกอิทธิพลได้ยาก
questions.evidence: Because families cannot easily be assigned at random to different levels of screen exposure, disentangling the influence of technology from other factors, such as household income or parental habits, remains a persistent obstacle for scientists.
options: Families cannot easily be assigned at random to different amounts of screen use.
Children refuse to report how long they use their devices each day.
Scientists lack the technology to measure screen time accurately.
Parents deliberately hide their household income from researchers.

questions.q: What did the large British study find about moderate screen use?
questions.a: It had almost no measurable connection to wellbeing.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C บอกว่าการใช้ระดับปานกลางแทบไม่มีความเชื่อมโยงที่วัดได้กับความเป็นอยู่ที่ดี
questions.evidence: A large British study following thousands of adolescents found that moderate use showed almost no measurable link to wellbeing, and that the strongest effects appeared only at the extremes of very heavy use.
options: It had almost no measurable connection to wellbeing.
It strongly improved the wellbeing of most adolescents.
It caused the same harm as very heavy use.
It was more damaging than passive consumption.

questions.q: Which use of screens does the passage suggest may support children's learning?
questions.a: A screen shared with a parent who discusses the content.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ระบุว่าจอที่ใช้ร่วมกับพ่อแม่ที่พูดคุยเกี่ยวกับเนื้อหาช่วยส่งเสริมการเรียนรู้
questions.evidence: The setting matters too: a screen shared with a parent who talks about the content appears to support learning, whereas the same device used silently late at night may not.
options: A screen shared with a parent who discusses the content.
A device used silently by a child late at night.
Watching videos alone for long periods.
Any activity, provided it stays under a fixed hourly limit.

questions.q: What is the main point made in paragraph D?
questions.a: Guidance is moving towards flexible principles rather than fixed limits.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D เน้นว่าหน่วยงานสุขภาพเลิกใช้การจำกัดชั่วโมงตายตัว แล้วหันไปใช้หลักการที่ยืดหยุ่นแทน เพราะไม่มีกฎเดียวเหมาะกับเด็กทุกคน
questions.evidence: Several health bodies have quietly moved away from fixed hourly limits, which are hard to enforce and poorly supported by data, towards more flexible principles.
options: Guidance is moving towards flexible principles rather than fixed limits.
Hourly limits have been proven to work for every child.
Screens should be banned from all homes with children.
Physical activity is unrelated to children's screen habits.

questions.q: Researchers distinguish between passive consumption and active, social or ___ uses of screens.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า C ใช้คำว่า creative ตรงตัว
questions.evidence: Researchers increasingly distinguish between passive consumption, such as watching videos alone, and active, social or creative uses, including messaging friends or making digital art.

questions.q: Experts advise keeping devices out of ___ at night.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ใช้คำว่า bedrooms ตรงตัว
questions.evidence: They now emphasise protecting sleep, ensuring that screens do not displace physical activity and conversation, and keeping devices out of bedrooms at night.

questions.q: The effect of a screen depends greatly on the ___ of the user and the purpose of the activity.
questions.why_th: ย่อหน้า D ใช้คำว่า age ตรงตัว
questions.evidence: Crucially, most experts agree that no single rule can suit every child, and that the influence of a screen depends heavily on the age of the user and the purpose of the activity.

title: Seeing the World in Colour: Vision Across the Animal Kingdom
passage:
[A] Colour vision is one of the most remarkable achievements of the animal eye, yet the way different creatures perceive colour varies enormously. The ability depends on specialised light-sensitive cells called cones, which sit alongside the rod cells responsible for vision in dim conditions. Each type of cone is tuned to a particular range of wavelengths, and the brain compares the signals from different cones to construct the sensation of colour. Humans possess three kinds of cone, a condition known as trichromacy, which allows the discrimination of roughly one million distinct hues. Many other mammals, however, have only two functional cone types and therefore experience a comparatively muted palette.

[B] The number of cone types is not, however, a reliable guide to the richness of an animal's colour world. Birds are a striking example. Most birds are tetrachromatic, possessing a fourth cone sensitive to ultraviolet light, which lies beyond the range that human eyes can detect. This additional channel means that plumage patterns invisible to us may appear vivid and conspicuous to another bird. Flowers, too, frequently display ultraviolet markings that guide pollinating insects towards nectar, revealing that the colours we observe represent only a fraction of the information available in reflected light.

[C] Perhaps the most extraordinary visual system belongs to the mantis shrimp, a marine crustacean that possesses up to sixteen distinct types of photoreceptor. It might be assumed that such an animal would perceive an overwhelmingly rich spectrum, but experiments suggest otherwise. Rather than finely comparing signals as mammals do, the mantis shrimp appears to recognise colours rapidly through a simpler, more direct process. Researchers propose that this arrangement allows the creature to identify prey and rivals quickly in the busy coral environments where it lives, prioritising speed over precision.

[D] Colour vision has evolved repeatedly because it confers clear survival advantages. For foraging animals, the capacity to distinguish ripe fruit from unripe, or nutritious young leaves from tough older ones, can be decisive. Colour also plays a central role in communication: the bright signals of many fish, birds and primates advertise health and readiness to mate. Because these functions are so valuable, the eye has been shaped by natural selection in a variety of directions, producing the astonishing diversity of visual systems observed today. Understanding this diversity reminds us that human colour perception, though impressive, is merely one solution among many to the challenge of interpreting light.

questions.q: Rod cells are mainly responsible for vision when light levels are low.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่า rod cells รับผิดชอบการมองเห็นในสภาพแสงสลัว (dim conditions)
questions.evidence: The ability depends on specialised light-sensitive cells called cones, which sit alongside the rod cells responsible for vision in dim conditions.

questions.q: All mammals possess three types of cone cell.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าสัตว์เลี้ยงลูกด้วยนมหลายชนิดมีเพียงสอง cone จึงขัดแย้งกับ 'ทั้งหมดมีสาม'
questions.evidence: Many other mammals, however, have only two functional cone types and therefore experience a comparatively muted palette.

questions.q: The number of cone types an animal has directly determines how rich its colour perception is.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุชัดว่าจำนวน cone ไม่ใช่ตัวชี้วัดความrichของโลกสีที่เชื่อถือได้
questions.evidence: The number of cone types is not, however, a reliable guide to the richness of an animal's colour world.

questions.q: Bees rely on ultraviolet flower markings more than other pollinating insects do.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความพูดถึงแมลงผสมเกสรทั่วไป แต่ไม่ได้เปรียบเทียบผึ้งกับแมลงชนิดอื่น จึงเป็น Not Given

questions.q: The mantis shrimp processes colour by finely comparing signals in the same way mammals do.
options: True
False
Not Given
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่ากุ้งตั๊กแตนใช้กระบวนการที่ง่ายและตรงกว่า ไม่ใช่การเปรียบเทียบแบบสัตว์เลี้ยงลูกด้วยนม
questions.evidence: Rather than finely comparing signals as mammals do, the mantis shrimp appears to recognise colours rapidly through a simpler, more direct process.

questions.q: According to the passage, what makes most birds different from humans in terms of colour vision?
questions.a: They have a fourth cone that is sensitive to ultraviolet light.
options: They have a fourth cone that is sensitive to ultraviolet light.
They have fewer cone types than humans do.
They rely mainly on rod cells to perceive colour.
They can detect one million distinct hues.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่านกส่วนใหญ่เป็น tetrachromatic มี cone ที่สี่ที่ไวต่อแสงยูวี
questions.evidence: Most birds are tetrachromatic, possessing a fourth cone sensitive to ultraviolet light, which lies beyond the range that human eyes can detect.

questions.q: What do experiments suggest about the mantis shrimp's colour vision?
questions.a: It recognises colours quickly through a relatively simple process.
options: It recognises colours quickly through a relatively simple process.
It perceives an overwhelmingly rich spectrum of colours.
It cannot distinguish prey from rivals effectively.
It compares cone signals more finely than any mammal.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่ากุ้งตั๊กแตนจดจำสีอย่างรวดเร็วผ่านกระบวนการที่ง่ายกว่า
questions.evidence: Rather than finely comparing signals as mammals do, the mantis shrimp appears to recognise colours rapidly through a simpler, more direct process.

questions.q: Why does the passage say colour vision has evolved repeatedly?
questions.a: Because it provides clear advantages for survival.
options: Because it provides clear advantages for survival.
Because it makes animals more attractive to humans.
Because it reduces the need for rod cells.
Because it allows animals to see in dim conditions.
questions.why_th: ข้อความระบุว่าการมองเห็นสีวิวัฒนาการซ้ำ ๆ เพราะให้ข้อได้เปรียบด้านการอยู่รอด
questions.evidence: Colour vision has evolved repeatedly because it confers clear survival advantages.

questions.q: What example does the passage give of how colour helps foraging animals?
questions.a: Distinguishing ripe fruit from unripe fruit.
options: Distinguishing ripe fruit from unripe fruit.
Detecting predators hidden among leaves.
Finding a mate in coral environments.
Seeing ultraviolet markings on other animals.
questions.why_th: ข้อความยกตัวอย่างการแยกผลไม้สุกจากผลไม้ดิบสำหรับสัตว์ที่หาอาหาร
questions.evidence: For foraging animals, the capacity to distinguish ripe fruit from unripe, or nutritious young leaves from tough older ones, can be decisive.

questions.q: The human condition of having three kinds of cone is known as ___.
questions.why_th: ข้อความเรียกภาวะที่มนุษย์มี cone สามชนิดว่า trichromacy
questions.evidence: Humans possess three kinds of cone, a condition known as trichromacy, which allows the discrimination of roughly one million distinct hues.

questions.q: Flowers often display ultraviolet markings that guide pollinating insects towards ___.
questions.why_th: ข้อความบอกว่าเครื่องหมายยูวีนำแมลงผสมเกสรไปสู่ nectar
questions.evidence: Flowers, too, frequently display ultraviolet markings that guide pollinating insects towards nectar, revealing that the colours we observe represent only a fraction of the information available in reflected light.

questions.q: The mantis shrimp is described as a marine ___ with many types of photoreceptor.
questions.why_th: ข้อความเรียกกุ้งตั๊กแตนว่า marine crustacean
questions.evidence: Perhaps the most extraordinary visual system belongs to the mantis shrimp, a marine crustacean that possesses up to sixteen distinct types of photoreceptor.
